This cross-workspace search is powerful — it answers the question 'does this data already exist somewhere?' before someone creates a duplicate.

NordGrid's platform team encourages writing thorough item descriptions specifically because of catalog search. A Lakehouse described as 'Silver layer meter readings, validated and deduplicated, partitioned by year and month, updated daily at 02:15 UTC' is far more discoverable than one described as 'Silver data' or left with no description at all. The description is the item's catalog listing — it determines whether someone searching for the data will find it.
Endorsement — Promoted and Certified

Not all items in the catalog are equally trustworthy. A test notebook created last week is less reliable than a production pipeline that has run daily for six months. Fabric provides two endorsement levels to distinguish between them. Promoted means the item's owner recommends it for use — it is stable and intended for consumption. Certified means an authorized reviewer (usually the platform team) has verified the item meets organizational standards for quality, documentation, and testing. NordGrid certifies all production Lakehouses and Warehouses, and promotes all production notebooks and pipelines. Experimental or test items receive no endorsement.

left.label: Promoted
points: Set by the item owner
Means: 'I recommend this item for use'
No external review required
NordGrid uses for: production notebooks, pipelines

right.label: Certified
points: Set by an authorized reviewer (platform team)
Means: 'This item meets organizational quality standards'
Requires review against documentation, testing, and governance criteria
NordGrid uses for: production Lakehouses, Warehouses, semantic models

blocks.title: The catalog is governance in action
blocks.content: The OneLake catalog is not just a search tool — it is the mechanism through which an organization makes its data discoverable, trustworthy, and manageable. When an analyst finds a certified Silver Lakehouse in the catalog, they know the data is validated, the schema is documented, and the pipeline is monitored. Without the catalog, the same analyst would need to ask the data engineering team which workspace to look in, which table to use, and whether the data is current. The catalog replaces those hallway conversations with a self-service experience.

A workspace is more than a container for items — it is a configuration boundary. The settings you apply to a workspace affect every item inside it: which Spark runtime notebooks use, whether Git integration is enabled, which capacity the workspace draws from, and which domain it belongs to. Getting these settings right during initial setup prevents configuration drift — the gradual divergence of settings that happens when each engineer configures their own defaults. This lesson covers the workspace settings that matter most for data engineers.
Spark Compute Defaults

When a notebook runs in a workspace, it uses the workspace's default Spark configuration unless the notebook overrides it. The workspace-level defaults include the Spark runtime version, the default pool size (how many nodes Spark uses), and the session timeout (how long a Spark session stays alive after the notebook finishes). NordGrid's platform team sets these defaults for each workspace: production workspaces use the stable runtime and a medium pool size, while development workspaces use a smaller pool to conserve capacity.

These defaults matter because they determine performance and cost. A workspace with an oversized default pool means every notebook starts with more compute than it needs — wasteful. A workspace with an undersized pool means engineers must override the default in every notebook — tedious and error-prone. The right default covers the common case: NordGrid's Silver workspace processes 1.5 GB of daily meter data, which runs comfortably on a medium pool. The rare exception — an annual reprocessing job that handles 550 GB — overrides the default in its specific notebook.
Git Integration

Fabric supports connecting a workspace to a Git repository (Azure DevOps or GitHub). When enabled, changes to items in the workspace are tracked as commits, and items can be synced between the workspace and the repository. This is how NordGrid's team manages version control for notebooks and pipelines — every change is committed to Git, reviewed in a pull request, and deployed through a controlled process. Git integration is a workspace-level setting: it applies to all supported item types in the workspace.

blocks.title: Enable Git early
blocks.content: Git integration is easiest to enable when the workspace is new and empty. Enabling it on a workspace that already contains dozens of items requires an initial commit that captures the current state, which can be complex if items have evolved without version tracking. NordGrid's policy is simple: enable Git integration on every production workspace at creation time, before the first item is created.
Default Environment

A workspace can have a default environment — the Environment item that notebooks use unless they specify a different one. The default environment includes the Python libraries and Spark settings that the team needs for standard work. NordGrid's Silver workspace has a default environment called env_nordgrid_prod that includes the data quality library the team uses, plus standard settings for shuffle partition count and timezone handling. When a new notebook is created in the workspace, it automatically uses this environment.
Capacity Assignment

Every workspace must be assigned to a capacity in order to run compute-intensive operations. The capacity determines how much computing power is available and how usage is billed. NordGrid's three production workspaces all share the same F64 capacity. If the organization needed to isolate billing between teams, they could assign each workspace to a different capacity — but this is a mid-level concern. At the junior level, the important concept is that the workspace's capacity assignment affects all items inside it: a notebook running in a workspace assigned to a small capacity will be slower than the same notebook running in a workspace assigned to a larger capacity.

rows: Runtime version, pool size, session timeout
Rarely — set at creation, updated for major runtime upgrades
Version control for notebooks, pipelines, and other items
Python libraries and Spark config for notebooks
Occasionally — when new libraries are needed
Rarely — only when capacity needs change

blocks.content: As an organization grows, the number of workspaces grows with it. NordGrid has three workspaces today. A company like GridUnion Continental — NordGrid's parent organization — might have fifty workspaces across six subsidiary companies and twelve teams. At that scale, the flat list of workspaces in the navigation pane becomes unmanageable. Domains are Fabric's solution: a grouping mechanism that organizes workspaces by business area, enabling decentralized ownership with centralized governance. This lesson explains what domains are, how they work, and why NordGrid's platform team uses them.
What a Domain Is

A domain in Fabric is a label that groups workspaces by business area. NordGrid belongs to the 'Grid Operations' domain, which contains all three of its workspaces. GridUnion Continental's corporate finance team has its own domain, 'Corporate Finance', with separate workspaces for budgeting, forecasting, and reporting. Domains do not change permissions — they do not determine who can access a workspace. They are an organizational layer that helps people find workspaces and helps administrators apply governance policies to groups of workspaces rather than one at a time.

Think of domains as floors in an office building. Each floor is dedicated to a business area: Grid Operations on floor 3, Corporate Finance on floor 4, Renewable Assets on floor 5. The floors do not have locks — anyone with a building badge can visit any floor. But the floor labels help visitors find the right department, and building management can apply fire safety rules per floor rather than per room. Domains work the same way: they organize and enable governance without restricting access.
Domains and Microsoft Purview

Domains connect Fabric to Microsoft Purview, the governance and compliance platform that manages data classification, sensitivity labels, and access policies across Microsoft services. When NordGrid's platform team assigns ws_nordgrid_bronze to the 'Grid Operations' domain, Purview policies for that domain — such as requiring sensitivity labels on all items, or restricting data export from certain workspaces — apply automatically. This integration means that governance rules defined by the compliance team in Purview flow through to Fabric without the data engineering team configuring them manually.

blocks.title: Domains organize workspaces by business area
blocks.content:
GridUnion Continental (Fabric Tenant)
│
├── Domain: Grid Operations
│     ├── ws_nordgrid_bronze
│     ├── ws_nordgrid_silver
│     └── ws_nordgrid_gold
│
├── Domain: Corporate Finance
│     ├── ws_finance_budget
│     └── ws_finance_reporting
│
└── Domain: Renewable Assets
      ├── ws_solar_monitoring
      └── ws_wind_analytics
blocks.caption: Domains group workspaces by business area within the tenant. Each domain can have its own governance policies, data stewards, and discoverability rules. NordGrid's three workspaces all belong to the Grid Operations domain.

blocks.content: Domain Roles

Each domain has its own set of roles, separate from workspace roles. A domain admin manages the domain's workspaces and governance settings. A domain contributor can assign workspaces to the domain. These roles are typically held by the platform team or business owners, not by individual data engineers. NordGrid's platform team lead is the domain admin for Grid Operations. Individual data engineers do not interact with domain settings in their daily work — but they benefit from them because the governance policies that the domain admin configures keep the environment consistent and compliant.

blocks.title: Junior-level takeaway
blocks.content: You do not need to configure domains — that is the platform team's responsibility. What you need to understand is that domains exist, that your workspaces belong to one, and that governance policies flow from the domain through Purview to your workspace. If you notice that your workspace requires sensitivity labels on every item, or that certain export options are disabled, the reason is likely a domain-level policy. Understanding this prevents confusion and avoids wasted time trying to change settings that are controlled at a higher level.

The previous nine lessons introduced the Fabric portal, workspaces, roles, items, the catalog, settings, and domains. This lesson brings everything together by walking through NordGrid's complete workspace structure — the same structure you will use in every subsequent module. By the end of this lesson, you will know exactly which workspace contains which items, who has access to each workspace, and how a data engineer navigates between them during a typical workday. This is the workspace map that the rest of the course builds on.
The Three Workspaces
A Morning at NordGrid

When a NordGrid data engineer starts their day, their first task is to check whether the overnight pipeline ran successfully. They open the Fabric portal, navigate to the Monitoring hub in the left pane, and filter the run history to show pipeline runs from the last 24 hours. They see pl_ingest_meters with a green checkmark (success) and a run duration of 4 minutes. They see nb_transform_meters with a green checkmark and a duration of 12 minutes. Everything is healthy.

Next, they need to verify the Silver data. They navigate to ws_nordgrid_silver in the Workspaces section, open lh_meter_readings, and check the Tables section. The silver_meters table shows a last-modified timestamp matching this morning's pipeline run. They preview the first 100 rows to confirm the data looks correct: meter_id values are populated, daily_kw values are positive, dates match yesterday. A quick SQL query in the Lakehouse's SQL endpoint confirms the row count matches expectations: approximately 500,000 rows for a normal day.
Switching Between Workspaces

The engineer now needs to update the Silver transformation notebook to add a new derived column that the operations team requested. They are already in ws_nordgrid_silver, so they find nb_transform_meters in the workspace item list and click to open it. The notebook editor appears in the main content area with the PySpark cells visible. After making the change, the engineer saves the notebook and checks the workspace's Git integration panel to commit the change. The commit triggers a peer review process before the change reaches the production pipeline.

Later, they need to check whether the Gold aggregates updated correctly. They navigate to ws_nordgrid_gold from the Workspaces list. They open dw_nordgrid_analytics and run a quick T-SQL query to verify the daily_region_summary table has four rows for yesterday (one per region). The numbers match expectations. Finally, they open the Power BI report linked to sm_grid_operations to verify the dashboard shows current data. The entire morning routine — monitoring, verification, a code change, and validation — involved three workspaces, four item types, and zero context-switching outside the Fabric portal.

blocks.title: A data engineer's navigation path through a typical morning
blocks.content:
Portal Home
    │
    ▼
Monitoring Hub ──▶ Check pipeline runs (pl_ingest_meters: ✓)
    │
    ▼
ws_nordgrid_silver
    ├──▶ lh_meter_readings ──▶ Preview tables, check row count
    │
    └──▶ nb_transform_meters ──▶ Edit notebook, commit to Git
    │
    ▼
ws_nordgrid_gold
    ├──▶ dw_nordgrid_analytics ──▶ Run verification query
    │
    └──▶ sm_grid_operations ──▶ Open linked Power BI report

Total: 3 workspaces, 5 items, 1 portal, ~30 minutes
blocks.caption: A typical morning involves moving between the monitoring hub and three workspaces. The portal's unified navigation makes this seamless — every item opens in the same browser window.

blocks.content: The Complete Picture

You now have the full workspace foundation. The portal is your single interface (lesson 1). Workspaces organize items and control access (lesson 2). Creating a workspace requires thoughtful naming and capacity assignment (lesson 3). Roles enforce least-privilege access (lesson 4). Items are the building blocks with dependencies between them (lesson 5). Creating and managing items follows a consistent pattern (lesson 6). The OneLake catalog makes items discoverable across workspaces (lesson 7). Workspace settings configure compute, Git, and environments (lesson 8). Domains group workspaces for governance (lesson 9). And NordGrid's three-workspace structure — Bronze, Silver, Gold — is the concrete implementation you will build on for the rest of the course.

blocks.title: What comes next
blocks.content: Module 3 takes you inside OneLake — the storage layer underneath every workspace and every item. You will learn how data is physically stored, how Delta Lake provides ACID transactions, how shortcuts enable data sharing without copying, and why the Files and Tables sections of a Lakehouse exist as separate concepts. The workspaces are set up. Now it is time to understand what lives inside them.

chapters.title: OneLake — The Unified Data Lake
chapters.description: Understand OneLake as the single storage layer underlying all Fabric workloads, learn how data is organized and accessed, and grasp why a unified lake eliminates the data duplication that plagued NordGrid's previous architecture.

blocks.content: Module 1 introduced Fabric's layered architecture — workloads at the top, shared compute in the middle, and shared storage at the bottom. Module 2 taught you how workspaces organize items and control access. This module goes one layer deeper: the storage foundation that makes everything else possible. OneLake is Fabric's built-in, organization-wide data lake. Every Lakehouse table, every Warehouse table, every file uploaded through the portal, and every dataset written by a notebook — all of it lives in OneLake. There is no separate storage account to provision, no bucket to create, no connection string to configure. OneLake is simply there, the moment your organization activates Fabric.
The OneDrive Analogy

The simplest way to understand OneLake is by comparison to OneDrive. In Microsoft 365, every user gets one OneDrive — a personal storage space that is automatically provisioned, automatically managed, and accessible from any Microsoft application. You do not create a OneDrive account or configure storage limits. It is part of the platform. OneLake works the same way, but for data. Every Fabric tenant gets one OneLake — an organizational data lake that is automatically provisioned, automatically managed, and accessible from every Fabric workload. You do not create a storage account, configure redundancy, or manage capacity. OneLake is part of Fabric.

The analogy extends further. OneDrive organizes files into folders that users create and share. OneLake organizes data into workspaces and items that administrators create and share. OneDrive files are accessible from Word, Excel, PowerPoint, and Teams — the same file, opened from different applications. OneLake data is accessible from notebooks, SQL queries, Power BI reports, and pipelines — the same data, accessed from different workloads. This shared access without copying is the single most important property of OneLake.
One Copy, Many Readers

In NordGrid's old architecture, the same meter readings existed in three places: Azure Data Lake Storage (raw files), Databricks Delta tables (transformed data), and Synapse managed storage (query-optimized copies). When a data quality fix was applied in Databricks, the Synapse copy remained stale until someone manually refreshed it. In Fabric, NordGrid's meter readings exist in one place — OneLake. When a notebook writes a Silver Delta table, that table is immediately queryable through the SQL analytics endpoint, immediately available to Power BI through the semantic model, and immediately accessible to the Warehouse through cross-database queries. There is no refresh delay because there is no copy to refresh.

blocks.title: One copy in OneLake, accessed by every workload
blocks.caption: Every Fabric workload reads from and writes to the same OneLake storage. There is no copying between engines — a table written by Spark is immediately queryable by SQL and visible to Power BI.

blocks.content: What OneLake Is Not

OneLake is not a database — it does not have a query engine built into the storage layer. The query engines (Spark, T-SQL, KQL) run separately and read from OneLake. OneLake is not a file system in the traditional sense — you do not SSH into it or mount it as a drive (though there is a Windows file explorer tool for browsing). OneLake is not optional — you cannot choose to store Fabric data somewhere else. Every Fabric item stores its data in OneLake, full stop. Understanding this mandatory, centralized nature is key: it is the design decision that eliminates data duplication and makes shared access possible.

blocks.title: Why OneLake matters more than any single workload
blocks.content: If you remember one concept from this module, remember this: OneLake is why Fabric is a unified platform and not just a bundle of tools. Without shared storage, the SQL engine and the Spark engine would need separate copies of the data — and you are back to NordGrid's old problem of duplication and staleness. OneLake is the foundation that makes unification real, not just marketing.

OneLake stores data as files and folders, organized in a hierarchy that mirrors Fabric's organizational model. Under the hood, OneLake is built on Azure Data Lake Storage Gen2, which means it supports hierarchical namespaces, role-based access control, and the ADLS Gen2 API. But as a Fabric user, you rarely interact with ADLS Gen2 directly — Fabric abstracts the storage layer so that you work with Lakehouses, tables, and files rather than containers and blobs. This lesson explains how OneLake organizes data physically, what the folder hierarchy looks like, and how NordGrid's items map to paths in OneLake.
The Hierarchy

OneLake's hierarchy has four levels. At the top is the tenant — your organization's Fabric instance. Below the tenant are workspaces — the same workspaces you created in Module 2. Below each workspace are items — Lakehouses, Warehouses, and other data-storing items. Below each item are the actual data files and folders. This hierarchy means that every file in OneLake has a unique path that includes the workspace name and the item name, making it possible to locate any piece of data in the organization by following the path from tenant to workspace to item to file.

blocks.title: OneLake's four-level hierarchy
blocks.content:
Tenant: GridUnion Continental
│
├── Workspace: ws_nordgrid_bronze
│     │
│     └── Lakehouse: lh_meter_readings
│           ├── Files/
│           │     └── meter_exports/
│           │           └── 2025/03/23/
│           │                 └── readings.csv
│           └── Tables/
│                 └── (empty in Bronze)
│
├── Workspace: ws_nordgrid_silver
│     │
│     └── Lakehouse: lh_meter_readings
│           ├── Files/
│           │     └── (empty — Silver uses tables)
│           └── Tables/
│                 └── silver_meters/
│                       ├── _delta_log/
│                       ├── year=2025/
│                       │     └── month=03/
│                       │           └── part-00001.parquet
│                       └── ...
│
└── Workspace: ws_nordgrid_gold
      └── ...
blocks.caption: Every file in OneLake has a path that starts with the workspace and item. NordGrid's Bronze Lakehouse stores raw CSV files in the Files section. The Silver Lakehouse stores Delta tables in the Tables section, with Parquet files organized by partition columns.

blocks.content: The abfss:// Path

When you access OneLake programmatically — from a notebook, an external tool, or an API — you use a path that starts with abfss://. This is the Azure Blob File System Secure protocol, the same protocol used by ADLS Gen2. A full OneLake path looks like abfss://workspace-name@onelake.dfs.fabric.microsoft.com/item-name/path/to/file. For NordGrid's Bronze meter readings, the path would be abfss://ws_nordgrid_bronze@onelake.dfs.fabric.microsoft.com/lh_meter_readings.Lakehouse/Files/meter_exports/2025/03/23/readings.csv. You do not need to memorize this path format — Fabric notebooks provide shorthand paths that are much simpler.
Shorthand Paths in Notebooks

Inside a Fabric notebook, you can reference Lakehouse data using simplified paths. When a Lakehouse is attached to a notebook, you can read its tables by name (spark.read.table('silver_meters')) and access its files using relative paths ('Files/meter_exports/2025/03/23/'). The notebook automatically resolves these shorthand references to the full abfss:// path. NordGrid's engineers use shorthand paths exclusively in their notebooks — the full path is only needed when accessing OneLake from outside Fabric or when referencing a Lakehouse in a different workspace.

# Shorthand: reading a table from the attached Lakehouse
df = spark.read.table("silver_meters")

# Shorthand: reading a file from the attached Lakehouse's Files section
df_raw = spark.read.csv("Files/meter_exports/2025/03/23/readings.csv", header=True)

# Full path: reading from a different workspace's Lakehouse
df_bronze = spark.read.csv(
    "abfss://ws_nordgrid_bronze@onelake.dfs.fabric.microsoft.com/"
    "lh_meter_readings.Lakehouse/Files/meter_exports/2025/03/23/readings.csv",
    header=True
)

The first two examples use shorthand paths that work because a Lakehouse is attached to the notebook. The third example uses the full abfss:// path to read from a Lakehouse in a different workspace. In practice, NordGrid's notebooks always attach the Lakehouse they primarily read from, making shorthand the default. Cross-workspace reads using full paths are less common and typically appear in Gold-layer notebooks that need to reference Silver tables in a different workspace.

blocks.title: Files are just files
blocks.content: OneLake does not impose a proprietary format on your data. A CSV file in OneLake is a standard CSV file. A Parquet file in OneLake is a standard Parquet file. Any tool that can read ADLS Gen2 can read OneLake — no special connector, no proprietary SDK. This openness is deliberate: it means your data is not locked into Fabric. If NordGrid decided to migrate away from Fabric, the data in OneLake is standard-format files that any other platform can read.

When you create a table in a Fabric Lakehouse, the table is stored in Delta Lake format — not raw CSV, not plain Parquet, but Delta. Delta Lake is the default and only managed table format in Fabric. Understanding what Delta is and why Fabric chose it is essential because Delta's properties — ACID transactions, schema enforcement, time travel, and optimized reads — determine what you can and cannot do with Lakehouse tables. This lesson explains Delta Lake at a conceptual level, focusing on the properties that matter for a junior data engineer.
What Delta Lake Is

Delta Lake is an open-source storage layer that sits on top of Parquet files and adds capabilities that plain Parquet lacks. A Delta table is a directory containing Parquet files (the actual data) plus a subdirectory called _delta_log (the transaction log). The Parquet files store the rows and columns — the actual meter readings, the actual daily_kw values. The transaction log records every change to the table: which files were added, which were removed, what the schema looked like at each version. This combination — standard Parquet files plus a structured transaction log — is what makes Delta more than just a file format.

blocks.title: Anatomy of a Delta table
blocks.content:
silver_meters/                         ← Delta table directory
│
├── _delta_log/                        ← Transaction log
│     ├── 00000000000000000000.json    ← Version 0: initial write
│     ├── 00000000000000000001.json    ← Version 1: appended data
│     └── 00000000000000000002.json    ← Version 2: schema change
│
├── year=2025/                         ← Partition directory
│     └── month=03/
│           ├── part-00001.parquet     ← Data file (Parquet)
│           ├── part-00002.parquet
│           └── part-00003.parquet
│
└── year=2024/
      └── month=12/
            └── part-00001.parquet
blocks.caption: A Delta table is a directory with two components: Parquet data files organized by partition columns, and a _delta_log directory that records every change. The transaction log is what gives Delta its power — without it, the Parquet files are just files.

blocks.content: ACID Transactions

The most important property of Delta Lake is ACID transactions. ACID stands for Atomicity, Consistency, Isolation, and Durability — four guarantees that prevent data corruption. In practical terms, this means that when NordGrid's notebook writes 500,000 meter readings to the Silver table, either all 500,000 rows are written successfully, or none of them are. There is no partial write — no scenario where a notebook crash leaves 300,000 rows written and 200,000 missing. If the write fails midway, the transaction log records the failure, and the table remains in its previous state as if the write never started.

Without ACID transactions, NordGrid's old Parquet-based pipeline had a recurring problem: if the Spark job crashed during a write, the output directory contained a mix of complete and incomplete Parquet files. The next pipeline run had to detect and clean up the partial files before writing new ones — fragile logic that itself could fail. Delta eliminates this entire class of problems. The transaction log acts as the definitive record of which files belong to the table. Files not recorded in the log are invisible to readers, even if they physically exist in the directory.
Schema Enforcement

Delta tables enforce a schema — when you write data, Delta checks that the columns, data types, and nullability of the incoming data match the table's registered schema. If a new CSV file from NordGrid's meter vendor suddenly includes an extra column called export_id, a write to the Silver Delta table will fail with a schema mismatch error rather than silently adding an unexpected column. This enforcement catches data quality issues at write time, when they are cheapest to fix, rather than at read time, when a downstream report displays unexpected results.
Time Travel

Because the transaction log records every version of the table, Delta supports time travel — the ability to read the table as it existed at a previous point in time. If NordGrid's Silver table was accidentally overwritten with bad data this morning, the team can query yesterday's version to see what the correct data looked like, and restore it. Time travel is accessed by specifying a version number or a timestamp when reading the table. NordGrid's team uses time travel primarily for debugging: when a Gold aggregate shows unexpected numbers, they query the Silver table at the version that the Gold pipeline read from, to determine whether the problem is in the Silver data or the Gold aggregation logic.

# Read the current version of the Silver table
df_current = spark.read.table("silver_meters")

# Read the table as it existed at version 5
df_v5 = spark.read.format("delta").option("versionAsOf", 5).table("silver_meters")

# Read the table as it existed yesterday at noon
df_yesterday = (
    spark.read.format("delta")
    .option("timestampAsOf", "2025-03-22T12:00:00")
    .table("silver_meters")
)

blocks.title: Time travel has limits
blocks.content: Delta retains historical versions for a configurable retention period (default 30 days in Fabric). After that period, old versions are cleaned up by a maintenance process called VACUUM. You cannot time-travel to a version that has been vacuumed. NordGrid's platform team sets the retention period to 14 days for Silver tables — long enough to debug most issues, short enough to keep storage costs manageable.

Every Lakehouse in Fabric has two sections visible in the portal explorer: Files and Tables. This dual structure is one of the first things you notice when you open a Lakehouse, and understanding the difference is essential because it determines how data is stored, how it is accessed, and which tools can read it. Files holds unmanaged data — any file in any format. Tables holds managed Delta tables with schemas, transaction logs, and automatic SQL endpoint access. The two sections serve different purposes in the pipeline, and NordGrid uses both.
The Files Section

The Files section is an unmanaged area where you can store any file: CSVs, JSONs, Parquet files, images, PDFs, or any other format. Unmanaged means that Fabric does not track these files in a transaction log, does not enforce a schema on them, and does not make them visible through the SQL analytics endpoint. Files in this section are just files — they sit in OneLake at the path lh_name.Lakehouse/Files/your_folder/your_file. You can organize them into any folder structure you want.

NordGrid uses the Files section exclusively for Bronze data. Raw CSV meter exports, JSON weather forecasts, and Parquet SCADA files land in the Files section of the Bronze Lakehouse. The folder structure follows a convention: source_name/year/month/day/filename. For example, meter_exports/2025/03/23/readings.csv. This structure makes it easy to locate a specific day's data and enables partition-style reading in Spark notebooks, even though the files are not part of a managed table.
The Tables Section

The Tables section holds managed Delta tables. When you write a DataFrame to a table using saveAsTable() in a notebook, the table appears in the Tables section. Managed means that Fabric tracks the table in a metadata catalog, enforces its schema, maintains the Delta transaction log, and automatically exposes it through the SQL analytics endpoint and the default semantic model. Tables in this section are first-class data assets — they are queryable, governed, and discoverable.

NordGrid uses the Tables section for Silver and Gold data. The Silver Lakehouse's Tables section contains silver_meters (validated meter readings) and silver_scada (validated turbine telemetry). The Gold Lakehouse's Tables section contains gold_daily_summary and gold_monthly_billing. Every table in the Tables section is automatically available through the Lakehouse's SQL analytics endpoint, which means analysts can query Silver and Gold data using T-SQL without any additional configuration.

left.label: Files Section
points: Any file format: CSV, JSON, Parquet, images, PDFs
No schema enforcement — files are stored as-is
Not visible in the SQL analytics endpoint
No transaction log — partial writes are possible
NordGrid use: Bronze raw data landing zone

right.label: Tables Section
points: Delta format only (Parquet + transaction log)
Schema enforced on every write
Automatically queryable through T-SQL
ACID transactions — writes are atomic
NordGrid use: Silver validated data, Gold aggregates

blocks.content: The Pipeline Pattern

The Files-to-Tables progression mirrors the Bronze-to-Silver transition. Raw data arrives in the Files section (unmanaged, unvalidated, any format). A notebook reads from Files, transforms the data, and writes it to the Tables section (managed, validated, Delta format). This pattern repeats in every NordGrid pipeline: data enters as files, exits as tables. The Files section is the inbox; the Tables section is the filing cabinet. You never serve downstream consumers from the Files section — you always promote data to a managed table before anyone else reads it.

blocks.title: Data promotion from Files to Tables
blocks.content:
Bronze Lakehouse                    Silver Lakehouse
┌──────────────────────┐           ┌──────────────────────┐
│ Files/                │           │ Tables/               │
│  └─ meter_exports/    │           │  └─ silver_meters     │
│      └─ readings.csv  │──────────▶│      (Delta table)    │
│                       │  notebook │                       │
│ Tables/               │ transform │ Files/                │
│  └─ (empty)           │           │  └─ (empty)           │
└──────────────────────┘           └──────────────────────┘

Raw files land in Files ──▶ Notebook transforms ──▶ Clean data in Tables
blocks.caption: The typical Fabric pipeline reads raw files from the Bronze Files section, transforms them in a notebook, and writes managed Delta tables to the Silver Tables section. This promotion from unmanaged files to managed tables is the core of the Bronze-to-Silver pattern.

blocks.title: Why both exist
blocks.content: You might wonder why Fabric has a Files section at all — why not require everything to be a Delta table? Because raw data arrives in formats that are not Delta: CSV exports from vendors, JSON responses from APIs, Parquet dumps from partner systems. Forcing everything into Delta at ingestion time would require transformation before storage, which violates the Bronze principle of storing data exactly as it arrived. The Files section exists so that Bronze can be truly raw, and the Tables section exists so that Silver and Gold can be truly managed.

OneLake eliminates data duplication by storing one copy of every dataset. But what happens when two workspaces need to access the same table? NordGrid's Silver Lakehouse contains the silver_meters table, and both the Gold workspace and a data science workspace need to read it. One option is to copy the table into each workspace — but that recreates the duplication problem that Fabric was designed to solve. The better option is a shortcut: a pointer that makes data in one location appear as if it exists in another, without copying a single byte. This lesson explains how shortcuts work, when to use them, and how NordGrid uses them in practice.
What a Shortcut Is

A shortcut is a symbolic link — a reference that points to data stored elsewhere. When you create a shortcut in a Lakehouse, a new entry appears in the Files or Tables section that looks like a regular file or table, but the data it references lives in the original location. Reading from the shortcut reads from the original data. There is no copy, no sync process, no delay. If the original data is updated, the shortcut immediately reflects the update because it is reading the same underlying files in OneLake.

Think of a shortcut like a library catalog card that says 'this book is on shelf 3, row B.' The card does not contain the book — it tells you where to find it. If someone moves the book to shelf 4, the card becomes stale. But OneLake shortcuts are smarter than catalog cards: they point to a location in OneLake, and the data at that location is always the current version. There is no staleness because there is no copy to become stale.
Internal Shortcuts

An internal shortcut points from one Fabric item to another within the same OneLake. NordGrid uses internal shortcuts to make Silver tables accessible in the Gold workspace. Instead of copying the silver_meters table into the Gold Lakehouse, the Gold Lakehouse contains a shortcut that points to the Silver Lakehouse's table. When a Gold notebook or a Warehouse query reads from this shortcut, it reads the Silver data directly. If the Silver table is updated by the nightly pipeline, the Gold workspace sees the updated data immediately.

blocks.title: Internal shortcut from Gold to Silver
blocks.content:
ws_nordgrid_silver                    ws_nordgrid_gold
┌──────────────────────┐           ┌──────────────────────┐
│ lh_meter_readings     │           │ lh_grid_analytics     │
│  └─ Tables/           │           │  └─ Tables/           │
│      └─ silver_meters │◀─ ─ ─ ─ ─│      └─ silver_meters │
│         (real data)   │  shortcut │         (shortcut)    │
└──────────────────────┘           └──────────────────────┘

  The Gold Lakehouse's silver_meters is a shortcut.
  It reads from the Silver Lakehouse's actual data.
  Zero bytes copied. Zero sync delay.
blocks.caption: An internal shortcut in the Gold Lakehouse points to the Silver Lakehouse's table. The Gold workspace can read Silver data without copying it, and any updates to the Silver table are immediately visible through the shortcut.

blocks.content: External Shortcuts

An external shortcut points to data stored outside of Fabric — in Azure Data Lake Storage Gen2, Amazon S3, or Google Cloud Storage. This is useful when an organization is migrating to Fabric gradually and some data still lives in external storage. NordGrid used external shortcuts during their migration: before the Bronze pipelines were fully operational in Fabric, they created shortcuts from the Bronze Lakehouse to their existing ADLS Gen2 account. Notebooks could read the external data as if it were in OneLake, without waiting for the full migration to complete.

rows: Another Lakehouse or Warehouse in Fabric
Gold workspace shortcuts to Silver tables
Azure Data Lake Storage Gen2 account
Migrating data incrementally, accessing legacy storage
Bronze shortcuts to existing ADLS during migration

blocks.content: When to Use Shortcuts

Use shortcuts when you need to read data from another location without owning or transforming it. NordGrid's Gold workspace reads Silver tables through shortcuts because the Gold workspace does not modify the Silver data — it only reads it for aggregation. If the Gold workspace needed to transform the Silver data (add columns, filter rows, change types), the correct approach would be to read from the Silver Lakehouse in a notebook and write a new table in the Gold Lakehouse, not to shortcut the data. Shortcuts are for sharing, not for transformation.

blocks.title: Shortcuts inherit the source's security
blocks.content: A shortcut does not bypass access control. If you create a shortcut to a Silver table in the Gold workspace, users who access the shortcut still need at least read permission on the source Silver table. A shortcut makes data discoverable in a new location, but it does not grant access that the user does not already have. NordGrid's analysts can read the Silver shortcut in the Gold workspace because they have Viewer access to the Silver workspace.

OneLake stores files in open formats — standard file formats that any tool can read, not proprietary formats locked to Microsoft. Understanding which format to use where is a practical decision that affects storage cost, query performance, and compatibility with downstream tools. NordGrid's pipeline uses four formats, each chosen for a specific stage in the data lifecycle. This lesson explains each format, its strengths and weaknesses, and when it appears in the NordGrid pipeline.
CSV — The Universal Input

CSV (Comma-Separated Values) is the format that most raw data arrives in. It is human-readable, universally supported, and the default export format for databases, SaaS platforms, and government data portals. NordGrid's meter readings arrive as CSV because the meter collection system was built a decade ago when CSV was the only format the vendor supported. CSV's weaknesses are significant: no built-in schema (column types are ambiguous), no compression (files are large), and no column pruning (reading three columns requires scanning all columns). These weaknesses are acceptable for Bronze because Bronze stores data as-is. They are unacceptable for Silver, which is why the transformation pipeline converts CSV to Delta.
JSON — The API Format

JSON (JavaScript Object Notation) is the standard format for API responses and nested data. NordGrid's weather data arrives as JSON from an external API: each record contains a station_id, a temperature reading, and a nested array of hourly forecasts. JSON supports nested structures that CSV cannot represent, but it shares CSV's weaknesses: no compression, no column pruning, and no built-in schema. JSON appears in NordGrid's Bronze layer for API-sourced data and is converted to flat Delta tables during the Silver transformation.
Parquet — The Columnar Standard

Parquet is a columnar file format designed for analytical workloads. Unlike CSV (which stores data row by row), Parquet stores data column by column. This means that a query reading only three columns out of twenty reads only those three columns from disk — the other seventeen are never touched. Parquet also supports built-in compression (typically reducing file sizes by 60-80% compared to CSV), self-describing schemas (the file includes column names and types), and predicate pushdown (the query engine can skip entire row groups that do not match a filter). NordGrid's SCADA telemetry arrives as Parquet because the turbine vendor's modern export system supports it.
Delta — The Managed Table Format

Delta Lake, introduced in lesson 3, is not a replacement for Parquet — it is an enhancement. A Delta table is a collection of Parquet files plus a transaction log. Every managed table in a Fabric Lakehouse is a Delta table. Delta adds ACID transactions, schema enforcement, and time travel on top of Parquet's columnar performance. The cost is minimal: the transaction log is a set of small JSON files that add negligible storage overhead. Every Silver and Gold table in NordGrid's pipeline is Delta.

rows: Bronze: SCADA telemetry from modern vendors
Silver and Gold: all managed tables

blocks.content: The Format Progression

NordGrid's data follows a format progression as it moves through the medallion layers. Bronze stores whatever format the source provides — CSV, JSON, or Parquet. The Bronze Lakehouse's Files section is format-agnostic. Silver converts everything to Delta. Regardless of whether the Bronze source was CSV, JSON, or Parquet, the Silver notebook reads the raw file, transforms it, and writes a managed Delta table. Gold is also Delta (or Warehouse tables, which use a similar managed format). This means that by the time data reaches consumers — analysts, reports, dashboards — it is always in a high-performance, schema-enforced, transactional format.

blocks.title: Format choice is a pipeline decision, not a preference
blocks.content: Junior engineers sometimes ask which format is 'best.' The answer depends on where in the pipeline you are. For Bronze, the best format is whatever the source provides — do not transform at ingestion. For Silver and Gold, the best format is always Delta, because Delta provides the transactions, schema enforcement, and time travel that production systems require. The format decision is not about preference — it is determined by the data's position in the pipeline.

OneLake stores all of an organization's data in one place, which makes security critically important. If OneLake's security model were weak, centralizing data would be a liability — one misconfiguration could expose everything. Fabric addresses this with a layered security model: workspace roles control who can access items, OneLake security controls who can access specific tables and folders within items, and Microsoft Purview provides organization-wide governance policies. This lesson explains the security model at a level appropriate for junior data engineers — you need to understand how security works so you can design pipelines that respect it, even if the platform team configures most of it.
Layer 1 — Workspace Roles

The first security layer is workspace roles, which you learned in Module 2. Workspace roles control access at the item level: if you have Contributor access to ws_nordgrid_silver, you can read and write to every Lakehouse, notebook, and pipeline in that workspace. If you have Viewer access, you can read but not write. If you have no role in the workspace, you cannot see its items at all. Workspace roles are the broadest security control — they grant or deny access to everything in the workspace as a whole.
Layer 2 — OneLake Security

Workspace roles are sometimes too broad. NordGrid's Silver workspace contains both meter readings (general data) and customer billing details (sensitive data). The data engineering team needs access to both, but the analyst team should only see the meter readings, not the billing data. OneLake security addresses this with table-level and folder-level permissions that operate within a workspace. An administrator can grant the analyst team read access to the silver_meters table while denying access to the silver_billing table — even though both tables are in the same Lakehouse in the same workspace.

OneLake security also supports row-level security and column-level security for more granular control. Row-level security filters which rows a user can see — for example, NordGrid could restrict an analyst to seeing only NORTH region data. Column-level security hides specific columns — for example, hiding the customer_type column from users who do not need it. These controls apply consistently across all Fabric engines: a SQL query, a Spark notebook, and a Power BI report all see the same filtered view.
Layer 3 — Microsoft Purview

Microsoft Purview is the governance and compliance platform that applies organization-wide policies to Fabric. Purview manages sensitivity labels (marking data as 'General', 'Confidential', or 'Highly Confidential'), data loss prevention policies (preventing sensitive data from being exported), and audit logs (recording who accessed what data and when). NordGrid's compliance team uses Purview to ensure that meter readings labeled 'Confidential' cannot be downloaded to a personal device, and that all access to billing data is logged for regulatory audits.

blocks.title: OneLake's three-layer security model
blocks.content:
Layer 3: Microsoft Purview
  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │ Sensitivity labels, DLP policies, audit │
  │ (Organization-wide, applied to domains) │
  └─────────────────────────────────────────┘
                    │
Layer 2: OneLake Security
  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │ Table-level, row-level, column-level    │
  │ (Within a workspace, per-item)          │
  └─────────────────────────────────────────┘
                    │
Layer 1: Workspace Roles
  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │ Admin, Member, Contributor, Viewer      │
  │ (Per workspace, all items in workspace) │
  └─────────────────────────────────────────┘
blocks.caption: Security in Fabric is layered. Workspace roles provide the broadest control. OneLake security adds granularity within a workspace. Purview adds organizational policies. Each layer narrows access further — a user must pass all three layers to access specific data.

blocks.content: What Junior Engineers Need to Know

As a junior data engineer, you will not configure OneLake security or Purview policies — that is the platform team's responsibility. But you need to understand the model for two reasons. First, your pipeline output must respect security boundaries. If the Silver Lakehouse contains both general and sensitive tables, your notebook must write to the correct table so that downstream security rules apply. Writing sensitive billing data to the general-access silver_meters table bypasses the security model. Second, when debugging, you may encounter access errors that are not bugs — they are security policies working as intended. Knowing that OneLake security exists helps you diagnose these situations quickly.

blocks.title: Security enables sharing
blocks.content: Security is not the opposite of sharing — it is what makes sharing safe. NordGrid can store meter readings and billing data in the same Lakehouse precisely because OneLake security can restrict access at the table level. Without granular security, NordGrid would need separate Lakehouses (and possibly separate workspaces) for every sensitivity level, which recreates the fragmentation that Fabric was designed to eliminate. Good security enables more sharing, not less.

Most of the time, you access OneLake data from inside Fabric — through notebooks, SQL queries, pipelines, and Power BI reports. But sometimes you need to access OneLake from outside the portal: browsing files on your local machine during development, connecting a third-party tool to OneLake data, or building an application that reads from OneLake programmatically. Fabric supports several external access methods, each designed for a different use case. This lesson introduces the methods NordGrid's engineers use and when each is appropriate.
OneLake File Explorer

The OneLake file explorer is a Windows application that integrates OneLake with Windows Explorer. Once installed, a OneLake drive appears in your file system — similar to how OneDrive appears as a drive. You can browse workspaces and Lakehouses as folders, open files by double-clicking, and drag files into the Bronze Lakehouse's Files section. NordGrid's engineers use the file explorer during development to quickly inspect raw CSV files that the pipeline ingested, or to upload a small test file to the Bronze Lakehouse without opening the portal. It is a convenience tool for development, not a production data access method.
Azure Storage Explorer

Azure Storage Explorer is a cross-platform desktop application for managing Azure storage accounts. Because OneLake is built on ADLS Gen2, Storage Explorer can connect to OneLake using the DFS endpoint (onelake.dfs.fabric.microsoft.com). This provides a detailed view of the OneLake hierarchy — tenants, workspaces, items, and files — with the ability to upload, download, delete, and manage files. NordGrid's engineers use Storage Explorer when they need to inspect the physical structure of a Delta table (viewing the _delta_log files and partition directories directly) — something the portal's Lakehouse explorer does not expose.
ADLS Gen2 APIs

For programmatic access, OneLake supports the ADLS Gen2 REST API and the Azure SDK for Python, .NET, and Java. Any application that can read from ADLS Gen2 can read from OneLake by using the OneLake DFS endpoint (onelake.dfs.fabric.microsoft.com) instead of the standard ADLS endpoint. Authentication uses Microsoft Entra ID (formerly Azure Active Directory). NordGrid's monitoring application uses the Python SDK to check whether today's meter file has landed in the Bronze Lakehouse — a lightweight health check that runs outside Fabric.

rows: Browsing files during development, quick uploads
Inspecting raw CSV files, uploading test data
Detailed file management, inspecting Delta internals
Cross-platform Spark jobs reading OneLake data
Not used — NordGrid runs Spark inside Fabric

blocks.content: The Open Access Principle

OneLake's support for standard protocols and APIs reflects a deliberate design choice: your data is not locked in. Unlike proprietary platforms where data can only be accessed through the vendor's tools, OneLake data is standard-format files accessible through standard protocols. A CSV in OneLake is a real CSV. A Parquet file is a real Parquet file. The ADLS Gen2 API is a standard Azure API. This openness means that NordGrid can access their data from any tool that supports ADLS Gen2, and they could migrate their data out of Fabric without format conversion.

blocks.title: External access respects security
blocks.content: Accessing OneLake from outside Fabric does not bypass Fabric's security model. The OneLake file explorer, Storage Explorer, and ADLS Gen2 APIs all authenticate through Microsoft Entra ID and respect workspace roles and OneLake security policies. You cannot read a table through the API that you could not read through the portal. External access is a different interface to the same data with the same security rules.

If you have heard of data lakes before — or if you have used Azure Data Lake Storage, Amazon S3, or Google Cloud Storage — you might wonder how OneLake differs. The concept is the same: a centralized storage layer for large-scale data. But the implementation differs significantly. Traditional data lakes require you to provision storage, configure security, manage access keys, and integrate with compute engines manually. OneLake is built into Fabric and handles all of this automatically. This lesson compares the two approaches across the dimensions that matter for data engineers.
Provisioning

In a traditional data lake, the first step is provisioning: creating a storage account, choosing a region, configuring redundancy (LRS, ZRS, GRS), setting up the hierarchical namespace, and creating containers. For NordGrid's ADLS Gen2 account, this setup took half a day and required coordination with the infrastructure team. In Fabric, OneLake is already provisioned. The moment you create a workspace and a Lakehouse, the storage exists. There is no storage account to create, no region to choose, no redundancy to configure. Fabric handles all of it.
Security Configuration

Traditional data lakes use a combination of storage account keys, shared access signatures (SAS tokens), and RBAC (role-based access control) applied to containers and folders. NordGrid's ADLS Gen2 account required managing access keys (which had to be rotated quarterly), creating SAS tokens for partner access (which expired and broke pipelines), and configuring RBAC policies for each container. OneLake eliminates all of this. Security is managed through workspace roles, OneLake security, and Purview policies — all integrated into Fabric's portal. There are no storage keys, no SAS tokens, and no separate security configuration.

left.label: Traditional Data Lake (ADLS Gen2)
points: Must provision storage account, choose region and redundancy
Security via storage keys, SAS tokens, and RBAC on containers
Compute engines (Spark, SQL) connect via connectors and credentials
Governance applied manually per storage account
You manage capacity, redundancy, and lifecycle policies
Open protocol access (ADLS Gen2 API, abfss://)

right.label: OneLake
points: Automatically provisioned with every Fabric tenant
Security via workspace roles, OneLake security, and Purview
Compute engines read directly — no connectors or credentials needed
Governance applied automatically via domains and Purview
Microsoft manages storage infrastructure entirely
Same open protocol access (ADLS Gen2 API, abfss://)

blocks.content: The Integration Advantage

The biggest practical difference is integration. In NordGrid's old architecture, connecting Databricks to ADLS Gen2 required configuring a service principal, granting it access to the storage account, and storing the credentials in Databricks' secret scope. Connecting Synapse required a different credential. Connecting Power BI required yet another. Each integration was a potential failure point, and rotating credentials meant updating every connected service. In Fabric, there are no credentials to configure. Notebooks, SQL queries, pipelines, and Power BI all read from OneLake natively because they are part of the same platform. The integration is zero-configuration.
What You Lose

OneLake's simplicity comes with trade-offs. You cannot choose the storage region independently of your Fabric capacity region. You cannot configure low-level storage settings like lifecycle policies that automatically move cold data to cheaper tiers. You cannot use OneLake for non-Fabric workloads that require raw ADLS Gen2 features like NFSv3 protocol access. For NordGrid, none of these limitations matter — the simplicity of OneLake outweighs the flexibility of raw ADLS Gen2. For organizations with specialized storage requirements, the trade-offs may be more significant.

blocks.title: The right question
blocks.content: The question is not whether OneLake is better than ADLS Gen2 in absolute terms. ADLS Gen2 is more flexible. OneLake is more integrated. The question is: does your organization need the flexibility more than the integration? For most data engineering teams building analytical pipelines — which is exactly what NordGrid does — the integration advantage of OneLake dramatically reduces operational overhead and eliminates entire categories of configuration errors.

The previous nine lessons covered what OneLake is, how it stores data, why Delta Lake is the default table format, how Files and Tables differ, how shortcuts enable sharing without copying, which data formats appear at each pipeline stage, how security works in layers, how to access OneLake from outside Fabric, and how OneLake compares to traditional data lakes. This lesson synthesizes everything into a single mental model — a way of thinking about OneLake that will serve you throughout the course and throughout your career working with Fabric.
The Mental Model

OneLake is the single storage layer for all Fabric data. Every workspace is a folder in OneLake. Every item is a subfolder within a workspace. Every file and table is a set of physical files within an item's subfolder. Every Fabric engine — Spark, T-SQL, KQL, Power BI — reads from and writes to OneLake directly, without copying data between engines. Security is layered: workspace roles at the broadest level, OneLake security at the table level, Purview policies at the organization level. Data enters OneLake as raw files in any format and is promoted to managed Delta tables through transformation pipelines. Shortcuts enable cross-workspace and cross-cloud data sharing without duplication.

blocks.title: The complete OneLake mental model
blocks.content:
┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                      ONELAKE                                  │
│  ┌─────────────────┐  ┌─────────────────┐  ┌──────────────┐ │
│  │ ws_nordgrid_     │  │ ws_nordgrid_     │  │ ws_nordgrid_ │ │
│  │ bronze           │  │ silver           │  │ gold         │ │
│  │                  │  │                  │  │              │ │
│  │ Files/           │  │ Tables/          │  │ Tables/      │ │
│  │  └─ CSV, JSON    │──│  └─ Delta tables │──│  └─ Delta/   │ │
│  │                  │  │                  │  │     Warehouse│ │
│  │ (raw, any format)│  │ (managed, ACID)  │  │ (aggregated) │ │
│  └─────────────────┘  └─────────────────┘  └──────────────┘ │
│                                                               │
│  Engines:  Spark ──────── T-SQL ──────── Power BI            │
│            (read/write)   (read)          (read)              │
│                                                               │
│  Security: Workspace Roles → OneLake Security → Purview       │
│                                                               │
│  Formats:  CSV/JSON/Parquet (Bronze) → Delta (Silver/Gold)    │
│                                                               │
│  Sharing:  Shortcuts (internal + external, zero-copy)         │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
blocks.caption: OneLake is the single storage layer: workspaces are folders, items are subfolders, data flows from raw files (Bronze) to managed Delta tables (Silver/Gold). Every engine reads from the same storage. Security is layered. Shortcuts enable sharing without copying.

blocks.content: Five Rules of Thumb

items: Raw data lands in Files. NordGrid's Bronze Lakehouse uses the Files section for CSV, JSON, and Parquet files from external sources. The Files section is the inbox — unmanaged, unvalidated, any format.
Clean data lives in Tables. NordGrid's Silver and Gold Lakehouses use the Tables section for managed Delta tables with enforced schemas, ACID transactions, and automatic SQL endpoint access.
Share with shortcuts, not copies. When the Gold workspace needs Silver data, use an internal shortcut. The data stays in one place. Zero bytes copied, zero sync delay, zero staleness.
Delta is the target format. Regardless of what format Bronze data arrives in, the Silver transformation converts everything to Delta. By the time data reaches consumers, it is always Delta.

Security is not your job to configure, but it is your job to respect. Write data to the correct tables, use the correct workspaces, and never bypass security boundaries by writing sensitive data to general-access locations.

blocks.content: How This Connects to What Comes Next

Module 4 takes you inside the Lakehouse — the item type that sits on top of OneLake and provides the Files and Tables sections you learned about in this module. You will learn how to create a Lakehouse, load data into it, explore its contents, and understand the three artifacts that Fabric generates automatically: the Lakehouse itself, the SQL analytics endpoint, and the default semantic model. The Lakehouse is where OneLake becomes tangible — where the abstract storage layer turns into concrete tables that you can query, transform, and serve to downstream consumers.

blocks.title: The foundation is complete
blocks.content: With Modules 1 through 3, you now have the full conceptual foundation: why Fabric exists (Module 1), how it is organized (Module 2), and where data lives (Module 3). Every remaining module builds on this foundation by teaching you how to create items, write code, build pipelines, and serve data. The 'why' and 'where' are settled. Starting with Module 4, the focus shifts to 'how.'

chapters.title: Lakehouses — The Core Abstraction
chapters.description: Master the Lakehouse as the primary data store for Fabric data engineering, understand how tables and files coexist, and learn how the automatically generated SQL analytics endpoint and semantic model extend the Lakehouse's reach.

blocks.content: Modules 1 through 3 built the conceptual foundation: why Fabric exists, how workspaces organize items, and how OneLake stores data as files and Delta tables. Starting with this module, the focus shifts from concepts to concrete Fabric items — the things you create, configure, and use daily as a data engineer. The first and most important item is the Lakehouse. A Lakehouse is a Fabric item that combines the flexibility of a data lake (store any file in any format) with the structure of a data warehouse (query tables with SQL, enforce schemas, track transactions). It is the single item where most data engineering work happens, and understanding it thoroughly is essential for everything that follows.
Lake + Warehouse

The name Lakehouse is a deliberate combination of data lake and data warehouse — two concepts that were historically separate. A data lake stores raw files cheaply and flexibly but lacks structure: no schemas, no SQL access, no transactions. A data warehouse provides structured tables with SQL access and ACID transactions but cannot store unstructured files and is expensive to scale. The Lakehouse merges both: it has a Files section that acts as a data lake (store anything) and a Tables section that acts as a warehouse (managed Delta tables with SQL access). NordGrid uses both sections: raw CSV meter exports land in Files, and validated Delta tables live in Tables.

left.label: Traditional Data Lake
points: Store any file format: CSV, JSON, Parquet, images
No built-in SQL access — requires an external engine
No ACID transactions — partial writes are possible
Cheap storage, scales to petabytes

right.label: Traditional Data Warehouse
points: Store only structured tables with defined schemas
Built-in SQL engine for fast analytical queries
Full ACID transactions — writes are atomic
Expensive storage, optimized for query speed

blocks.content: The Fabric Lakehouse gives you both sides of this comparison in one item. The Files section provides the lake's flexibility. The Tables section provides the warehouse's structure. And because both sections live in OneLake, every Fabric engine — Spark, T-SQL, Power BI — can access the data without copying it. This dual nature is what makes the Lakehouse the central item in Fabric data engineering.
Three Items in One

When you create a Lakehouse in Fabric, you do not get just one thing — you get three. Fabric automatically generates a SQL analytics endpoint and a default semantic model alongside the Lakehouse itself. The SQL analytics endpoint provides a read-only T-SQL interface to the Lakehouse's Delta tables. The semantic model provides a Power BI-ready data layer. These three items appear together in the workspace, sharing the same underlying data. This automatic generation is one of Fabric's most practical features: the moment you write a Delta table in a notebook, analysts can query it with T-SQL and report on it with Power BI, without any additional configuration.

blocks.title: One Lakehouse creates three workspace items
blocks.content:
You create:
                  ┌──────────────────┐
                  │    LAKEHOUSE      │
                  │ lh_meter_readings │
                  └────────┬─────────┘
                           │
             Fabric auto-generates:
              ┌────────────┼────────────┐
              │                         │
              ▼                         ▼
   ┌────────────────────┐   ┌────────────────────┐
   │ SQL Analytics       │   │ Default Semantic    │
   │ Endpoint            │   │ Model               │
   │ (read-only T-SQL)   │   │ (Power BI ready)    │
   └────────────────────┘   └────────────────────┘

   All three share the same Delta tables in OneLake.
blocks.caption: Creating a Lakehouse triggers Fabric to generate two companion items: a SQL analytics endpoint for T-SQL queries and a semantic model for Power BI. All three items read from the same underlying data — there is no copy.

blocks.content: The Lakehouse in NordGrid's Architecture

NordGrid uses Lakehouses at every layer of the medallion architecture. The Bronze workspace has lh_meter_readings, where raw CSV files land in the Files section. The Silver workspace has its own lh_meter_readings, where validated Delta tables live in the Tables section. The Gold workspace has lh_grid_analytics, where aggregated tables live alongside the Warehouse and semantic model. Each Lakehouse serves a different purpose, but they are all the same item type — the Lakehouse — with the same Files/Tables structure and the same auto-generated companions.

blocks.title: The Lakehouse is your home base
blocks.content: As a data engineer, the Lakehouse is the item you interact with most. Your notebooks read from and write to Lakehouses. Your pipelines copy data into Lakehouses. Your SQL queries read from Lakehouse endpoints. Your Power BI reports connect to Lakehouse semantic models. Every other Fabric item — notebooks, pipelines, dataflows, warehouses — exists to move data into, through, or out of Lakehouses. Understanding the Lakehouse deeply is the single highest-leverage investment in your Fabric education.

Creating a Lakehouse is one of the simplest operations in Fabric — it takes less than thirty seconds. But the decisions around the Lakehouse — its name, its workspace, its role in the pipeline, and the items that Fabric generates alongside it — have lasting consequences. This lesson walks through the creation process and explains what happens behind the scenes when you click Create, using NordGrid's Bronze Lakehouse as the example.
The Creation Workflow

To create a Lakehouse, navigate to the workspace where you want it (for example, ws_nordgrid_bronze), click the New button in the toolbar, and select Lakehouse from the item type menu. If the Lakehouse option does not appear, check the workload switcher — it should be set to Data Engineering or Data Factory. Fabric prompts you for a name. Enter the name following your team's convention (NordGrid uses lh_meter_readings), and click Create. The Lakehouse appears in the workspace within seconds, along with its auto-generated SQL analytics endpoint and default semantic model.
What Happens Behind the Scenes

When you create a Lakehouse, Fabric performs several operations in the background. First, it creates a directory structure in OneLake at the path workspace-name/lakehouse-name.Lakehouse/, with two subdirectories: Files/ and Tables/. Second, it registers the Lakehouse in the workspace's item catalog so it appears in the portal. Third, it provisions a SQL analytics endpoint — a read-only T-SQL engine that will expose any Delta tables you create in the Tables section. Fourth, it creates a default semantic model that will automatically include any tables from the Tables section. All four of these operations complete in seconds.

items: OneLake directory created: workspace-name/lh_meter_readings.Lakehouse/ with Files/ and Tables/ subdirectories.
Item registered in the workspace catalog, visible in the portal and the OneLake catalog.
SQL analytics endpoint provisioned: a T-SQL interface that will reflect any Delta tables added to the Tables section.
Default semantic model created: a Power BI data layer that will include all tables from the Tables section.

blocks.content: The Empty Lakehouse

A freshly created Lakehouse is empty. The Files section contains no files. The Tables section contains no tables. The SQL analytics endpoint has no tables to query. The semantic model has no data to report on. The Lakehouse becomes useful only after you load data into it — by uploading files through the portal, copying data with a pipeline, or writing tables from a notebook. NordGrid's Bronze Lakehouse starts empty and is populated by the pl_ingest_meters pipeline that copies CSV files from the SFTP server. The Silver Lakehouse starts empty and is populated by the nb_transform_meters notebook that writes Delta tables.
The Lakehouse Explorer

When you open a Lakehouse in the portal, you see the Lakehouse explorer — a two-pane interface with a tree view on the left and a detail view on the right. The tree view shows the Files and Tables sections as expandable folders. Clicking a folder in Files shows its contents (subfolders and files). Clicking a table in Tables shows a preview of the first rows. The explorer is the portal's primary interface for inspecting what data exists in the Lakehouse, verifying that a pipeline wrote the expected files, and confirming that a notebook created the expected tables.

blocks.title: The Lakehouse explorer in the portal
blocks.caption: The Lakehouse explorer shows Files on the left as a folder tree and Tables as expandable items. Clicking a table previews its rows. This is the primary portal interface for inspecting Lakehouse contents.

blocks.content: Naming Matters

The Lakehouse name becomes part of the OneLake path, appears in the SQL analytics endpoint's connection string, and is referenced by every notebook and pipeline that reads from or writes to it. Renaming a Lakehouse after other items reference it can break those references. NordGrid's naming convention — lh_ prefix followed by the data domain — is chosen for clarity and stability: lh_meter_readings, lh_scada_telemetry, lh_weather_data. The prefix makes the item type instantly recognizable, and the domain name describes the content. Choose the name carefully at creation time, because changing it later is disruptive.

blocks.title: One Lakehouse name, three items
blocks.content: Because the SQL analytics endpoint and semantic model are auto-generated from the Lakehouse, all three items share a name base. If you name your Lakehouse 'test', the SQL endpoint will be 'test' and the semantic model will be 'test'. A workspace with three Lakehouses named 'test', 'data', and 'new' quickly becomes unnavigable. Invest thirty seconds in a good name at creation time — it saves hours of confusion later.

Every Lakehouse has a Files section — an unmanaged storage area where you can place any file in any format. Module 3 introduced the Files section as the Bronze landing zone. This lesson goes deeper: how to organize files within the section, how to upload and manage them through the portal, how Spark notebooks read from them, and the conventions NordGrid follows to keep the Files section navigable as data accumulates over months and years.
What Unmanaged Means

The Files section is unmanaged, which means Fabric does not track or govern the files inside it. There is no schema enforcement — you can place a CSV with 10 columns next to a CSV with 12 columns, and Fabric will not flag the inconsistency. There is no transaction log — if a pipeline writes half a file and then crashes, the half-written file remains. There is no automatic SQL access — files in the Files section are not queryable through the SQL analytics endpoint. The Files section is a raw storage area, a shelf where data sits until a notebook or pipeline processes it.

This lack of management is intentional. The Files section exists so that Bronze data can be stored exactly as it arrives, without Fabric imposing structure. NordGrid's meter vendor sends CSV files with occasional extra columns, inconsistent date formats, and embedded headers. If the Bronze landing zone enforced a schema, these files would be rejected. Instead, the Files section accepts everything, and the Silver transformation notebook handles the inconsistencies during processing.
Folder Organization

Fabric does not impose a folder structure on the Files section — you can create any folders you want. NordGrid uses a convention that organizes files by source and date: source_name/year/month/day/filename. The Bronze Lakehouse's Files section has folders like meter_exports/2025/03/23/readings.csv and weather_data/2025/03/23/forecast.json. This convention serves two purposes: it prevents naming collisions (today's file does not overwrite yesterday's), and it enables time-based processing in notebooks (read all files for March 2025 by specifying the path meter_exports/2025/03/*).

# Reading a specific day's file from the Files section
df_today = spark.read.csv(
    "Files/meter_exports/2025/03/23/readings.csv",
    header=True,
    inferSchema=False  # Never infer schema in production — use explicit schema
)

# Reading all files for March 2025 using a wildcard path
df_march = spark.read.csv(
    "Files/meter_exports/2025/03/*/readings.csv",
    header=True,
    inferSchema=False
)

# Reading all files for the entire year
df_year = spark.read.csv(
    "Files/meter_exports/2025/*/*/readings.csv",
    header=True,
    inferSchema=False
)

The wildcard paths in the code above are possible because of the date-partitioned folder structure. If NordGrid stored all files in a flat folder (Files/meter_exports/readings_20250323.csv, readings_20250322.csv, etc.), reading a specific month would require listing all files and filtering by name — slower and more error-prone. The hierarchical folder structure turns file selection into a path operation, which Spark handles efficiently.
Uploading Files

There are three ways to get files into the Files section. The first is manual upload through the portal: open the Lakehouse explorer, navigate to the target folder in the Files section, and click the Upload button. This is useful for small files during development but impractical for daily production ingestion. The second is a Data Factory pipeline with a Copy activity, which is how NordGrid's daily meter ingestion works — the pipeline copies the CSV from the SFTP server to the Files section automatically. The third is writing from a notebook using Spark's file system APIs, which NordGrid uses for temporary intermediate files during complex transformations.

rows: Click Upload in the Lakehouse explorer
Small files during development, ad-hoc uploads
Uploading test CSV files for notebook development
Data Factory pipeline copies from external source
Scheduled production ingestion from external systems
Daily meter CSV ingestion from SFTP server
Spark file system API writes files programmatically
Saving temporary checkpoint files during reprocessing

blocks.title: Files are the inbox, not the library
blocks.content: Think of the Files section as an inbox: data arrives, sits temporarily, and then gets processed into managed tables. It is not a permanent archive. NordGrid's platform team sets a policy that Bronze files older than 90 days are moved to cold storage (or deleted, if the Silver table contains the processed version). Without this policy, the Files section grows indefinitely — NordGrid's 1.5 GB daily file accumulates to 550 GB per year.

The Tables section is where the Lakehouse becomes a warehouse. Unlike the Files section, which accepts any file without structure, the Tables section holds managed Delta tables with enforced schemas, ACID transactions, and automatic SQL access. When you write a DataFrame to a table using saveAsTable() in a notebook, the table appears in the Tables section, becomes queryable through the SQL analytics endpoint, and becomes visible in the default semantic model. The Tables section is the destination for all validated data — the place where messy Bronze files become clean, queryable, trustworthy Silver and Gold tables.
Creating Tables

Tables in the Tables section are created by writing data from a Spark notebook. There is no portal form for creating an empty table — you create tables by writing to them. The most common method is saveAsTable(), which writes a Spark DataFrame as a managed Delta table. The first write creates the table and defines its schema. Subsequent writes (using append or overwrite mode) add or replace data while maintaining the Delta transaction log.

# Create a new table by writing a DataFrame
# The first write defines the table's schema
