df_clean.write.mode("overwrite").saveAsTable("silver_meters")

# Append new data to an existing table
# The schema must match — Delta enforces this
df_today.write.mode("append").saveAsTable("silver_meters")

# Write with partitioning — organizes data into subdirectories
# by the specified columns for efficient reads
df_clean.write \
    .mode("overwrite") \
    .partitionBy("year", "month") \
    .saveAsTable("silver_meters")

The partitionBy option in the third example organizes the Parquet files inside the Delta table into directories by year and month. When a downstream query filters by year=2025 and month=03, Spark reads only the partition directory for that month, skipping all other data. NordGrid partitions all Silver tables by year and month because most analytical queries are time-bounded — the operations dashboard shows the last 30 days, the billing system processes one month at a time, and the regulatory report covers one quarter.
What Happens When You Write

When saveAsTable() executes, several things happen in sequence. Spark writes Parquet files into the Tables section of the Lakehouse's OneLake directory, organized by partition columns if specified. Delta writes a new entry to the _delta_log recording which files were added and what the schema looks like. The SQL analytics endpoint detects the new or updated table and makes it queryable via T-SQL — typically within seconds. The semantic model updates to include the table's columns. All of this happens automatically. The data engineer writes one line of code, and four systems update in response.
Managed vs External Tables

Tables created with saveAsTable() are managed tables — Fabric owns both the metadata (schema, partitioning, properties) and the underlying data files. If you drop a managed table, both the metadata and the data files are deleted. This is the default and recommended approach for Silver and Gold tables. There are also external tables, which point to data stored at a different location (like the Files section or an external shortcut). External tables have metadata in the catalog but do not own the data — dropping them removes the metadata but leaves the files. NordGrid uses managed tables for all Silver and Gold data because managed tables provide the tightest integration with the SQL endpoint and semantic model.

left.label: Managed Table
points: Created with saveAsTable() — Fabric manages data and metadata
Dropping the table deletes the data files
Full SQL endpoint and semantic model integration
NordGrid use: all Silver and Gold tables

right.label: External Table
points: Points to existing files at a specified path
Dropping the table removes only the catalog entry — files remain
SQL endpoint access depends on format and location
NordGrid use: temporary tables over Files section data during exploration

blocks.content: Viewing Table Details

In the Lakehouse explorer, clicking a table name in the Tables section shows a preview of the first rows and a Schema tab that lists every column with its name, data type, and nullability. NordGrid's engineers use the Schema tab to verify that a notebook's output matches the expected Silver schema — a quick visual check before running downstream pipelines. The preview is limited to 100 rows and does not support filtering; for more detailed inspection, open the table in the SQL analytics endpoint and run a T-SQL query.

blocks.title: Schema changes require care
blocks.content: Once a table exists with a schema, Delta enforces that schema on every subsequent write. If your notebook tries to write a DataFrame with a different schema — a new column, a changed type, a removed column — the write will fail with a schema mismatch error. This is a safety feature: it prevents accidental schema changes from corrupting downstream consumers. When a legitimate schema change is needed, you must use the mergeSchema option deliberately. Module 6 covers this in detail.

A Lakehouse is only as useful as the data inside it. There are multiple ways to get data in, and choosing the right method depends on where the data comes from, how often it arrives, and whether it needs transformation before landing. This lesson covers the three primary loading methods — portal upload, pipeline Copy activity, and notebook writes — and maps each to its role in NordGrid's pipeline. By the end of this lesson, you will know which method to use for each data loading scenario you are likely to encounter.
Method 1 — Portal Upload

The simplest way to load data is through the portal. Open the Lakehouse explorer, navigate to the Files section, choose a folder (or create one), and click the Upload button. A file picker opens, you select a file from your local machine, and the file uploads to the specified location. This method is useful during development — uploading a sample CSV to test a transformation notebook — but it is not suitable for production. Portal uploads are manual, do not support scheduling, have no retry logic, and create no audit trail. NordGrid uses portal uploads exclusively during notebook development and testing.
Method 2 — Pipeline Copy Activity

The production method for loading data from external sources is a Data Factory pipeline with a Copy activity. The Copy activity connects to an external data source (SFTP server, Azure SQL database, REST API, cloud storage), reads data, and writes it to the Lakehouse's Files section. The pipeline can be scheduled to run automatically, includes retry logic for transient failures, and logs every execution in the monitoring hub. NordGrid's pl_ingest_meters pipeline runs daily at 02:00 UTC, copying the meter CSV from the SFTP server to lh_meter_readings/Files/meter_exports/. Module 7 covers pipelines in full detail.
Method 3 — Notebook Writes

The third method is writing data from a Spark notebook, which handles two scenarios. First, writing to the Tables section: a notebook reads raw files from the Files section (or from another Lakehouse), transforms the data, and writes the result as a managed Delta table using saveAsTable(). This is how NordGrid's Silver tables are created — the transformation notebook reads Bronze CSV files and writes clean Delta tables. Second, writing to the Files section: a notebook can write files using Spark's file system APIs, which NordGrid uses for intermediate checkpoint files during complex reprocessing jobs.

# Method 3a: Writing transformed data to the Tables section
# Read raw CSV from the Bronze Lakehouse's Files section
df_raw = spark.read.csv(
    "Files/meter_exports/2025/03/23/readings.csv",
    header=True,
    schema=meter_schema  # Explicit schema defined earlier in the notebook
)

# Apply transformations (filtering, casting, deduplication)
df_clean = (
    df_raw
    .filter(col("meter_id").isNotNull())
    .filter(col("daily_kw") > 0)
    .dropDuplicates(["meter_id", "date"])
)

# Write to the Tables section as a managed Delta table
df_clean.write.mode("overwrite").saveAsTable("silver_meters")

The code above shows the complete Bronze-to-Silver pattern in miniature: read from Files, transform, write to Tables. The explicit schema (meter_schema, defined earlier in the notebook) prevents type inference issues. The filters remove null meter IDs and negative daily_kw values. The deduplication ensures one row per meter per day. The write creates a managed Delta table that is immediately queryable through SQL. This pattern is the core of Module 6, which covers transformations in full detail.
Choosing the Right Method

rows: Testing a notebook with sample data
Fastest for small files during development
Requires PySpark logic for cleaning and validation
Reference table refresh from external DB
Low-code option for simple table-to-table copies

blocks.title: The pattern is always the same
blocks.content: Regardless of the loading method, the underlying pattern is consistent: data enters the Lakehouse (either Files or Tables), lives in OneLake, and becomes accessible to every Fabric engine. The loading method determines how the data gets in — manually, through a scheduled pipeline, or through a notebook transformation. The data's behavior after loading is identical regardless of how it arrived.

After data is loaded into a Lakehouse, the next step is exploration — verifying that the data looks correct, checking schemas, inspecting row counts, and confirming that the pipeline produced the expected output. Fabric provides several exploration tools, each suited to a different level of depth. The Lakehouse explorer gives a quick visual overview. The SQL analytics endpoint supports ad-hoc queries for deeper investigation. Spark notebooks provide the most powerful exploration with full DataFrame operations. This lesson covers each exploration method and the NordGrid team's daily verification workflow.
The Lakehouse Explorer — Quick Visual Check

The Lakehouse explorer in the portal provides the fastest way to check what data exists. Open the Lakehouse, expand the Tables section, and click a table name to see a preview of the first 100 rows. The preview shows column names, data types, and sample values — enough to confirm that a table was created, has the expected columns, and contains plausible data. For the Files section, expanding a folder shows the files with their names, sizes, and modification timestamps — enough to confirm that today's ingestion pipeline deposited the expected file.

The explorer also provides a Schema tab for each table. The Schema tab lists every column's name, data type, and nullability. NordGrid's engineers use this tab as a first check after running a transformation notebook: does the table have the correct number of columns, are the types right (daily_kw should be double, not string), and are the expected nullable/non-nullable constraints in place? This takes five seconds and catches the most common schema errors.
The SQL Endpoint — Ad-Hoc Queries

For deeper exploration, open the Lakehouse's SQL analytics endpoint. This opens a T-SQL editor where you can run queries against the Lakehouse's Delta tables. The SQL endpoint is read-only — you cannot INSERT, UPDATE, or DELETE — but you can run any SELECT query, create views, and use all standard T-SQL functions. NordGrid's engineers use the SQL endpoint for daily verification queries: checking row counts, confirming date ranges, and computing summary statistics.

-- Check row count for today's data
SELECT COUNT(*) AS row_count
FROM silver_meters
WHERE date = '2025-03-23';

-- Check for unexpected null values
SELECT
    SUM(CASE WHEN meter_id IS NULL THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS null_meter_ids,
    SUM(CASE WHEN daily_kw IS NULL THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS null_daily_kw,
    SUM(CASE WHEN region IS NULL THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS null_regions
FROM silver_meters
WHERE date = '2025-03-23';

-- Summary statistics per region
SELECT
    region,
    COUNT(*) AS meters,
    AVG(daily_kw) AS avg_kw,
    MAX(daily_kw) AS max_kw
FROM silver_meters
WHERE date = '2025-03-23'
GROUP BY region;

These three queries form the core of NordGrid's daily verification. The first confirms that approximately 500,000 rows arrived (a number significantly lower than expected would indicate an ingestion problem). The second confirms that the Silver transformation correctly filtered out nulls. The third confirms that all four regions are present and that the average and maximum values are within expected ranges. A max_kw of 1,000,000 would indicate a data quality issue that the Silver filter should have caught.
Notebook Exploration — Full Power

For the most thorough exploration, use a Spark notebook. Notebooks provide the full DataFrame API: show() for previewing rows, printSchema() for inspecting the schema in detail, describe() for summary statistics, and custom analysis using filter, groupBy, and any other PySpark operation. NordGrid's engineers use notebooks for initial data exploration when onboarding a new data source — inspecting the full range of values in each column, checking for unexpected patterns, and profiling the data before designing the Silver transformation.

# Read the Silver table
df = spark.read.table("silver_meters")

# Show the schema — column names, types, nullability
df.printSchema()

# Preview the first 10 rows
df.show(10, truncate=False)

# Summary statistics for numeric columns
df.describe("daily_kw").show()

# Count rows per region for today
df.filter(col("date") == "2025-03-23") \
  .groupBy("region") \
  .count() \
  .show()

blocks.title: Match the tool to the question
blocks.content: Use the Lakehouse explorer for 'does the data exist?' questions (five seconds). Use the SQL endpoint for 'are the numbers right?' questions (thirty seconds). Use a notebook for 'what does the data look like in detail?' questions (five minutes). NordGrid's morning verification uses the monitoring hub first (did the pipeline succeed?), the SQL endpoint second (are the counts correct?), and a notebook only when something looks wrong.

blocks.title: Three exploration tools at increasing depth
blocks.content:
Speed ◀──────────────────────────────────────▶ Depth

Lakehouse Explorer     SQL Endpoint           Notebook
┌────────────────┐  ┌──────────────────┐  ┌────────────────────┐
│ Does data exist?│  │ Are counts right? │  │ What does the data  │
│ Right columns?  │  │ Any nulls?        │  │ look like in detail?│
│ Recent modified?│  │ Stats per region? │  │ Full profiling?     │
│                 │  │                   │  │ Custom analysis?    │
│ Time: 5 seconds │  │ Time: 30 seconds  │  │ Time: 5 minutes     │
└────────────────┘  └──────────────────┘  └────────────────────┘
blocks.caption: Each tool provides a different level of depth. Use the fastest tool that answers your question. The explorer confirms existence, the SQL endpoint confirms correctness, and the notebook provides full investigative power.

blocks.content: Every Lakehouse comes with an auto-generated SQL analytics endpoint — a read-only T-SQL interface that lets you query Delta tables without running Spark. This is one of Fabric's most practical features for data engineers because it bridges two worlds: data engineers write data using PySpark in notebooks, and analysts query it using T-SQL in the SQL editor. No additional configuration is needed — the moment you write a Delta table with saveAsTable(), it becomes queryable through T-SQL. This lesson explains what the SQL endpoint can do, what it cannot, and how NordGrid uses it.
What the Endpoint Supports

The SQL analytics endpoint supports a broad subset of T-SQL. You can run SELECT queries with all standard clauses (WHERE, GROUP BY, ORDER BY, HAVING, LIMIT), join multiple tables, use aggregate functions (SUM, AVG, COUNT, MIN, MAX), use window functions (ROW_NUMBER, RANK, LAG, LEAD), create views, and create table-valued functions. For NordGrid's analysts, this means they can write the same SQL they would write against any other SQL database — no new syntax, no new concepts, no need to learn PySpark.
What the Endpoint Does Not Support

The SQL analytics endpoint is read-only. It does not support INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE, CREATE TABLE, or stored procedures. You cannot write data through the endpoint — only read it. This limitation is by design: the endpoint exists so that consumers can query Lakehouse data without risking modifications. If you need read-write SQL access, you need a Fabric Warehouse (covered in Module 9). NordGrid's analysts use the SQL endpoint for all their Silver queries because they only need to read data. The data engineering team uses the Warehouse for Gold-layer writes that require T-SQL.
Automatic Table Detection

The SQL endpoint automatically detects Delta tables in the Lakehouse's Tables section. When a notebook writes a new table with saveAsTable('silver_meters'), the SQL endpoint registers the table within seconds. You do not need to create a schema, define columns, or run any setup command. The endpoint reads the Delta table's schema from the _delta_log and exposes it as a T-SQL table. If the notebook adds a new column to the Delta table (using mergeSchema), the SQL endpoint reflects the change automatically.
Creating Views

Although the SQL endpoint cannot create tables, it can create views. A view is a saved query that appears as a table to anyone who queries it. NordGrid's analysts create views on the Silver tables to provide simplified, pre-filtered perspectives. For example, the view v_north_region_meters selects only NORTH region meters, and the view v_monthly_summary computes monthly aggregates. These views make common queries reusable and hide the complexity of the underlying table structure from business users who query the endpoint directly.

-- Create a view that filters to NORTH region only
CREATE VIEW v_north_region_meters AS
SELECT meter_id, daily_kw, date, customer_type
FROM silver_meters
WHERE region = 'NORTH';

-- Create a view that computes monthly summaries
CREATE VIEW v_monthly_summary AS
SELECT
    region,
    YEAR(date) AS year,
    MONTH(date) AS month,
    COUNT(*) AS reading_count,
    SUM(daily_kw) AS total_kw,
    AVG(daily_kw) AS avg_kw
FROM silver_meters
GROUP BY region, YEAR(date), MONTH(date);

These views are stored in the SQL endpoint's metadata — not as data files in OneLake. They do not consume additional storage and always reflect the current state of the underlying Silver table. When the notebook writes new data to silver_meters tonight, both views will return updated results tomorrow morning without any action from the analyst. NordGrid's analysts have created a library of eight views on the Silver tables, covering the most common query patterns. New analysts start by querying views rather than writing SQL against the base tables, which reduces errors and enforces consistent filtering logic.

blocks.title: The bridge between engineering and analysis
blocks.content: The SQL analytics endpoint is the handoff point between data engineering and data analysis. The data engineer writes clean, validated Delta tables. The analyst queries those tables using familiar T-SQL. No data is copied. No connector is configured. No ETL pipeline is needed to make the data 'SQL-ready.' The endpoint turns every Lakehouse into a read-only SQL database automatically — and this automatic bridge is one of the strongest arguments for using Lakehouses as the primary data store.

The third item that Fabric generates when you create a Lakehouse is the default semantic model — a Power BI data layer that makes Lakehouse tables available for reporting. If the SQL analytics endpoint bridges data engineering and SQL analysis, the semantic model bridges data engineering and business intelligence. When an analyst creates a Power BI report and connects to the semantic model, they see the Lakehouse's Delta tables as data sources they can drag into charts, tables, and dashboards. This lesson explains what a semantic model is, how the default model works, and what it means for a data engineer.
What a Semantic Model Is

A semantic model is a business-friendly representation of data. It sits between the raw tables and the Power BI report, providing names that make sense to business users (Total Energy Consumption instead of SUM(daily_kw)), relationships between tables (meters linked to substations linked to regions), and pre-defined measures (Year-over-Year Growth, Average Daily Consumption). Semantic models exist because business users should not need to know table names, column types, or SQL syntax to get answers from data. The model translates the technical schema into a business vocabulary.

In the broader Power BI ecosystem, semantic models are carefully designed artifacts — teams spend days defining measures, relationships, and hierarchies. In Fabric, the default semantic model is generated automatically and is intentionally minimal: it includes all tables from the Lakehouse's Tables section with their original column names, but no custom measures, no relationships, and no business-friendly naming. The default model is a starting point, not a finished product.
Direct Lake Mode

The default semantic model uses a connection mode called Direct Lake. In traditional Power BI, data is either imported into the model's memory (Import mode — fast queries but stale data) or queried live from the source database (DirectQuery mode — current data but slower queries). Direct Lake is a third option unique to Fabric: the semantic model reads directly from OneLake's Delta tables without importing them into memory and without a live query to a SQL database. This gives NordGrid's reports both current data (updated as soon as the notebook writes new data) and fast queries (because Delta's Parquet files are optimized for analytical reads).

left.label: Import Mode (traditional)
points: Data copied into the semantic model's memory
Requires scheduled refresh to update data
Fast queries (data is in memory)
Consumes memory capacity for the copied data

right.label: Direct Lake (Fabric)
points: Data read directly from OneLake Delta tables
Data is current — reflects latest Delta table version
Fast queries (reads optimized Parquet from OneLake)
No data copy — reads from existing OneLake storage

blocks.title: Direct Lake reads from OneLake without copying
blocks.content:
Traditional Import Mode:         Direct Lake Mode (Fabric):

  OneLake          Semantic        OneLake
  ┌──────┐  copy   Model           ┌──────┐
  │Delta │──────▶ ┌──────┐         │Delta │
  │Table │        │Memory│         │Table │
  └──────┘        │Copy  │         └───┬──┘
                  └──┬───┘             │
                     │            reads directly
                     ▼                 │
              ┌──────────┐             ▼
              │ Power BI  │     ┌──────────┐
              │ Report    │     │ Power BI  │
              └──────────┘     │ Report    │
                               └──────────┘
  Stale until refresh.    Always current.
blocks.caption: Import mode copies data into memory, creating a separate copy that becomes stale between refreshes. Direct Lake reads directly from OneLake's Delta tables — no copy, no staleness, no extra memory consumption.

blocks.content: What the Data Engineer Needs to Know

As a data engineer, you do not build semantic models — that is typically the BI analyst's job. But you need to understand three things. First, the tables you write to the Lakehouse automatically appear in the default semantic model. If you add a table that should not be visible to report builders (a temporary staging table, for example), it will appear unless you explicitly exclude it. Second, your table schema becomes the semantic model's schema. If your column is named daily_kw, the report builder sees daily_kw. Clear, descriptive column names in your notebook make the analyst's job easier. Third, your partitioning and data quality directly affect report performance. A poorly partitioned table or a table with millions of null values will produce slow reports.

NordGrid's data engineers and analysts have an agreement: the data engineering team is responsible for clean, well-structured Delta tables with clear column names. The analyst team is responsible for building the custom semantic model on top of those tables — adding measures, relationships, and business-friendly naming. The default semantic model serves as a quick-start that analysts can use immediately, and the custom model evolves over time as reporting requirements grow.

blocks.title: Staging tables leak into the semantic model
blocks.content: If your notebook creates a temporary staging table (like staging_meters_raw) in the Lakehouse's Tables section, it automatically appears in the default semantic model. Analysts browsing the model may see it and assume it is a production table. NordGrid's convention is to never create staging tables in the production Lakehouse — staging goes in a separate development Lakehouse or uses temporary views instead. This keeps the production semantic model clean and trustworthy.

The previous eight lessons covered what Lakehouses are, how to create them, how to load and explore data, and how the SQL endpoint and semantic model extend their reach. This lesson distills NordGrid's operational experience into a set of practical conventions — the patterns that prevent confusion, reduce errors, and make the Lakehouse environment manageable as data and team size grow. These are not theoretical principles — they are the result of NordGrid's data engineering team learning what works after six months of daily Fabric operations.
One Lakehouse per Layer per Domain

NordGrid's most important Lakehouse decision was the granularity rule: one Lakehouse per medallion layer per data domain. The meter readings domain has a Bronze Lakehouse and a Silver Lakehouse. The SCADA telemetry domain has its own Bronze and Silver Lakehouses. This separation prevents the 'everything in one Lakehouse' anti-pattern where Bronze files, Silver tables, and Gold aggregates all coexist in a single item — making it impossible to set different access controls per layer and creating a navigation nightmare in the explorer.
Naming Conventions

rows: Identifies the data domain. The workspace already identifies the layer.
Enables time-based wildcard reads and prevents naming collisions
Identifies the medallion layer and data content
Aligns with common query patterns for time-bounded analysis
Consistent across all courses and all Lakehouses

blocks.content: Files Section Hygiene

The Files section grows indefinitely unless you manage it. NordGrid's 1.5 GB daily meter file accumulates to 550 GB per year. The platform team sets a retention policy: Bronze files older than 90 days are archived or deleted, because the corresponding Silver Delta tables contain the processed and validated version. Without this policy, NordGrid's Bronze Lakehouse would consume 1.6 terabytes after three years — storage that serves no operational purpose since no pipeline reads files older than the last reprocessing window.
Table Maintenance

Delta tables require periodic maintenance. Over time, as data is appended and overwritten, the table accumulates small files (many tiny Parquet files instead of a few large ones) and obsolete files (files from old versions that VACUUM has not yet cleaned up). Fabric provides maintenance commands — OPTIMIZE to compact small files into larger ones, and VACUUM to remove obsolete files. NordGrid runs OPTIMIZE on Silver tables weekly and VACUUM with a 14-day retention period. These commands improve query performance and reduce storage consumption. Module 6 covers maintenance in more detail.
The Checklist

items: Name the Lakehouse for its data domain with the lh_ prefix. Verify the name before creating, because renaming breaks references from notebooks and pipelines.
Use separate Lakehouses for Bronze and Silver. Never mix raw files and clean tables in the same Lakehouse — access control and navigation suffer.
Organize the Files section with date-partitioned folders. Use the source/year/month/day/ convention to enable wildcard reads and prevent file collisions.
Partition Silver tables by year and month. This aligns with most analytical query patterns and enables efficient partition pruning.
Set a retention policy for Bronze files. Archive or delete files older than your reprocessing window to prevent storage bloat.
Run OPTIMIZE weekly on Silver and Gold tables. Compact small files to maintain query performance.
Never create staging tables in the production Lakehouse. Use a development Lakehouse or temporary views instead, to keep the semantic model clean.

blocks.title: Conventions are cheap, cleanup is expensive
blocks.content: Every convention in this lesson costs seconds to follow and saves hours to fix later. Naming a Lakehouse correctly takes 10 seconds. Renaming one after 15 notebooks reference it takes an afternoon. Setting a retention policy during setup takes 5 minutes. Cleaning up 2 terabytes of stale Bronze files takes a day of careful work to ensure nothing important is deleted. Build the conventions into your workflow from the beginning.

The previous nine lessons covered the Lakehouse from every angle: what it is, how to create it, how its Files and Tables sections work, how to load and explore data, how the SQL analytics endpoint and semantic model extend its reach, and the best practices that keep it manageable. This lesson brings everything together by showing how the Lakehouse connects to every other Fabric item in NordGrid's architecture. The Lakehouse is not just a data store — it is the hub that connects pipelines, notebooks, SQL queries, and Power BI reports into a functioning data platform.
The Lakehouse at the Center

blocks.title: The Lakehouse as the hub of NordGrid's Fabric architecture
blocks.caption: Every major Fabric item connects to the Lakehouse. Pipelines write to Files. Notebooks read Files and write Tables. Dataflows write Tables. The SQL endpoint and semantic model read Tables. The Lakehouse is the convergence point where all data engineering and data consumption meet.

blocks.content: The Three Artifacts in Action

NordGrid's Silver Lakehouse (lh_meter_readings in ws_nordgrid_silver) demonstrates how the three auto-generated artifacts work together in practice. The Lakehouse itself holds the silver_meters Delta table, written nightly by the transformation notebook. The SQL analytics endpoint makes silver_meters queryable via T-SQL — NordGrid's analysts run verification queries every morning, and the Gold Warehouse uses cross-database queries to read from it. The default semantic model makes silver_meters available to Power BI — the operations dashboard's trend chart reads from this model using Direct Lake, showing data that was updated just hours ago.

All three artifacts read from the same Delta table in OneLake. When the notebook writes new data to silver_meters at 02:15 UTC, the SQL endpoint returns the updated data immediately. The semantic model reflects the change the next time a report queries it. The analyst, the SQL query, and the Power BI report all see the same data because they all read from the same source. This is the practical realization of Module 1's unified platform promise: one copy, many consumers, zero staleness.
What You Can Build Now

With the Lakehouse foundation in place, you can now understand the full scope of what remains in this course. Module 5 teaches you how to write the notebooks that transform data inside Lakehouses. Module 6 teaches the specific transformation patterns — schema enforcement, filtering, deduplication, and Delta writes. Module 7 teaches pipelines that automate everything on a schedule. Module 8 teaches Dataflows Gen2 for low-code transformations. Module 9 dives deeper into the SQL endpoint and Warehouse. Module 10 ties everything into the complete medallion architecture. Every one of these modules operates on Lakehouses — creating them, reading from them, writing to them, or querying them.
The Complete Lakehouse Mental Model

A Lakehouse is a Fabric item that stores files and Delta tables in OneLake. It has two sections: Files (unmanaged, any format) and Tables (managed Delta, schema-enforced, ACID). Creating a Lakehouse auto-generates a SQL analytics endpoint (read-only T-SQL) and a default semantic model (Power BI Direct Lake). Data enters through portal uploads, pipeline Copy activities, notebook writes, or dataflow outputs. Data is explored through the portal explorer, the SQL endpoint, or notebook code. The Lakehouse is the central item in Fabric data engineering — the hub that connects every pipeline, notebook, query, and report.

blocks.title: What comes next
blocks.content: Module 5 opens the notebook editor. You will create your first Fabric notebook, attach it to a Lakehouse, write PySpark code that reads data, and see Spark compute in action. The Lakehouse is your data store. The notebook is your code editor. Together, they form the core of Fabric data engineering.

chapters.title: Notebooks — Writing Code in Fabric
chapters.description: Learn to use Fabric notebooks for data engineering, understand how Spark compute works in Fabric, and write PySpark code that reads from and writes to Lakehouses.

blocks.content: Modules 1 through 4 built the platform foundation: why Fabric exists, how workspaces organize items, how OneLake stores data, and how Lakehouses provide the Files and Tables sections that hold NordGrid's pipeline data. Starting with this module, you write code. The Fabric notebook is an interactive code editor that runs on Apache Spark — the same distributed processing engine that the PySpark Junior-Level Track covers in depth. If you completed that track, you already know PySpark syntax. What this module teaches is how PySpark works inside Fabric specifically: how notebooks connect to Lakehouses, how Spark sessions are managed, how to read and write Lakehouse data, and how notebooks fit into the broader pipeline.
The Notebook Concept

A notebook is a document that interleaves code cells with markdown cells. You write code in a cell, run it, and see the output immediately below the cell. Then you write the next cell, building your transformation step by step. This interactive style — write, run, inspect, iterate — is fundamentally different from writing a complete script and running it all at once. Notebooks let you see intermediate results at every step, which makes them ideal for data exploration and transformation development. NordGrid's data engineers develop all their transformation logic in notebooks before scheduling the notebooks to run as automated pipeline activities.
Fabric Notebooks vs Jupyter Notebooks

If you have used Jupyter notebooks before, Fabric notebooks will feel familiar — the cell-based structure, the code-output pattern, and the markdown formatting are similar. But Fabric notebooks have several important differences. First, Spark integration: Fabric notebooks provide a pre-configured Spark session with access to OneLake and Lakehouse data — no setup code needed. Second, Lakehouse attachment: you can attach a Lakehouse to the notebook, which enables shorthand paths for reading and writing data. Third, collaboration: Fabric notebooks support real-time co-authoring, where multiple engineers can edit the same notebook simultaneously. Fourth, scheduling: Fabric notebooks can be run as pipeline activities on a schedule, turning interactive development code into automated production jobs.

left.label: Jupyter Notebook
points: Requires installing PySpark and configuring a Spark session manually
Reads files from the local file system or configured cloud storage
Single-user editing — no real-time collaboration
Scheduling requires external tools (cron, Airflow, etc.)
Runs on whatever compute you configure (local, cluster, cloud VM)

right.label: Fabric Notebook
points: Spark session pre-configured and ready — the spark variable exists immediately
Reads directly from attached Lakehouse tables and files in OneLake
Real-time co-authoring — multiple engineers edit simultaneously
Built-in scheduling through Data Factory pipeline integration
Runs on Fabric's managed Spark compute — no cluster management

blocks.content: The Notebook in NordGrid's Pipeline

NordGrid's pipeline uses notebooks at two points. First, the Silver transformation notebook (nb_transform_meters) reads raw CSV files from the Bronze Lakehouse, applies schema enforcement, filters invalid records, deduplicates, and writes clean Delta tables to the Silver Lakehouse. Second, the Gold aggregation notebook (nb_aggregate_gold) reads from Silver tables, computes regional summaries, and writes aggregated tables to the Gold Lakehouse. Both notebooks are developed interactively — cell by cell — and then scheduled to run automatically as part of a Data Factory pipeline. This module covers the notebook itself. Module 6 covers the transformation patterns inside it. Module 7 covers the pipeline that schedules it.

blocks.title: The notebook is where data engineering happens
blocks.content: Lakehouses store the data. Pipelines schedule the work. The SQL endpoint serves queries. Power BI displays reports. But the notebook is where the actual transformation logic lives — the code that converts messy Bronze files into clean Silver tables. If the Lakehouse is the filing cabinet, the notebook is the desk where the work gets done. Mastering notebooks is mastering the core skill of Fabric data engineering.

Creating a notebook follows the same pattern as creating any Fabric item: open the workspace, click New, select the item type, and give it a name. But notebooks have an additional configuration step that other items do not — attaching a Lakehouse. The attached Lakehouse determines which tables and files the notebook can access using shorthand paths, and it is the most common source of confusion for new Fabric users. This lesson walks through the creation process, explains Lakehouse attachment, and establishes the workflow NordGrid's engineers follow when starting a new notebook.
Creating a Notebook

Navigate to the workspace where you want the notebook (for NordGrid, this is typically ws_nordgrid_silver for transformation notebooks). Click New and select Notebook. Fabric creates the notebook with a default name like 'Notebook 1' and opens it immediately in the editor. The first thing you should do — before writing any code — is rename the notebook. Click the name at the top of the editor and change it to something descriptive. NordGrid's convention is nb_ followed by the purpose: nb_transform_meters, nb_aggregate_gold, nb_explore_scada. Never keep the default name.
Attaching a Lakehouse

On the left side of the notebook editor, there is a Lakehouse explorer pane. When you first create a notebook, this pane is empty — no Lakehouse is attached. To attach one, click the Add button in the Lakehouse pane and select an existing Lakehouse from the current workspace or another workspace you have access to. Once attached, the Lakehouse's Files and Tables sections appear in the pane, and you can reference its data using shorthand paths in your code. A notebook can have one default Lakehouse and additional Lakehouses pinned for reference.

The default Lakehouse determines which tables spark.read.table() accesses without a full path. If you attach the Silver Lakehouse as the default, then spark.read.table('silver_meters') reads from the Silver Lakehouse's silver_meters table. If you need to read from a different Lakehouse (for example, the Bronze Lakehouse's files), you either attach it as an additional Lakehouse or use the full abfss:// path. NordGrid's transformation notebooks attach the Silver Lakehouse as the default (because that is where they write output) and reference the Bronze Lakehouse by full path (because that is where they read input).

blocks.title: The notebook editor layout with attached Lakehouse
blocks.content:
┌────────────────┬───────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                │  nb_transform_meters          ▶ Run All   │
│  LAKEHOUSE     │                                            │
│  EXPLORER      │  Cell 1 [PySpark]  ─────────────────────  │
│                │  │ df = spark.read.csv(                │  │
│  ▼ lh_meter_   │  │   "Files/meter_exports/...",        │  │
│    readings    │  │   header=True, schema=meter_schema)  │  │
│    ▼ Files/    │  │                                      │  │
│      └─ meter_ │  ├─ Output ─────────────────────────────┤  │
│         exports│  │  (DataFrame preview)                 │  │
│    ▼ Tables/   │                                            │
│      └─ silver_│  Cell 2 [PySpark]  ─────────────────────  │
│         meters │  │ df_clean = df.filter(...)             │  │
│                │  │                                      │  │
└────────────────┴───────────────────────────────────────────┘
blocks.caption: The notebook editor has the Lakehouse explorer on the left and code cells on the right. The attached Lakehouse's Files and Tables are browsable in the explorer, and shorthand paths in code reference this Lakehouse's data.

blocks.content: The Notebook File

A Fabric notebook is stored as an item in the workspace, not as a file on your local machine. When you save the notebook (Ctrl+S or the Save button), Fabric stores it in the workspace's metadata. If Git integration is enabled, the notebook is also serialized to your Git repository as a set of files. NordGrid's production notebooks are always saved to Git — the Git repository serves as the backup, the version history, and the code review platform. Without Git, a deleted notebook is gone permanently, with no way to recover previous versions.

blocks.title: Attach the Lakehouse before writing code
blocks.content: The most common beginner mistake is writing code that references Lakehouse tables before attaching a Lakehouse. The code will fail with a 'table not found' error that looks like a data issue but is actually a configuration issue. NordGrid's onboarding checklist for new notebooks has three steps: (1) rename the notebook, (2) attach the Lakehouse, (3) then start writing code. Following this order prevents the most frequent source of confusion.

If you have used PySpark outside of Fabric — on Databricks, on a standalone cluster, or in local mode — you know that every PySpark script begins by creating a SparkSession. You write SparkSession.builder.appName('my-app').getOrCreate(), wait for the session to start, and then proceed with your code. In Fabric, this step is unnecessary. Fabric provides a pre-configured Spark session in every notebook, accessible through a variable called spark. The session is created automatically when you run your first code cell. This lesson explains how Fabric's managed Spark session works, what it handles for you, and the one thing you need to be aware of: startup time.
The spark Variable

The moment you open a Fabric notebook, a variable called spark is available in every PySpark cell. This variable is a fully configured SparkSession that can read from OneLake, write to Lakehouses, run SQL queries, and access the Spark catalog. You do not need to import SparkSession, call builder(), configure any properties, or call getOrCreate(). Fabric handles all of that behind the scenes. Your first line of code can immediately use the session.

# In Fabric, the spark variable is ready immediately
# No SparkSession.builder needed — Fabric configures it for you

# Read a table from the attached Lakehouse
df = spark.read.table("silver_meters")

# Run a SQL query
result = spark.sql("SELECT COUNT(*) FROM silver_meters WHERE region = 'NORTH'")
result.show()

Compare this to a standalone PySpark script where you would first create the session, then configure the connection to your storage, then authenticate, and then read the data. In Fabric, all of that is a single line: spark.read.table('silver_meters'). The session knows where OneLake is, how to authenticate (using your Fabric identity), and which Lakehouse is attached to the notebook. This simplicity is one of the practical advantages of the SaaS model — the infrastructure is pre-configured so you can focus on the data.
Session Startup Time

The one downside of Fabric's managed Spark session is startup time. When you run the first code cell in a notebook, Fabric allocates Spark compute resources from your capacity pool, starts the Spark driver and executors, configures the environment, and initializes the session. This cold start takes 30 to 60 seconds, sometimes longer depending on the capacity's current load. Subsequent cells run immediately because the session is already active. NordGrid's engineers learn to expect the startup delay and use the first cell to import libraries and define schemas — tasks that do not need data access and complete as soon as the session starts.

If the session is idle for too long (the default timeout is typically 20 minutes, configurable through the workspace's Spark settings), Fabric terminates the session to free capacity for other users. The next cell execution triggers another cold start. During active development, NordGrid's engineers run a cell every few minutes to keep the session alive. During scheduled pipeline runs, the startup time is part of the pipeline's execution time — NordGrid's transformation notebook takes 30 seconds to start and 12 minutes to execute, for a total runtime of about 13 minutes.
What the Session Configures

rows: Set by workspace defaults or environment
Yes — via environment or workspace settings
Set by workspace pool size defaults
Automatic — session authenticates with your Fabric identity
Set by the workspace default environment

blocks.title: Do not create a SparkSession in Fabric
blocks.content: If you copy PySpark code from a tutorial or from your standalone Spark environment, it may begin with SparkSession.builder.appName('...').getOrCreate(). In a Fabric notebook, this line is unnecessary and can cause confusion — it creates a second session that may not have the correct OneLake configuration. Delete the SparkSession creation code and use the pre-existing spark variable directly. If you see 'table not found' errors in code that works outside Fabric, this is often the cause.

blocks.title: Spark session lifecycle in a Fabric notebook
blocks.content:
Run first cell
      │
      ▼
┌──────────────────┐
│  COLD START       │
│  30-60 seconds    │
│  Allocate compute │
│  Start Spark      │
│  Load environment │
└────────┬─────────┘
         │
         ▼
┌──────────────────┐     idle > 20 min     ┌───────────────┐
│  SESSION ACTIVE   │ ────────────────────▶ │  SESSION ENDED │
│  Cells run        │                       │  Next cell     │
│  instantly        │ ◀──── run a cell ──── │  triggers new  │
│                   │                       │  cold start    │
└──────────────────┘                       └───────────────┘
blocks.caption: The first cell triggers a cold start (30-60 seconds). Subsequent cells run instantly while the session is active. After 20 minutes of inactivity, the session ends and the next cell triggers another cold start.

blocks.content: Fabric notebooks support four programming languages: PySpark (Python with Spark), Spark SQL, Scala, and R. Each cell in the notebook can use a different language, which means you can mix PySpark transformations with SQL queries in the same notebook. The default language is PySpark — when you create a new cell, it uses PySpark unless you change it. NordGrid's data engineering team standardizes on PySpark for transformation logic and uses Spark SQL for ad-hoc queries and validation checks. This lesson explains each language, how to switch between them, and when each is the right choice.
PySpark — The Default

PySpark is Python with access to the Spark DataFrame API. It is the default language for Fabric notebook cells and the language NordGrid uses for all transformation logic: reading files, filtering rows, casting types, joining tables, and writing Delta tables. PySpark is the right choice when the transformation requires programming constructs — loops, conditionals, functions, error handling — or when the operation is naturally expressed as a chain of DataFrame method calls. If you completed the PySpark Junior-Level Track, you already know the syntax. In Fabric, the only difference is that the spark session is pre-configured.
Spark SQL — The Query Language

Spark SQL lets you write SQL queries directly in a notebook cell by prefixing the cell with %%sql. The queries run against the attached Lakehouse's tables and return results as a formatted table below the cell. Spark SQL is the right choice for ad-hoc queries, data validation, and quick aggregations — any operation that is naturally expressed as a SELECT statement. NordGrid's engineers use %%sql cells at the end of transformation notebooks to verify the output: checking row counts, computing summary statistics, and confirming that all four regions are present.

# PySpark cell — transformation logic
df = spark.read.table("silver_meters")
df_north = df.filter(col("region") == "NORTH")
print(f"NORTH region count: {df_north.count()}")

-- Spark SQL cell (prefix with %%sql in Fabric)
-- Quick validation: row count per region for today
SELECT region, COUNT(*) AS meter_count
FROM silver_meters
WHERE date = '2025-03-23'
GROUP BY region
ORDER BY region

Both cells above access the same silver_meters table from the attached Lakehouse. The PySpark cell uses the DataFrame API with method chaining. The SQL cell uses a SELECT statement with GROUP BY. Both produce correct results — the choice is about readability and convenience. For the simple aggregation above, most engineers find the SQL version more readable. For a complex transformation with multiple steps, chained conditionals, and custom functions, PySpark is clearer.
Scala and R

Fabric notebooks also support Scala (%%scala) and R (%%sparkr). Scala provides direct access to the JVM-based Spark API without the Python-to-JVM translation overhead, which can matter for performance-critical operations. R provides the SparkR API for data scientists who prefer R syntax. NordGrid does not use either language in production — the team standardized on PySpark because all team members know Python, and the performance difference between PySpark and Scala is negligible for most data engineering workloads. This course does not cover Scala or R, but knowing they exist helps you understand code you might encounter in other teams' notebooks.
Mixing Languages

A single notebook can contain cells in different languages. NordGrid's transformation notebooks follow a pattern: PySpark cells for the transformation logic, then a %%sql cell at the end for verification. Variables defined in PySpark cells are available in subsequent PySpark cells but not in SQL cells — the languages share the Spark session but not the Python namespace. To pass data between languages, write it to a temporary view using createOrReplaceTempView(), which makes it accessible from SQL cells.

# Register a DataFrame as a temporary view so SQL cells can query it
df_clean = spark.read.table("silver_meters").filter(col("daily_kw") > 0)
df_clean.createOrReplaceTempView("clean_meters")

# Now a %%sql cell can query clean_meters:
# SELECT region, AVG(daily_kw) FROM clean_meters GROUP BY region

blocks.title: Pick one primary language
blocks.content: Mixing languages in a notebook is technically possible but can make the code harder to follow. NordGrid's convention is simple: use PySpark for all transformation logic, use %%sql only for read-only verification queries at the end of the notebook. This keeps the primary logic in one language and uses SQL only where SQL is genuinely clearer — counting rows, computing averages, and checking group membership.

The most common operation in a Fabric notebook is reading data from a Lakehouse. NordGrid's transformation notebook reads raw CSV files from the Bronze Lakehouse and clean Delta tables from the Silver Lakehouse. The aggregation notebook reads Silver tables to compute Gold summaries. Every notebook begins with a read operation — and getting the read right (correct path, correct schema, correct format) determines whether the rest of the notebook works. This lesson covers the two primary read patterns: reading tables from the Tables section and reading files from the Files section.
Reading Tables

When the data you need is a managed Delta table in the attached Lakehouse's Tables section, reading is one line of code. The spark.read.table() method takes the table name and returns a DataFrame. Fabric resolves the table name against the attached Lakehouse's catalog and reads the underlying Delta files from OneLake. The schema, partitioning, and data types are all defined by the Delta table — you do not need to specify them.

# Read a managed Delta table from the attached Lakehouse
df = spark.read.table("silver_meters")

# The DataFrame inherits the table's schema — no need to specify columns or types
df.printSchema()
# root
#  |-- meter_id: string (nullable = true)
#  |-- daily_kw: double (nullable = true)
#  |-- date: date (nullable = true)
#  |-- region: string (nullable = true)
#  |-- customer_type: string (nullable = true)
#  |-- year: integer (nullable = true)
#  |-- month: integer (nullable = true)

The output block above shows the schema that printSchema() displays for NordGrid's Silver meter table. Every column has a defined type — meter_id is a string, daily_kw is a double, date is a date. These types were set when the Silver transformation notebook wrote the table using an explicit schema. Reading the table inherits these types automatically, which means downstream code can rely on daily_kw being numeric without additional casting.
Reading Files

When the data is in the Files section — raw CSVs in Bronze, for example — you use the Spark reader API with a file path. The path starts with Files/ (a shorthand for the attached Lakehouse's Files section) and includes the folder structure and filename. Unlike table reads, file reads may require you to specify options like header handling, delimiter, and schema — because raw files do not carry metadata the way Delta tables do.

from pyspark.sql.types import StructType, StructField, StringType, DoubleType, DateType

# Define the expected schema explicitly
# Never rely on inferSchema for production reads — it is slow and unreliable
meter_schema = StructType([
    StructField("meter_id", StringType(), nullable=False),
    StructField("daily_kw", DoubleType(), nullable=True),
    StructField("date", DateType(), nullable=False),
    StructField("region", StringType(), nullable=True),
    StructField("customer_type", StringType(), nullable=True),
])

# Read a CSV file from the attached Lakehouse's Files section
df_raw = spark.read.csv(
    "Files/meter_exports/2025/03/23/readings.csv",
    header=True,
    schema=meter_schema  # Explicit schema — types are guaranteed
)

df_raw.show(5)

The explicit schema definition (meter_schema) is critical. Without it, Spark would infer types by sampling the file — a process that is slow on large files and unreliable when a column contains mostly numbers but occasional strings. NordGrid's convention is to define the schema as a StructType variable at the top of every transformation notebook. This schema serves as documentation (what columns do we expect?), validation (does the file match?), and type guarantee (daily_kw is always DoubleType, not StringType).
Reading from a Different Lakehouse

When you need to read from a Lakehouse that is not the attached default — for example, NordGrid's Silver notebook reading from the Bronze Lakehouse — you have two options. The first is to pin the other Lakehouse in the notebook's Lakehouse explorer, which makes its tables and files browsable but does not change the default. The second is to use the full abfss:// path, which works regardless of which Lakehouse is attached. NordGrid's transformation notebooks use the full path for Bronze reads and shorthand for Silver writes.

# Reading from a different Lakehouse using the full OneLake path
df_bronze = spark.read.csv(
    "abfss://ws_nordgrid_bronze@onelake.dfs.fabric.microsoft.com/"
    "lh_meter_readings.Lakehouse/Files/meter_exports/2025/03/23/readings.csv",
    header=True,
    schema=meter_schema
)

# Reading from the attached Lakehouse using shorthand — much simpler
df_silver = spark.read.table("silver_meters")

blocks.title: inferSchema is a development shortcut, not a production pattern
blocks.content: Setting inferSchema=True tells Spark to scan the file and guess column types. This works for quick exploration but fails in production for two reasons: it is slow (Spark must read the file twice — once to infer, once to load) and it is unreliable (a column with values 1, 2, 3, 'N/A' might be inferred as string instead of integer). NordGrid's coding standard prohibits inferSchema in any notebook that runs in the production pipeline. Always define an explicit StructType schema.

Reading data gets it into your notebook. Writing data puts it back into the Lakehouse where other tools — the SQL endpoint, Power BI, other notebooks — can access it. Writing is the operation that turns your notebook's transformation logic into a permanent, queryable asset. NordGrid's transformation notebook reads Bronze files, applies transformations, and writes Silver Delta tables. This lesson covers the write operations: saveAsTable for managed tables, write to files, write modes, and partitioned writes.
Writing Managed Tables

The primary write operation for data engineers is saveAsTable(), which writes a DataFrame as a managed Delta table in the attached Lakehouse's Tables section. The table immediately appears in the Lakehouse explorer, becomes queryable through the SQL analytics endpoint, and is included in the default semantic model. This is the operation that makes your transformation output available to the rest of the organization.

# Write a DataFrame as a managed Delta table
# mode("overwrite") replaces the entire table
df_clean.write.mode("overwrite").saveAsTable("silver_meters")

# Write with partitioning — organizes Parquet files by year and month
# Enables partition pruning: queries filtered by year/month skip irrelevant files
df_clean.write \
    .mode("overwrite") \
    .partitionBy("year", "month") \
    .saveAsTable("silver_meters")

# Append new data to an existing table — schema must match
df_today.write.mode("append").saveAsTable("silver_meters")

The partitionBy option in the second example is a crucial production decision. NordGrid partitions Silver tables by year and month because most downstream queries filter by time — the operations dashboard shows the last 30 days, billing covers one month, and regulatory reports cover one quarter. Partitioning means these queries read only the relevant partition directories, skipping months or years of data they do not need. For NordGrid's table with a year of daily data (182 million rows), partition pruning can reduce a query from scanning the entire table to scanning just one month — roughly a 12x speedup.
Write Modes

rows: Replaces the entire table with the new data
Full rebuild of the Silver table from all Bronze files
Daily Silver refresh: overwrite with today's full dataset
Adds new rows to the existing table without removing old ones
Incremental loads where new data is added daily
Appending today's readings to a running Silver table
Fails if the table already exists
Does nothing if the table already exists
Idempotent writes: safe to retry without duplicating data
Rarely used — NordGrid prefers explicit overwrite or append

blocks.content: NordGrid's Silver pipeline uses overwrite mode because the daily transformation reads all Bronze files for the current processing window (not just today's file) and rewrites the complete Silver table. This approach is simpler than append — there is no risk of duplicate rows if the pipeline runs twice, and no need to track which files have already been processed. The trade-off is that overwrite rewrites the entire table every day, which consumes more compute than appending only new data. For NordGrid's daily volume (500,000 rows), this trade-off is acceptable. At larger scale, append with deduplication would be more efficient.
Writing Files

Occasionally, you need to write files to the Files section rather than managed tables. This is useful for intermediate outputs, export files, or data that does not fit the Delta table model (like JSON reports or CSV exports for external systems). NordGrid's weekly regulatory report notebook writes a summary CSV to the Files section because the regulatory system expects a CSV upload, not a Delta table.

# Write a DataFrame as CSV to the Files section
df_report.coalesce(1).write \
    .mode("overwrite") \
    .option("header", True) \
    .csv("Files/reports/regulatory/2025/Q1/quarterly_summary")

# coalesce(1) ensures a single output file instead of many partitioned files
# This is important when the downstream consumer expects exactly one CSV file

blocks.title: Tables are the default destination
blocks.content: In most data engineering workflows, write to the Tables section using saveAsTable(). This creates a managed Delta table with ACID transactions, SQL access, and semantic model integration. Writing to the Files section is the exception — reserved for specific use cases like CSV exports, intermediate checkpoints, or data formats that Delta does not support. If you are unsure which section to write to, choose Tables.

Fabric notebooks come with a standard set of pre-installed libraries: PySpark, pandas, numpy, matplotlib, and others. But most data engineering teams need additional libraries — data validation frameworks, custom utility packages, or specific versions of existing libraries. Fabric environments solve this by letting you define a set of Python libraries and Spark configuration properties that are applied to notebooks when they run. This lesson explains what environments are, how to create them, and how NordGrid uses them to ensure every notebook runs with consistent dependencies.
What an Environment Is

An environment is a Fabric item that bundles Python library specifications and Spark configuration properties. Think of it as a recipe for the runtime: it says 'install these libraries at these versions, and set these Spark properties.' When a notebook runs with an environment attached, Fabric installs the specified libraries before executing any code and applies the Spark properties to the session. Without an environment, the notebook uses only the pre-installed libraries and the workspace's default Spark settings.
Creating an Environment

To create an environment, navigate to the workspace, click New, and select Environment. Give it a name following NordGrid's convention: env_ followed by the purpose, such as env_nordgrid_prod. The environment editor opens with two sections: Public Libraries (packages from PyPI, the Python Package Index) and Custom Libraries (wheel files uploaded manually). For each public library, you specify the package name and optionally a version constraint. After adding all libraries, click Publish to build the environment — Fabric resolves dependencies, downloads packages, and makes the environment available for notebooks.

Publishing an environment takes several minutes because Fabric builds a complete Python environment with all dependencies resolved. Changes to the environment (adding or updating a library) require republishing, which triggers a fresh dependency resolution and package download. NordGrid's platform team manages environment publishing and discourages individual engineers from modifying the production environment without review — a broken dependency or an incompatible library version can fail every notebook in the workspace the next time it runs.
Attaching an Environment

An environment can be attached at two levels. The workspace default environment applies to all notebooks in the workspace unless they specify a different one. An individual notebook can override the default by selecting a specific environment in the notebook's settings. NordGrid uses the workspace default for production (env_nordgrid_prod) and individual overrides for experimental notebooks that need libraries not in the production environment.

# Once the environment is attached, installed libraries are available immediately
# No pip install needed in the notebook — the environment handles it

# Example: using great_expectations for data validation
# (installed via the env_nordgrid_prod environment)
import great_expectations as gx

# Example: using a custom NordGrid utility library
# (uploaded as a .whl file in the environment's Custom Libraries section)
from nordgrid_utils import validate_meter_schema
Inline Library Installation

For quick experimentation during development, Fabric also supports installing libraries directly in a notebook cell using %pip install. This installs the library for the current session only — it does not persist after the session ends and does not affect other notebooks. NordGrid's convention allows %pip install in development notebooks (for trying new libraries) but prohibits it in production notebooks (where all dependencies must be in the environment for reproducibility and auditability).

# Session-scoped library installation — for development only
# The library disappears when the Spark session ends
%pip install pyarrow==14.0.1

# After installation, import and use normally
import pyarrow
print(pyarrow.__version__)  # 14.0.1

blocks.title: Production notebooks use environments, not %pip install
blocks.content: A %pip install in a production notebook creates a hidden dependency: the notebook works today because the install succeeds, but it may fail tomorrow if PyPI is unreachable, the package is yanked, or a new version introduces a breaking change. Environments pin library versions and cache the packages, making builds reproducible. NordGrid's pipeline code review checklist includes: 'no %pip install in production notebooks.'

One of the notebook's greatest strengths is cell-by-cell execution — writing a small piece of code, running it, inspecting the result, and then writing the next piece. This iterative workflow is fundamentally different from writing a complete script and running it all at once. Cell-by-cell development lets you catch errors early (after 3 lines, not after 300), inspect intermediate DataFrames at every step, and experiment with different approaches without rerunning the entire transformation. This lesson covers the development workflow NordGrid's engineers use to build transformation notebooks efficiently.
The Development Rhythm

NordGrid's engineers follow a consistent rhythm when developing a transformation notebook. The first cell imports libraries and defines the schema. The second cell reads the source data. The third cell inspects the data — show(), printSchema(), describe(). Then each subsequent cell applies one transformation: a filter, a type cast, a derived column. After each transformation cell, they run it and inspect the result with show() or count() to verify the transformation did what they expected. This one-transformation-per-cell approach makes bugs visible immediately.

# Cell 1: Setup — imports and schema definition
from pyspark.sql.functions import col, year, month, lit, current_timestamp
from pyspark.sql.types import StructType, StructField, StringType, DoubleType, DateType

meter_schema = StructType([
    StructField("meter_id", StringType(), False),
    StructField("daily_kw", DoubleType(), True),
    StructField("date", DateType(), False),
    StructField("region", StringType(), True),
    StructField("customer_type", StringType(), True),
])

# Cell 2: Read — load raw data from Bronze
df_raw = spark.read.csv(
    "Files/meter_exports/2025/03/23/readings.csv",
    header=True,
    schema=meter_schema
)
print(f"Raw rows: {df_raw.count()}")  # Expect ~500,000
df_raw.show(5)

# Cell 3: Filter — remove invalid records
df_filtered = (
    df_raw
    .filter(col("meter_id").isNotNull())   # Drop rows with no meter ID
    .filter(col("daily_kw") > 0)           # Drop negative or zero readings
    .filter(col("date").isNotNull())        # Drop rows with no date
)
print(f"After filtering: {df_filtered.count()}")  # Compare to raw count
df_filtered.show(5)

Each cell above is a self-contained step. Cell 1 sets up imports and the schema — it runs once and the variables are available to all subsequent cells. Cell 2 reads the data and shows the count — if the count is wrong (zero rows, or dramatically fewer than expected), you debug here before moving on. Cell 3 applies filters and shows the count again — the difference between the raw count and the filtered count tells you how many invalid records were removed. This step-by-step verification is far more efficient than writing the entire pipeline and debugging a wrong final result.
Cell State and Execution Order

Notebook cells share state within the Spark session. A variable defined in Cell 1 (like meter_schema) is available in Cell 5. A DataFrame created in Cell 2 (like df_raw) can be filtered in Cell 3, transformed in Cell 4, and written in Cell 5. This shared state is powerful but requires discipline: cells must be run in order. If you skip Cell 2 and run Cell 3, the df_raw variable does not exist and the cell fails. NordGrid's convention is to use Run All (a button at the top of the notebook) to verify that the complete notebook works from top to bottom before committing changes.

blocks.title: Run All before committing
blocks.content: During interactive development, you often run cells out of order — rerunning Cell 3 after fixing a bug, skipping Cell 4 temporarily, jumping to Cell 6 to check something. This is fine for exploration, but it can leave the notebook in a state where individual cells work but the complete top-to-bottom execution fails. NordGrid's rule: before committing a notebook to Git or scheduling it in a pipeline, always run Run All from a fresh session to confirm the notebook works end-to-end.

blocks.title: Cell-by-cell development with verification at each step
blocks.content:
Cell 1: Imports + Schema   ──▶ Run ──▶ ✓ No errors
         │
Cell 2: Read data           ──▶ Run ──▶ ✓ 500,000 rows
         │
Cell 3: Filter nulls        ──▶ Run ──▶ ✓ 498,200 rows (1,800 removed)
         │
Cell 4: Filter negatives    ──▶ Run ──▶ ✓ 497,900 rows (300 removed)
         │
Cell 5: Deduplicate         ──▶ Run ──▶ ✓ 497,500 rows (400 duplicates)
         │
Cell 6: Derive columns      ──▶ Run ──▶ ✓ Schema has year, month, daily_mw
         │
Cell 7: Write table         ──▶ Run ──▶ ✓ silver_meters updated
         │
Cell 8: %%sql verification  ──▶ Run ──▶ ✓ 4 regions, counts match
blocks.caption: Each cell is run and verified before moving to the next. The row count at each step tells the engineer exactly how many records were affected by each transformation. If a count is unexpected, the engineer debugs that cell before proceeding.

blocks.content: A notebook that only works for one specific date or one specific file is not a production notebook — it is a one-time script. Production notebooks need to process any date's data, accept different input paths, and adapt to different environments. Fabric notebook parameters solve this by letting you define variables that pipelines can override at runtime. This lesson explains how parameters work, how NordGrid uses them, and how they connect to the pipeline scheduling covered in Module 7.
What Parameters Are

A parameter is a variable defined in a special cell at the top of the notebook, marked with the Toggle parameter cell option. When the notebook runs interactively, parameters use their default values. When a pipeline runs the notebook as an activity, the pipeline can override any parameter with a different value. This means the same notebook handles both interactive development (using today's date as the default) and scheduled production runs (using the pipeline's date parameter).

# Parameters cell — toggle this cell as a parameter cell in the notebook toolbar
# Default values are used during interactive development
# Pipeline activities override these values at runtime

processing_date = "2025-03-23"    # The date to process — pipeline overrides this daily
input_path_base = "Files/meter_exports"  # Base path for raw files
output_table = "silver_meters"    # Target table name

The three parameters above make NordGrid's transformation notebook fully reusable. During interactive development, the engineer runs the notebook with the default processing_date of 2025-03-23. When the pipeline runs the notebook tomorrow, it passes processing_date='2025-03-24'. When the team needs to reprocess last month's data after a schema fix, the pipeline passes processing_date='2025-02-15'. The same notebook, the same transformation logic, a different date each time. The engineer never edits the notebook to change the date — the pipeline handles it.
Using Parameters in Code

# Use parameters to build dynamic paths and filter conditions
# The same code works for any processing_date value

# Build the input path dynamically from the processing date
year, month, day = processing_date.split("-")
input_path = f"{input_path_base}/{year}/{month}/{day}/readings.csv"

# Read the file for the specified date
df_raw = spark.read.csv(input_path, header=True, schema=meter_schema)
print(f"Processing {processing_date}: {df_raw.count()} rows")

# Write to the target table
df_clean.write.mode("overwrite").saveAsTable(output_table)
How Pipelines Pass Parameters

In Module 7, you will learn how to create a Data Factory pipeline with a Notebook activity. The Notebook activity has a settings panel where you specify parameter overrides — key-value pairs where the key is the parameter name (processing_date) and the value is the override (the pipeline's date expression). The pipeline can compute the value dynamically — for example, using an expression that evaluates to yesterday's date — so that the notebook always processes the correct day's data without manual intervention.

This connection between parameters and pipelines is the mechanism that turns a development notebook into a production automation. The notebook itself does not know whether it was run interactively or by a pipeline — it simply uses the parameter values. The pipeline does not know the notebook's transformation logic — it simply passes the date and triggers execution. This clean separation means the data engineer can update the transformation logic without changing the pipeline, and the pipeline team can change the schedule without editing the notebook.

blocks.title: Parameters are the bridge to automation
blocks.content: A notebook without parameters is a manual tool — you must edit it every time the input changes. A notebook with parameters is an automation-ready component — a pipeline can run it with any input, on any schedule, without human intervention. NordGrid parameterizes every production notebook with at least the processing date. This single practice is what makes the daily pipeline possible: one notebook, running automatically every night, processing whatever date the pipeline tells it to.

The previous nine lessons covered what notebooks are, how to create and configure them, how the Spark session works, which languages are available, how to read and write Lakehouse data, how environments manage libraries, how cell-by-cell development works, and how parameters enable reuse. This lesson brings everything together by tracing NordGrid's complete notebook workflow — from creating a new transformation notebook to having it running in production as a scheduled pipeline activity.
Phase 1 — Setup

A NordGrid data engineer receives a request: build a transformation notebook for the new SCADA telemetry data. The engineer navigates to ws_nordgrid_silver, creates a new notebook, renames it to nb_transform_scada, and attaches the Silver Lakehouse (lh_scada_telemetry) as the default. They verify that the workspace's default environment (env_nordgrid_prod) is active, which provides the data quality libraries the team uses. They add the Bronze Lakehouse (lh_scada_telemetry in ws_nordgrid_bronze) as a pinned Lakehouse for file browsing. The notebook is now configured and ready for code.
Phase 2 — Exploration

Before writing transformation logic, the engineer explores the source data. They write a cell that reads the Bronze Parquet file with inferred schema (acceptable for exploration, not for production). They run show() to see sample rows, printSchema() to see inferred types, and describe() to check value ranges. They discover that the rpm column is sometimes negative (sensor error), the temp column has null values for offline turbines, and there are duplicate rows from overlapping export windows. These discoveries shape the transformation logic they will write next.
Phase 3 — Development

The engineer writes the transformation logic cell by cell. Cell 1: imports and explicit schema definition. Cell 2: read Bronze Parquet with explicit schema. Cell 3: filter negative RPM values. Cell 4: handle null temperature (fill with -999 as the team's 'offline' sentinel). Cell 5: deduplicate on (turbine_id, ts). Cell 6: derive new columns (hour, day_of_week). Cell 7: add a processing_timestamp column. Cell 8: write to the Silver table. After each cell, they run it and check the output — counts, samples, schemas. They add parameters for the processing date and input path.
Phase 4 — Validation

The engineer adds verification cells at the bottom of the notebook. A %%sql cell checks the row count. Another checks for remaining nulls in required columns. A PySpark cell computes summary statistics per turbine and compares them to expected ranges. Finally, the engineer runs Run All from a fresh session to verify the complete notebook executes top-to-bottom without errors. The notebook works. The transformation logic is correct. The output table appears in the Lakehouse explorer with the expected schema.
Phase 5 — Production

The engineer commits the notebook to Git through the workspace's Git integration panel. A colleague reviews the code in a pull request, checking the schema definition, filter logic, deduplication approach, and parameter usage. After approval, the notebook is merged into the main branch. The pipeline team adds a Notebook activity to the existing pl_ingest_scada pipeline, configuring it to run nb_transform_scada after the Bronze Copy activity succeeds, passing the pipeline's processing_date parameter. The notebook is now a production component — running every night, automatically, as part of the scheduled pipeline.

blocks.title: The five phases of notebook development
blocks.content:
Phase 1          Phase 2          Phase 3          Phase 4          Phase 5
SETUP            EXPLORATION      DEVELOPMENT      VALIDATION       PRODUCTION
┌──────────┐   ┌──────────┐   ┌──────────┐   ┌──────────┐   ┌──────────┐
│Create     │   │Read with  │   │Write     │   │Run All   │   │Git commit│
│notebook   │──▶│inferred   │──▶│transform │──▶│from fresh│──▶│+ review  │
│Attach LH  │   │schema     │   │cells     │   │session   │   │Pipeline  │
│Name it    │   │Inspect    │   │one by one│   │Add SQL   │   │activity  │
│           │   │Show/desc  │   │Add params│   │checks    │   │Schedule  │
└──────────┘   └──────────┘   └──────────┘   └──────────┘   └──────────┘

  Minutes          30 min          2-4 hours         30 min         30 min
blocks.caption: The notebook workflow moves through five phases: setup (5 minutes), exploration (30 minutes for a new data source), development (2-4 hours for a complex transformation), validation (30 minutes for thorough testing), and production (30 minutes for Git, review, and pipeline integration).

blocks.content: The Complete Picture

You now have the full notebook foundation. Notebooks are interactive code editors that run on managed Spark compute (lesson 1). Creating a notebook requires naming it and attaching a Lakehouse (lesson 2). The Spark session is pre-configured — no SparkSession.builder needed (lesson 3). PySpark is the primary language, with SQL for verification (lesson 4). Reading uses spark.read.table() for tables and spark.read.csv() for files (lesson 5). Writing uses saveAsTable() for managed Delta tables (lesson 6). Environments manage libraries (lesson 7). Cell-by-cell development catches errors early (lesson 8). Parameters make notebooks reusable and pipeline-ready (lesson 9). And the five-phase workflow takes a notebook from creation to production (this lesson).

blocks.title: What comes next
