blocks.content: Module 6 fills in the transformation logic that lives inside the notebook. You will learn the specific PySpark patterns for building a Bronze-to-Silver pipeline: schema enforcement, filtering, type casting, null handling, deduplication, derived columns, and Delta table writes with validation. The notebook is your editor. Module 6 teaches you what to write in it.

chapters.title: Transforming Data in Notebooks
chapters.description: Apply PySpark transformation patterns to Lakehouse data, build the Bronze-to-Silver cleansing pipeline, and learn how Fabric's Spark environment handles the operations that turn raw ingestion into validated, queryable tables.

blocks.content: Module 5 taught you how to use notebooks — creating them, configuring the Spark session, reading and writing Lakehouse data, and developing code cell by cell. This module fills in what you write inside those cells. The transformation pipeline is the sequence of PySpark operations that converts messy Bronze files into clean Silver tables. Every operation in this module — schema enforcement, filtering, type casting, null handling, deduplication, and derived columns — is a step in that sequence. Together, they form the Bronze-to-Silver pipeline that is the core of NordGrid's data engineering work.
Why Transformation Matters

Bronze data is raw. NordGrid's meter CSV files contain string columns where numbers should be, null values where readings are missing, duplicate rows from overlapping export windows, and inconsistent region codes (sometimes 'NORTH', sometimes 'North', sometimes 'north'). If this data were served directly to analysts and dashboards, every consumer would need to handle these problems independently — the billing team would write their own null-handling logic, the operations team would write their own deduplication, and both would quietly get different answers because their logic differs. The Silver layer exists to solve this problem once, centrally, so that every downstream consumer receives clean, consistent, trustworthy data.
The Transformation Sequence

blocks.title: NordGrid's Bronze-to-Silver transformation sequence
blocks.content:
Bronze CSV File
      │
      ▼
┌─────────────────┐
│ 1. Read with     │  Apply explicit StructType schema
│    explicit      │  Catch type mismatches at read time
│    schema        │
├─────────────────┤
│ 2. Filter        │  Remove nulls in required columns
│    invalid       │  Remove negative daily_kw
│    records       │  Remove out-of-range dates
├─────────────────┤
│ 3. Cast and      │  Uppercase region codes
│    standardize   │  Trim whitespace from strings
│    types         │  Cast string dates to DateType
├─────────────────┤
│ 4. Handle        │  Replace missing regions with 'UNKNOWN'
│    nulls         │  Fill missing customer_type with default
│                  │
├─────────────────┤
│ 5. Deduplicate   │  Remove duplicate (meter_id, date) pairs
│                  │  Keep first occurrence
├─────────────────┤
│ 6. Derive new    │  Add year, month, daily_mw columns
│    columns       │  Add processing_timestamp
├─────────────────┤
│ 7. Write Delta   │  saveAsTable with partitionBy
│    table         │  Overwrite mode for daily refresh
├─────────────────┤
│ 8. Validate      │  Check row counts, null counts,
│    output        │  value ranges, region completeness
└─────────────────┘
      │
      ▼
Silver Delta Table
blocks.caption: The transformation pipeline has eight steps, each addressing a specific data quality concern. The order matters: schema enforcement comes first (catch structural problems early), validation comes last (confirm the final output is correct). Every step between produces an intermediate DataFrame that the next step builds on.

blocks.content: One Concept per Lesson

Each lesson in this module covers one step of the transformation sequence. Lesson 2 covers schema enforcement. Lesson 3 covers filtering. Lesson 4 covers derived columns. Lesson 5 covers null handling. Lesson 6 covers type casting and standardization. Lesson 7 covers deduplication. Lesson 8 covers Delta table writes. Lesson 9 covers output validation. Lesson 10 brings everything together into the complete Silver pipeline. By the end of the module, you will have seen every line of code in NordGrid's production transformation notebook.
The Immutability Pattern

Every transformation in PySpark produces a new DataFrame — it does not modify the original. When you filter a DataFrame, you get a new DataFrame with fewer rows; the original is unchanged. When you add a column with withColumn, you get a new DataFrame with one more column; the original still has the old column set. NordGrid's notebook chains these transformations by assigning each result to a new variable: df_raw becomes df_filtered becomes df_standardized becomes df_deduped becomes df_final. This chain makes it easy to inspect any intermediate step — just run df_filtered.show() to see the data after filtering but before deduplication.

blocks.title: Transform once, serve many
blocks.content: The Silver transformation runs once per day and produces one clean table. That table is read by the Gold aggregation notebook, the analyst SQL queries, the data science forecast model, and the Power BI dashboard. Every consumer gets the same clean data because the cleaning happened once, in one place, with one set of rules. This is the fundamental value of the transformation pipeline: it centralizes data quality decisions so that downstream consumers do not need to make them independently.

The first step in any transformation pipeline is enforcing a schema — defining exactly which columns you expect, what data types they should have, and whether they can be null. Without schema enforcement, you are at the mercy of the source system: if the meter vendor adds an extra column, changes a column name, or starts sending dates in a different format, your pipeline may silently produce wrong results instead of failing with a clear error. Schema enforcement catches structural problems at read time, when they are cheapest to diagnose and fix.
Why Not Infer?

Spark can infer a CSV file's schema by sampling the data — reading a portion of the file and guessing column types. This works for quick exploration, but it fails in production for two reasons. First, inference is unreliable: a column containing 499,999 numbers and one string ('N/A') might be inferred as StringType, losing the numeric nature of the data. Second, inference is inconsistent: today's file might infer daily_kw as DoubleType because all values are numeric, but tomorrow's file might infer it as StringType because a sensor malfunction wrote 'ERROR' in one row. Explicit schema enforcement eliminates both problems by defining the types upfront.
Defining the Schema

from pyspark.sql.types import (
    StructType, StructField, StringType, DoubleType, DateType
)

# NordGrid's meter reading schema — the contract between Bronze and Silver
# This schema defines exactly what the pipeline expects from the raw CSV
meter_schema = StructType([
    StructField("meter_id", StringType(), nullable=False),     # Required: unique meter identifier
    StructField("daily_kw", DoubleType(), nullable=True),      # Nullable: sensors can malfunction
    StructField("date", DateType(), nullable=False),            # Required: reading date
    StructField("region", StringType(), nullable=True),         # Nullable: some legacy meters lack region
    StructField("customer_type", StringType(), nullable=True),  # Nullable: not always populated
])

The schema above is NordGrid's contract with the Bronze data. It says: the file must have exactly five columns with these names and these types. meter_id and date are non-nullable — a reading without a meter ID or a date is meaningless. daily_kw, region, and customer_type are nullable — sensors malfunction, legacy meters lack region data, and customer_type is not always populated. This schema is defined once, at the top of the notebook, and used in every read operation.
Applying the Schema

# Read the Bronze CSV with the explicit schema
df_raw = spark.read.csv(
    "Files/meter_exports/2025/03/23/readings.csv",
    header=True,
    schema=meter_schema  # Enforces column names, types, and nullability
)

# Verify the schema was applied correctly
df_raw.printSchema()
# root
#  |-- meter_id: string (nullable = false)
#  |-- daily_kw: double (nullable = true)
#  |-- date: date (nullable = false)
#  |-- region: string (nullable = true)
#  |-- customer_type: string (nullable = true)

print(f"Rows read: {df_raw.count()}")

When Spark reads the CSV with this schema, it maps each CSV column to the specified type. If the CSV has an extra column that the schema does not include, the extra column is silently ignored. If the CSV is missing a column that the schema requires, Spark fills it with null. If a value cannot be cast to the specified type (for example, the string 'ERROR' cannot become a DoubleType), Spark sets that cell to null rather than failing the entire read. This permissive behavior is useful because it lets the pipeline read the file even when individual cells have problems — the filtering step (lesson 3) handles the nulls.
What Schema Enforcement Catches

rows: Extra column appears in DataFrame, may confuse downstream
Extra column silently ignored — DataFrame matches expected shape
Column type changes (string in numeric field)
Inferred as StringType — arithmetic fails later
Value set to null — caught by null filter in next step
DataFrame has wrong column names — code fails on column reference
Column filled with null — caught immediately by row count check
Inferred inconsistently — some rows parse, others become strings
Non-parseable dates become null — caught by null filter

blocks.title: Schema enforcement does not validate data quality
blocks.content: Schema enforcement ensures structural correctness — the right columns with the right types. It does not ensure data quality — it will not catch a daily_kw value of 999,999 that is technically a valid DoubleType but clearly a sensor error, or a date of 2099-01-01 that is a valid DateType but clearly wrong. Data quality validation (filtering unreasonable values) is a separate step covered in the next lesson. Schema enforcement is the first line of defense, not the last.

After schema enforcement ensures that the DataFrame has the right columns and types, the next step is filtering — removing rows that do not meet NordGrid's data quality criteria. A row might have the correct types but still be invalid: a null meter_id (the reading belongs to no meter), a negative daily_kw (physically impossible — meters do not generate negative energy), or a date in the future (the reading claims to be from tomorrow, which has not happened yet). Filtering removes these rows from the DataFrame so that only valid data reaches the Silver table.
NordGrid's Quality Rules

NordGrid's Silver layer enforces five data quality rules. Each rule eliminates a specific category of invalid data, and each is implemented as a filter() call on the DataFrame. The rules are applied in sequence: the output of one filter becomes the input to the next. The order does not matter logically (all five rules must pass for a row to survive), but applying the cheapest filters first (null checks) before expensive filters (date range comparisons) is a minor performance optimization that NordGrid follows by convention.

from pyspark.sql.functions import col, current_date

# Apply NordGrid's five quality rules as sequential filters
# Each filter removes rows that fail one specific quality criterion

df_filtered = (
    df_raw
    # Rule 1: meter_id must not be null — a reading without a meter is meaningless
    .filter(col("meter_id").isNotNull())
    
    # Rule 2: daily_kw must be positive — negative energy is physically impossible
    .filter(col("daily_kw") > 0)
    
    # Rule 3: date must not be null — a reading without a date cannot be placed in time
    .filter(col("date").isNotNull())
    
    # Rule 4: date must not be in the future — future readings are data errors
    .filter(col("date") <= current_date())
    
    # Rule 5: daily_kw must be below 10,000 — values above this are sensor malfunctions
    .filter(col("daily_kw") < 10000)
)

print(f"Rows before filtering: {df_raw.count()}")
print(f"Rows after filtering:  {df_filtered.count()}")
print(f"Rows removed:          {df_raw.count() - df_filtered.count()}")

The output of this code tells NordGrid's engineers exactly how many rows were removed. On a typical day, the raw file contains approximately 500,000 rows, and filtering removes 1,500 to 2,500 — roughly 0.3% to 0.5% of the data. A removal rate significantly higher than this (say, 5%) would signal a source-system problem: perhaps the vendor changed the export format, or a region's meters are malfunctioning. The engineers check this percentage every morning as part of their verification routine.
Chaining Filters vs Separate Filters

The code above chains all five filters into one expression using the dot-chaining pattern. An alternative approach is to apply each filter as a separate statement with a new variable: df_no_nulls = df_raw.filter(...), then df_positive = df_no_nulls.filter(...), and so on. Both approaches produce identical results because Spark's Catalyst optimizer combines chained filters into a single operation regardless of how you write them. NordGrid uses the chained style because it is more concise, but the separate-variable style is useful during debugging — you can inspect the DataFrame after each filter to see which rule removed which rows.

# Alternative: separate filters for debugging — inspect after each step
df_step1 = df_raw.filter(col("meter_id").isNotNull())
print(f"After null meter_id filter: {df_step1.count()}")

df_step2 = df_step1.filter(col("daily_kw") > 0)
print(f"After positive kw filter:   {df_step2.count()}")

df_step3 = df_step2.filter(col("date").isNotNull())
print(f"After null date filter:     {df_step3.count()}")

# Use this style when you need to identify which rule removes the most rows
# Switch to the chained style for production code
Documenting Quality Rules

Every filter in the pipeline should have a comment explaining the business reason — not just what it does, but why. The comment '# negative energy is physically impossible' is more valuable than '# filter negative values' because it tells a future reader why the rule exists, which matters when someone asks whether the rule should be changed. NordGrid maintains a data quality rules document that lists every Silver rule, its justification, the date it was added, and who approved it. The notebook comments reference this document, creating a traceable link between code and business logic.

blocks.title: Filters are business decisions
blocks.content: Every filter in this notebook is a business decision disguised as a line of code. Deciding that daily_kw above 10,000 is invalid is not a technical choice — it is a domain judgment about what constitutes a sensor malfunction. NordGrid's data engineers did not choose this threshold alone; they consulted with the grid operations team, reviewed historical data, and settled on 10,000 as the cutoff. If the threshold changes (because NordGrid installs higher-capacity meters), the notebook code changes — but only after the business approves the new rule.

Raw data rarely contains every column that downstream consumers need. NordGrid's Bronze CSV has five columns: meter_id, daily_kw, date, region, and customer_type. But the Silver table needs additional columns that do not exist in the source: year and month for partition-based querying, daily_mw for megawatt-scale analysis, and processing_timestamp to record when each row was transformed. These columns are derived — computed from existing columns using PySpark functions. This lesson covers the withColumn method and the most common derivation patterns.
The withColumn Pattern

The withColumn method adds a new column to a DataFrame (or replaces an existing one). It takes two arguments: the column name and an expression that computes the column's value for each row. Because DataFrames are immutable, withColumn returns a new DataFrame with the additional column — the original DataFrame is unchanged. NordGrid chains multiple withColumn calls to add all derived columns in one fluent expression.

from pyspark.sql.functions import (
    col, year, month, round, lit, current_timestamp
)

# Derive new columns from existing data
df_enriched = (
    df_filtered
    # Extract year and month from the date column — used for partitioning
    .withColumn("year", year(col("date")))
    .withColumn("month", month(col("date")))
    
    # Convert daily_kw to daily_mw (megawatts = kilowatts / 1000)
    # Round to 4 decimal places for consistent precision
    .withColumn("daily_mw", round(col("daily_kw") / 1000, 4))
    
    # Add processing metadata — when was this row transformed?
    .withColumn("processing_timestamp", current_timestamp())
)

The year and month columns are the most important derivations because they enable partition pruning. When NordGrid writes the Silver table with partitionBy('year', 'month'), Spark organizes the Parquet files into directories like year=2025/month=03/. A query that filters WHERE year = 2025 AND month = 3 reads only that directory, skipping all other months. Without the derived year and month columns, the table cannot be partitioned by time — and every query must scan the entire table.
Common Derivation Patterns

rows: Kilowatts to megawatts for regulatory reports
Adding a source identifier to multi-source tables

blocks.content: Conditional Derivations

from pyspark.sql.functions import when

# Categorize daily consumption into tiers
# NordGrid's operations team uses these tiers for anomaly detection
df_with_tier = df_enriched.withColumn(
    "consumption_tier",
    when(col("daily_kw") < 10, "LOW")
    .when(col("daily_kw") < 50, "NORMAL")
    .when(col("daily_kw") < 200, "HIGH")
    .otherwise("CRITICAL")  # Above 200 kW triggers an operations alert
)

The when().otherwise() pattern is PySpark's equivalent of a CASE WHEN expression in SQL. The conditions are evaluated in order: the first matching condition determines the value. NordGrid's consumption tiers are a business classification defined by the operations team — LOW readings are typical for residential meters, NORMAL for small commercial, HIGH for industrial, and CRITICAL triggers an automated alert. Adding this classification in the Silver layer means every downstream consumer (dashboards, reports, alerts) uses the same tier definitions.

blocks.title: Derive, do not duplicate
blocks.content: Every derived column should be computable from the existing columns — it should not require external data that is not in the DataFrame. If you need data from another table (like substation capacity for a per-substation percentage), use a join (covered in the PySpark Junior-Level Track, Module 7) rather than a derivation. Derived columns add information that is implicit in the existing data. Joins add information from a different data source.

Null values are the most pervasive data quality challenge in any pipeline. NordGrid's Bronze files contain nulls for many reasons: a meter's sensor malfunctioned and reported no reading, a legacy meter was installed before the region code system existed, a customer account was created without a type classification, or the vendor's export process truncated a row. Some nulls are fatal (a reading with no meter_id is useless), while others are manageable (a missing region can be replaced with 'UNKNOWN'). This lesson covers how nulls propagate through PySpark expressions and the strategies NordGrid uses to handle them.
How Nulls Propagate

In PySpark, null propagates through most expressions. If daily_kw is null, then daily_kw / 1000 is also null — not zero, not an error, just null. If region is null, then upper(region) is null. If date is null, then year(date) is null. This propagation means that a single null in a row can cascade through every derived column that depends on it. NordGrid's transformation handles nulls explicitly rather than letting them propagate silently, because silent propagation leads to unexpected nulls in the Silver table that confuse downstream consumers.
Strategy 1 — Remove Rows with Critical Nulls

For columns where null makes the row meaningless, the correct strategy is to remove the row entirely. NordGrid removes rows where meter_id is null (a reading with no meter cannot be attributed to any customer) and where date is null (a reading with no date cannot be placed in time). This removal was already covered in lesson 3 as part of the filtering step. The important concept is that null removal should happen early in the pipeline — before derived columns are computed — so that derived columns never encounter the null in the first place.
Strategy 2 — Replace with a Default Value

from pyspark.sql.functions import col, coalesce, lit, when

# Replace null region with 'UNKNOWN' — the operations team agreed
# that unregioned meters should appear as a separate category, not disappear
df_nulls_handled = (
    df_filtered
    # coalesce returns the first non-null value from its arguments
    .withColumn("region", coalesce(col("region"), lit("UNKNOWN")))
    
    # Replace null customer_type with 'UNCLASSIFIED'
    .withColumn("customer_type", coalesce(col("customer_type"), lit("UNCLASSIFIED")))
)

# Alternative: use fillna for multiple columns at once
df_nulls_handled = df_filtered.fillna({
    "region": "UNKNOWN",
    "customer_type": "UNCLASSIFIED"
})

The choice between coalesce and fillna is stylistic — both produce the same result. NordGrid uses coalesce for single-column replacements embedded in a withColumn chain, and fillna when replacing defaults for many columns at once. The replacement values ('UNKNOWN', 'UNCLASSIFIED') are business decisions: the operations team decided that meters without a region should appear in dashboards as a distinct 'UNKNOWN' category rather than being excluded or defaulting to a real region. This way, the operations team can see how many unregioned meters exist and track the problem.
Strategy 3 — Conditional Replacement

# Replace null daily_kw with 0 only for meters that are known to be offline
# (identified by a specific customer_type pattern)
# Do NOT replace null daily_kw for active meters — those nulls indicate
# genuine sensor failures that should be investigated

df_conditional = df_filtered.withColumn(
    "daily_kw",
    when(
        col("daily_kw").isNull() & col("customer_type").startswith("OFFLINE"),
        lit(0.0)  # Known offline meter — zero consumption is correct
    ).otherwise(col("daily_kw"))  # Keep original value (including null for active meters)
)

This conditional pattern is more nuanced than a blanket fillna. NordGrid distinguishes between nulls that have a known cause (offline meters should report zero) and nulls that indicate a problem (active meters with null readings need investigation). The when().otherwise() pattern lets you apply different null-handling logic based on the row's context. This prevents the common mistake of replacing all nulls with zero — which would hide genuine sensor failures in the data.

blocks.title: Null handling is a design decision
blocks.content: There is no universally correct way to handle nulls. Removing the row, replacing with a default, leaving it as null, and conditional replacement are all valid strategies — the right choice depends on what the null means in context. NordGrid's data engineers do not make these decisions alone; they work with the operations team and the billing team to define the null-handling rules for each column. The notebook implements those rules, but the rules themselves are business decisions.

blocks.title: Three null-handling strategies applied to NordGrid columns
blocks.content:
Column          Null Meaning              Strategy            Result
────────────    ────────────────────      ──────────────      ─────────────
meter_id        Reading has no meter      REMOVE ROW          Row deleted
                (meaningless data)        filter(isNotNull)

region          Legacy meter lacks        REPLACE WITH        'UNKNOWN'
                region assignment         DEFAULT VALUE
                                          coalesce(lit(...))

daily_kw        Active: sensor failure    CONDITIONAL         Keep null
(active meter)  (needs investigation)     REPLACEMENT         (flag for review)

daily_kw        Offline: expected zero    CONDITIONAL         Replace with 0.0
(offline meter) consumption               REPLACEMENT
blocks.caption: Different columns require different null strategies. meter_id nulls are fatal (remove the row). region nulls are manageable (replace with a default). daily_kw nulls depend on context (keep null for active meters, replace with zero for offline meters). The strategy is determined by the business meaning of the null, not by a blanket rule.

blocks.content: Even with an explicit schema applied at read time, the data inside columns may need further standardization. Region codes arrive in mixed case ('NORTH', 'North', 'north'), string columns have leading or trailing whitespace, and some columns that should be numeric arrive as strings because the source system encodes them that way. Type casting and standardization ensure that every value in the Silver table conforms to a consistent format — uppercase region codes, trimmed strings, correct numeric types — so that downstream consumers never need to handle formatting inconsistencies.
String Standardization

from pyspark.sql.functions import col, upper, trim, regexp_replace

# Standardize string columns for consistency
df_standardized = (
    df_nulls_handled
    # Uppercase region codes — 'North' and 'north' become 'NORTH'
    .withColumn("region", upper(col("region")))
    
    # Trim whitespace from all string columns
    # Leading/trailing spaces cause silent join failures and groupBy mismatches
    .withColumn("meter_id", trim(col("meter_id")))
    .withColumn("region", trim(col("region")))
    .withColumn("customer_type", trim(col("customer_type")))
    
    # Remove non-printable characters from meter_id
    # Some vendor exports include control characters that are invisible but break equality
    .withColumn("meter_id", regexp_replace(col("meter_id"), "[^\\x20-\\x7E]", ""))
)

The trim operation is especially important and easy to overlook. A region value of 'NORTH ' (with a trailing space) is not equal to 'NORTH' in PySpark comparisons, groupBy operations, or joins. Without trimming, a groupBy('region') would produce two separate groups — 'NORTH' and 'NORTH ' — splitting what should be a single region into two. NordGrid discovered this problem when their regional summary showed five regions instead of four. The fifth region was an invisible trailing space. Adding trim to every string column prevents this class of bug entirely.
Type Casting

When the explicit schema handles type mapping at read time (as described in lesson 2), most columns arrive with the correct types. But some scenarios require explicit casting in the transformation logic. If a column arrives as StringType from a source that does not support numeric types (some CSV exports and JSON APIs), you need to cast it to the correct type after reading. NordGrid's weather data JSON has temperature values encoded as strings ('12.5' instead of 12.5) because the API serializes all values as strings.

from pyspark.sql.types import DoubleType, IntegerType

# Cast string columns to their correct types
# Use this pattern when the source encodes numbers as strings
df_cast = (
    df_standardized
    # Cast daily_kw from string to double (if schema inference was used for exploration)
    .withColumn("daily_kw", col("daily_kw").cast(DoubleType()))
    
    # Cast a string year column to integer
    .withColumn("year", col("year").cast(IntegerType()))
)

# Verify the types after casting
df_cast.printSchema()

When a cast fails — for example, casting the string 'ERROR' to DoubleType — PySpark sets the value to null rather than raising an error. This means that after casting, you should check for unexpected nulls that might indicate failed casts. NordGrid adds a validation step after casting: counting the nulls in the cast column and comparing to the null count before casting. If the count increased, some values failed to cast, and the team investigates the source data to understand why.
The Standardization Checklist

items: Uppercase all categorical string columns (region, customer_type, status codes) to eliminate case-sensitivity issues in groupBy and joins.
Trim all string columns to remove leading and trailing whitespace that causes invisible matching failures.
Remove non-printable characters from identifier columns (meter_id) that can arrive from vendor exports with encoding artifacts.
Cast any columns that arrived as StringType but should be numeric (DoubleType, IntegerType) or temporal (DateType, TimestampType).
Verify null counts after casting to detect failed type conversions that PySpark silently converts to null.

blocks.title: Standardize before deduplication
blocks.content: Type casting and standardization must happen before deduplication (lesson 7). If 'M-00001' and 'M-00001 ' (with a trailing space) are two rows with the same meter_id and date, they should be treated as duplicates. But without trimming first, PySpark treats them as different keys and keeps both rows. NordGrid's pipeline applies standardization in step 3 and deduplication in step 5 specifically to prevent this class of error.

Duplicate rows appear in NordGrid's Bronze data for a straightforward reason: the meter collection system's export process overlaps. The system generates each day's CSV at 01:30 UTC, but the export window includes a 30-minute buffer — readings from 00:00 to 00:30 appear in both yesterday's file and today's file. If both files are loaded without deduplication, meters that reported during that overlap window have two rows for the same date. The Silver table should have exactly one row per meter per day. Deduplication ensures this uniqueness.
The Composite Key

Deduplication requires defining which columns constitute the unique key — the combination of columns that identifies a single logical record. For NordGrid's meter readings, the key is (meter_id, date): each meter should have exactly one reading per day. If two rows have the same meter_id and the same date, they are duplicates regardless of whether the other columns (daily_kw, region) differ. The duplicate with the different daily_kw value is a data error from the overlapping export window, and one of them must be discarded.

# Deduplicate on the composite key (meter_id, date)
# dropDuplicates keeps the first occurrence and removes subsequent duplicates
df_deduped = df_standardized.dropDuplicates(["meter_id", "date"])

print(f"Before deduplication: {df_standardized.count()}")
print(f"After deduplication:  {df_deduped.count()}")
print(f"Duplicates removed:   {df_standardized.count() - df_deduped.count()}")

The dropDuplicates method is the simplest deduplication approach: it keeps one row per unique key combination and discards the rest. Which row it keeps is not guaranteed — Spark may keep any of the duplicates. For NordGrid's daily overwrite pipeline, this is acceptable because the duplicate rows have the same daily_kw value (they are the same reading exported twice). If the duplicates had different values and the choice mattered, you would need a more sophisticated approach using window functions — a topic covered in the PySpark Junior-Level Track's Module 9.
Full-Row vs Key-Based Deduplication

left.label: distinct() — Full-Row
points: Removes rows where ALL columns are identical
Two rows with same meter_id/date but different daily_kw are NOT duplicates
Use when you want exact row-level deduplication
NordGrid use: never — full-row match is too strict

right.label: dropDuplicates([cols]) — Key-Based
points: Removes rows where SPECIFIED columns are identical
Two rows with same meter_id/date but different daily_kw ARE duplicates
Use when you have a business key that defines uniqueness
NordGrid use: always — the business key is (meter_id, date)

blocks.content: Monitoring Duplicate Rates

The number of duplicates removed is a useful diagnostic metric. NordGrid's daily pipeline typically removes 400 to 800 duplicates (the overlap window for 500,000 meters). If the duplicate count suddenly jumps to 50,000, it signals a source-system problem — perhaps the vendor exported the same file twice, or the overlap window expanded. NordGrid logs the duplicate count as a pipeline metric and alerts the team when it exceeds 2,000 (four times the normal maximum). This monitoring turns a routine data quality step into an early warning system for upstream issues.

blocks.title: Deduplication is the last cleaning step
blocks.content: In NordGrid's pipeline, deduplication runs after schema enforcement, filtering, null handling, and standardization — it is the last cleaning step before derived columns and the final write. This order matters: standardization ensures that 'M-00001' and 'M-00001 ' are recognized as the same meter_id, and null handling ensures that two rows with null meter_id are not incorrectly treated as duplicates of each other (null does not equal null in PySpark). Running deduplication earlier, before standardization, would miss duplicates that differ only in whitespace or casing.

After all transformations are applied — schema enforcement, filtering, null handling, standardization, deduplication, and derived columns — the final DataFrame is ready to be written to the Silver Lakehouse as a managed Delta table. Module 5 introduced the saveAsTable method. This lesson goes deeper into the write operation: choosing the right write mode, partitioning for performance, handling schema evolution when the table structure changes, and the maintenance operations that keep Delta tables healthy over time.
The Production Write

# The complete production write for NordGrid's Silver meter table
df_final.write \
    .format("delta") \
    .mode("overwrite") \
    .partitionBy("year", "month") \
    .option("overwriteSchema", "false") \
    .saveAsTable("silver_meters")

Every option in this write has a reason. The format is delta because Delta is the only managed table format in Fabric Lakehouses. The mode is overwrite because NordGrid's daily pipeline rebuilds the entire Silver table from all Bronze files in the processing window. The partitionBy columns are year and month because most downstream queries filter by time. The overwriteSchema option is false as a safety measure — if the DataFrame's schema differs from the existing table's schema, the write will fail rather than silently changing the table's structure.
Schema Evolution

When a legitimate schema change is needed — for example, NordGrid's Silver table needs a new column called consumption_tier — the overwriteSchema safety must be temporarily relaxed. Fabric supports two schema evolution modes. The mergeSchema option adds new columns to the table without removing existing ones. The overwriteSchema option replaces the table's schema entirely. NordGrid uses mergeSchema for additive changes (new columns) and avoids overwriteSchema unless the table is being rebuilt from scratch.

# Adding a new column to an existing table — use mergeSchema
# The new 'consumption_tier' column is added to the table's schema
# Existing rows get null for the new column until they are reprocessed
df_with_tier.write \
    .format("delta") \
    .mode("overwrite") \
    .partitionBy("year", "month") \
    .option("mergeSchema", "true") \
    .saveAsTable("silver_meters")

After a mergeSchema write, the table's Delta log records the new schema. The SQL analytics endpoint detects the change and adds the new column to its metadata — analysts can query the new column immediately through T-SQL. The semantic model also updates to include the new column. This automatic propagation is one of the advantages of the managed table model: a single write updates the Lakehouse, the SQL endpoint, and the semantic model simultaneously.
Table Maintenance

Over time, Delta tables accumulate small files (from frequent appends) and obsolete files (from old table versions). Small files degrade query performance because Spark must open many files instead of a few large ones. Obsolete files consume storage. Fabric provides two maintenance operations: OPTIMIZE compacts small files into larger ones, and VACUUM removes obsolete files beyond the retention period. NordGrid runs both weekly as part of a maintenance notebook.

-- Run in a %%sql cell or in the SQL endpoint

-- OPTIMIZE: compact small files into larger ones
-- Improves read performance by reducing the number of files Spark opens
OPTIMIZE silver_meters;

-- VACUUM: remove obsolete files older than the retention period
-- Default retention is 7 days (168 hours) — do not set below this
VACUUM silver_meters RETAIN 336 HOURS;  -- 14 days retention

blocks.title: VACUUM deletes time travel history
blocks.content: VACUUM removes the physical files that back older table versions. After vacuuming with a 14-day retention, you can no longer time-travel to versions older than 14 days — those files no longer exist. NordGrid sets retention to 14 days as a balance between debugging capability (two weeks is enough to investigate most issues) and storage efficiency (keeping every version forever would triple storage costs). Never set retention below 7 days without understanding the consequences.

Writing the Silver table is not the last step — validation is. A transformation that runs without errors can still produce wrong results: a filter condition with a typo that removes too many rows, a withColumn expression that produces nulls instead of values, or a partitionBy on the wrong column. Validation catches these errors by checking the output against known expectations. NordGrid validates every Silver table write with a set of automated checks that run at the end of the transformation notebook. If any check fails, the team investigates before downstream consumers read the data.
Row Count Validation

# Validate row count — should be approximately 500,000 for a normal day
silver_count = spark.read.table("silver_meters") \
    .filter(col("date") == processing_date) \
    .count()

print(f"Silver row count for {processing_date}: {silver_count}")

# Alert if count is outside expected range
if silver_count < 450000 or silver_count > 550000:
    print(f"WARNING: Row count {silver_count} outside expected range [450000, 550000]")
    # In production, this would trigger a notification or fail the pipeline

The expected range (450,000 to 550,000) is derived from historical data: NordGrid's daily meter count has been between 480,000 and 520,000 for the past year, with ±10% margin for seasonal variation (more meters active in winter when heating demand is high). A count below 450,000 suggests a source-system problem (partial export, missing region). A count above 550,000 suggests a deduplication failure (duplicates not removed) or a sudden meter fleet expansion (legitimate but worth verifying).
Null Count Validation

-- Validate that required columns have no nulls after transformation
-- This should return all zeros — any non-zero value indicates a pipeline bug
SELECT
    SUM(CASE WHEN meter_id IS NULL THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS null_meter_ids,
    SUM(CASE WHEN daily_kw IS NULL THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS null_daily_kw,
    SUM(CASE WHEN date IS NULL THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS null_dates,
    SUM(CASE WHEN region IS NULL THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS null_regions,
    SUM(CASE WHEN year IS NULL THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS null_years
FROM silver_meters
WHERE date = '2025-03-23'

After the transformation, meter_id, date, and year should have zero nulls (they were filtered or derived from non-null sources). daily_kw should have zero nulls (negative and null values were removed in the filter step). region should have zero nulls (nulls were replaced with 'UNKNOWN'). If any of these counts are non-zero, a transformation step has a bug — either the filter did not work as expected, or the null-handling logic missed a case.
Region Completeness

-- Validate that all expected regions are present
-- NordGrid expects exactly 4 regions (plus potentially UNKNOWN)
SELECT region, COUNT(*) AS meter_count
FROM silver_meters
WHERE date = '2025-03-23'
GROUP BY region
ORDER BY region

This query should return at least four rows: EAST, NORTH, SOUTH, and WEST, each with a count in the range of 100,000 to 150,000. A missing region means that an entire region's meters were lost during transformation — a serious data loss that would cause the regional dashboard to show zero consumption for that region. An extra region (other than UNKNOWN) means the source data contains a region code that NordGrid's standardization did not expect — perhaps the vendor introduced a new region without notifying the data team.
The Validation Checklist

items: Row count within the expected range for a normal day (450,000 to 550,000 for NordGrid). Flag significant deviations as warnings.
Zero nulls in columns that should be non-null after transformation (meter_id, daily_kw, date, region, year).
All four expected regions present in the output, each with a reasonable count.
daily_kw values within expected range: minimum > 0, maximum < 10,000, average between 5 and 50.
No duplicate (meter_id, date) pairs — deduplication should have eliminated all duplicates.

blocks.title: Validation catches what unit tests cannot
blocks.content: Unit tests verify that individual functions work correctly — that a filter removes the right rows, that a cast produces the right type. Validation checks verify that the complete pipeline produces correct results on real data. A unit test might pass while the validation fails because the real data contains a pattern the unit test did not anticipate. NordGrid runs both: unit tests during development (covered in the mid-level track) and validation checks at the end of every production run.

The previous nine lessons covered each transformation step individually: schema enforcement, filtering, null handling, derived columns, type casting, standardization, deduplication, Delta table writes, and validation. This lesson assembles every step into NordGrid's complete Silver transformation notebook — the same notebook that runs every night as a pipeline activity, converting raw Bronze CSV files into the clean, queryable Silver Delta table that feeds the entire organization.
The Complete Notebook

# ── Cell 1: Parameters (toggle as parameter cell) ──────────────────────
processing_date = "2025-03-23"
input_path_base = "abfss://ws_nordgrid_bronze@onelake.dfs.fabric.microsoft.com/lh_meter_readings.Lakehouse/Files/meter_exports"
output_table = "silver_meters"

# ── Cell 2: Imports and Schema ──────────────────────────────────────────
from pyspark.sql.functions import (
    col, year, month, round, lit, upper, trim, regexp_replace,
    coalesce, when, current_timestamp, current_date
)
from pyspark.sql.types import StructType, StructField, StringType, DoubleType, DateType

meter_schema = StructType([
    StructField("meter_id", StringType(), nullable=False),
    StructField("daily_kw", DoubleType(), nullable=True),
    StructField("date", DateType(), nullable=False),
    StructField("region", StringType(), nullable=True),
    StructField("customer_type", StringType(), nullable=True),
])

# ── Cell 3: Read Bronze ─────────────────────────────────────────────────
y, m, d = processing_date.split("-")
input_path = f"{input_path_base}/{y}/{m}/{d}/readings.csv"

df_raw = spark.read.csv(input_path, header=True, schema=meter_schema)
print(f"Raw rows: {df_raw.count()}")

# ── Cell 4: Filter, Standardize, Handle Nulls, Deduplicate ─────────────
df_clean = (
    df_raw
    # Filter invalid records
    .filter(col("meter_id").isNotNull())
    .filter(col("daily_kw") > 0)
    .filter(col("daily_kw") < 10000)
    .filter(col("date").isNotNull())
    .filter(col("date") <= current_date())
    # Standardize strings
    .withColumn("meter_id", trim(regexp_replace(col("meter_id"), "[^\\x20-\\x7E]", "")))
    .withColumn("region", upper(trim(col("region"))))
    .withColumn("customer_type", trim(col("customer_type")))
    # Handle nulls
    .withColumn("region", coalesce(col("region"), lit("UNKNOWN")))
    .withColumn("customer_type", coalesce(col("customer_type"), lit("UNCLASSIFIED")))
    # Deduplicate
    .dropDuplicates(["meter_id", "date"])
)
print(f"Clean rows: {df_clean.count()}")

# ── Cell 5: Derive Columns and Write ────────────────────────────────────
df_final = (
    df_clean
    .withColumn("year", year(col("date")))
    .withColumn("month", month(col("date")))
    .withColumn("daily_mw", round(col("daily_kw") / 1000, 4))
    .withColumn("processing_timestamp", current_timestamp())
)

df_final.write \
    .format("delta") \
    .mode("overwrite") \
    .partitionBy("year", "month") \
    .saveAsTable(output_table)

print(f"Written to {output_table}")

-- ── Cell 6: Validation (%%sql) ──────────────────────────────────────────
SELECT
    COUNT(*) AS total_rows,
    SUM(CASE WHEN meter_id IS NULL THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS null_meter_ids,
    SUM(CASE WHEN daily_kw IS NULL THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS null_kw,
    SUM(CASE WHEN region IS NULL THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS null_regions,
    COUNT(DISTINCT region) AS distinct_regions,
    ROUND(AVG(daily_kw), 2) AS avg_kw,
    MAX(daily_kw) AS max_kw
FROM silver_meters
WHERE date = '2025-03-23'

Six cells. That is the complete NordGrid Silver transformation notebook. Cell 1 defines parameters that the pipeline overrides daily. Cell 2 imports functions and defines the schema. Cell 3 reads the Bronze CSV. Cell 4 applies all cleaning operations in one chained expression. Cell 5 derives new columns and writes the Delta table. Cell 6 validates the output. When this notebook runs as a pipeline activity, it takes approximately 12 minutes to process 500,000 rows — 30 seconds for the Spark cold start, 2 minutes for the read, 8 minutes for the transformation and write, and 1 minute for the validation query.
The Transformation at a Glance

rows: withColumn for year, month, daily_mw, timestamp
SQL count, null check, region check

blocks.title: What comes next
blocks.content: Module 7 takes the notebook you just built and puts it on a schedule. You will learn how Data Factory pipelines orchestrate the complete flow: a Copy activity ingests the Bronze file, a Notebook activity runs the transformation, and the pipeline runs automatically every night at 02:00 UTC. The transformation logic is complete. Now it needs automation.

chapters.title: Data Pipelines — Orchestration in Fabric
chapters.description: Understand how Data Factory pipelines orchestrate data movement and transformation in Fabric, learn to build pipelines that schedule notebooks and copy data, and implement NordGrid's daily ingestion and transformation schedule.

blocks.content: Module 5 taught you how to write notebooks. Module 6 taught you what to write inside them — the transformation logic that converts Bronze files into Silver tables. But a notebook sitting in a workspace does nothing on its own. Someone must run it. Someone must ensure it runs after the Bronze data arrives, not before. Someone must handle the case where it fails. A data pipeline is the automation layer that handles all of this: it defines what runs, in what order, on what schedule, and what happens when something goes wrong. This module teaches you how to build and manage pipelines in Fabric using Data Factory.
Notebooks vs Pipelines

Notebooks contain transformation logic — the PySpark code that reads, filters, enriches, and writes data. Pipelines contain orchestration logic — the sequence of activities, the schedule, the dependencies, and the error handling. A notebook answers the question 'how do I transform this data?' A pipeline answers the question 'when does the transformation run, what runs before it, and what happens if it fails?' NordGrid's Silver transformation notebook (nb_transform_meters) contains the PySpark code from Module 6. NordGrid's ingestion pipeline (pl_ingest_meters) contains the schedule and sequence that runs that notebook every night.

left.label: Notebook
points: Contains PySpark/SQL transformation logic
Runs interactively (cell by cell) or as a scheduled job
Processes data: reads, transforms, writes
Developed in a code editor with cells
NordGrid: nb_transform_meters

right.label: Pipeline
points: Contains orchestration logic: sequence, schedule, error handling
Runs on a schedule or on demand, executing activities in sequence
Coordinates work: copies data, runs notebooks, triggers dataflows
Developed on a visual canvas with drag-and-drop activities
NordGrid: pl_ingest_meters

blocks.content: The Assembly Line Analogy

Think of NordGrid's data pipeline like a factory assembly line. The assembly line has stations: Station 1 receives raw materials (the Copy activity downloads the CSV from the SFTP server). Station 2 processes the materials (the Notebook activity runs the Silver transformation). Station 3 packages the product (another Notebook activity computes Gold aggregates). The assembly line itself does not perform any processing — each station does. The assembly line's job is to move the product from station to station in the right order, at the right time, and to stop the line if a station fails. A Fabric pipeline works the same way: it does not transform data itself, but it orchestrates the activities that do.
What a Pipeline Contains

A Fabric pipeline contains three things: activities, connections between activities, and a schedule. Activities are the individual steps — copy data, run a notebook, execute a dataflow, send a notification. Connections define the order and conditions — run the notebook after the copy succeeds, send a notification if the copy fails. The schedule defines when the pipeline runs — daily at 02:00 UTC, every hour, or on demand. Together, these three elements turn manual, ad-hoc notebook execution into automated, reliable, monitored production workflows.

blocks.title: Pipelines make notebooks production-ready
blocks.content: A notebook without a pipeline is a development tool — useful for exploration and prototyping, but dependent on a human to run it. A notebook scheduled through a pipeline is a production component — it runs automatically, handles failures, and produces results that downstream consumers depend on. The transition from 'notebook that works when I run it' to 'pipeline that runs it reliably every night' is the transition from development to production. This module teaches that transition.

Fabric pipelines are built on a visual canvas — a drag-and-drop design surface where you place activities, connect them with arrows, and configure their properties. The canvas is the primary interface for pipeline development, and it is fundamentally different from the code-based notebook editor. Instead of writing Python, you select activities from a palette, draw connections between them, and configure settings in property panels. This visual approach makes pipeline logic visible at a glance — you can see the entire flow, the branching conditions, and the dependencies without reading code.
Opening the Canvas

To create a pipeline, navigate to the workspace (for NordGrid, ws_nordgrid_bronze for ingestion pipelines), click New, and select Data pipeline. Fabric prompts for a name — NordGrid uses the pl_ prefix convention: pl_ingest_meters. After naming, the pipeline canvas opens. The canvas has three zones: the activity palette on the left (listing all available activity types), the design surface in the center (where you drag activities and draw connections), and the properties panel at the bottom (where you configure the selected activity's settings).

blocks.title: The pipeline canvas layout
blocks.caption: The pipeline canvas has three zones: the activity palette on the left, the design surface in the center where activities are connected visually, and the properties panel at the bottom where the selected activity's configuration is displayed.

blocks.content: Working on the Canvas

To add an activity, drag it from the palette onto the design surface. To connect two activities, hover over the first activity until a small arrow appears on its right edge, then drag that arrow to the second activity. The connection arrow represents a dependency: the second activity runs after the first, subject to a condition (success, failure, completion, or skip). To configure an activity, click it on the canvas and edit its properties in the panel below. The canvas auto-saves as you work, but NordGrid's engineers manually save (Ctrl+S) after significant changes as a habit.
Canvas vs Code

If you have used Apache Airflow, you know that Airflow pipelines are defined in Python code — DAGs are Python scripts. Fabric pipelines are defined visually on the canvas. This difference reflects a design philosophy: Airflow prioritizes code-first orchestration for engineers who think in code, while Fabric prioritizes visual orchestration that is accessible to both engineers and less technical users. NordGrid's data engineers were initially skeptical of the visual approach but found that the canvas makes pipeline logic more reviewable — in a code review, a screenshot of the pipeline canvas communicates the flow faster than reading 200 lines of Airflow DAG code.

blocks.title: The canvas is for orchestration, not transformation
blocks.content: The pipeline canvas is where you define what runs and in what order. It is not where you write transformation logic — that belongs in notebooks and dataflows. A common mistake is trying to embed complex data manipulation directly in pipeline expressions. If you find yourself writing multi-line expressions in the pipeline properties panel, the logic probably belongs in a notebook that the pipeline calls. Keep the pipeline simple: copy, run notebook, handle errors. Keep the notebook powerful: read, transform, write.

The Copy activity is the most common starting point for a data pipeline. It moves data from an external source into a Fabric Lakehouse — or between Fabric items — without transformation. The data arrives at the destination exactly as it existed at the source: same format, same content, same encoding. NordGrid's daily pipeline begins with a Copy activity that downloads the meter reading CSV from the external SFTP server and places it in the Bronze Lakehouse's Files section. This lesson covers how the Copy activity works, how to configure its source and destination, and the connection concept that makes external access possible.
Source and Destination

Every Copy activity has two sides: a source (where data comes from) and a destination (where data goes). The source can be an external system — an SFTP server, an Azure SQL database, a REST API, an Amazon S3 bucket, or dozens of other connectors. The destination is typically a Fabric Lakehouse (Files section for raw data, Tables section for structured data) or a Fabric Warehouse. NordGrid's Copy activity reads from the SFTP server at sftp.nordgrid-vendor.eu and writes to lh_meter_readings/Files/meter_exports/. The configuration specifies the exact file path on the SFTP server and the exact folder path in the Lakehouse.
Connections

To access an external system, the Copy activity needs a connection — a saved credential that tells Fabric how to authenticate with the source. NordGrid's SFTP connection (named conn_sftp_meters) stores the server hostname, port, username, and SSH key. The connection is created once and reused by every pipeline that reads from the same SFTP server. Connections are managed in the Fabric portal under the workspace settings or the Manage connections section, and they are shared across pipelines within the same workspace.

Connections are the security boundary between Fabric and external systems. The connection stores the credential — the pipeline references the connection by name. This means individual pipeline authors never see the actual password or SSH key. NordGrid's platform team creates and manages connections; data engineers reference them in their pipelines. If the SFTP password changes, the platform team updates the connection once, and every pipeline that uses it continues to work without modification.
Configuring the Copy

rows: Determines which connection type and file format options are available
The saved credential for authenticating with the SFTP server
The exact file to download from the external server
Determines where the data lands in Fabric
The Bronze Lakehouse that receives raw data
The folder structure within the Lakehouse's Files section
The name of the output file in the destination folder

blocks.content: Dynamic Paths

The destination folder path in the table above uses curly-brace placeholders ({year}/{month}/{day}/) that look like variables — and they are. Fabric pipeline expressions let you compute folder paths dynamically based on the current date, pipeline parameters, or activity outputs. NordGrid's Copy activity uses the expression @concat('meter_exports/', formatDateTime(utcNow(), 'yyyy'), '/', formatDateTime(utcNow(), 'MM'), '/', formatDateTime(utcNow(), 'dd'), '/') to generate today's date-partitioned folder path automatically. Lesson 8 covers pipeline expressions in detail.

blocks.title: The Copy activity does not transform
blocks.content: The Copy activity moves data exactly as-is. It does not filter rows, rename columns, change types, or modify the file in any way. If the source CSV has a header row, the destination CSV has the same header row. If the source has encoding issues, the destination has the same encoding issues. Transformation is the notebook's job. The Copy activity's job is to get the raw file into OneLake reliably. Do not try to add transformation logic to the Copy activity — use a notebook or dataflow instead.

After the Copy activity delivers raw data to the Bronze Lakehouse, the next step is transformation — and the Notebook activity is how a pipeline triggers it. The Notebook activity runs a Fabric notebook as a pipeline step, passing parameters, waiting for completion, and reporting the result (success or failure) back to the pipeline. This is the mechanism that turns the interactive transformation notebook from Module 6 into an automated production job. NordGrid's pipeline runs nb_transform_meters as a Notebook activity immediately after the Bronze Copy activity succeeds.
Configuring the Notebook Activity

To add a Notebook activity, drag it from the palette onto the canvas, then configure it in the properties panel. The key settings are the notebook reference (which notebook to run), the Lakehouse reference (which Lakehouse to attach), and the parameter overrides (values to pass to the notebook's parameter cell). NordGrid's Notebook activity references nb_transform_meters in ws_nordgrid_silver, attaches lh_meter_readings as the default Lakehouse, and passes the processing_date parameter with today's date.

rows: Which Lakehouse to attach as default
Key-value pairs that override the notebook's parameter cell
Maximum time the activity waits before failing
How many times to retry if the notebook fails

blocks.content: Parameter Passing

The parameter override is where the pipeline meets the notebook. In Module 5, you learned that notebooks have a parameter cell with default values. The Notebook activity overrides those defaults at runtime. NordGrid's pipeline passes processing_date as an expression: @formatDateTime(utcNow(), 'yyyy-MM-dd'), which evaluates to today's date (for example, '2025-03-23'). When the notebook runs, its processing_date variable contains this pipeline-provided value instead of the hardcoded default. The notebook code does not know or care whether it was run interactively or by a pipeline — it simply uses the parameter value.

The JSON above is a simplified representation of the Notebook activity's configuration. In the portal, you configure this through the properties panel rather than editing JSON directly. The @formatDateTime expression is evaluated by the pipeline at runtime — the notebook receives the resolved string '2025-03-23', not the expression itself. NordGrid's pipeline team uses these expressions to make pipelines date-aware without hardcoding any values.
What Happens When the Notebook Runs

When the pipeline triggers the Notebook activity, Fabric starts a Spark session (the same cold start from Module 5, taking 30-60 seconds), executes every cell in the notebook from top to bottom, and reports the outcome back to the pipeline. If all cells succeed, the activity reports success and the pipeline continues to the next activity. If any cell throws an exception, the activity reports failure and the pipeline follows the failure path (if one is configured). The notebook's output — print statements, display results, error messages — is captured in the pipeline's monitoring log, where the engineer can review it the next morning.

blocks.title: Interactive cells are not production cells
blocks.content: When a pipeline runs a notebook, it executes every cell from top to bottom, including cells you may have added for interactive debugging — exploratory show() calls, temporary schema inspections, or test filters. These extra cells consume time and compute but produce no production value. NordGrid's convention: before scheduling a notebook in a pipeline, remove or comment out all interactive debugging cells. Keep only the cells that the production pipeline needs: parameters, imports, read, transform, write, validate.

Not every transformation needs a Spark notebook. Some transformations are simple enough that a low-code tool is faster to build, easier to maintain, and less expensive to run. The Dataflow activity runs a Dataflow Gen2 (the Power Query-based transformation tool from Module 8) as a pipeline step. NordGrid uses Dataflow activities for simple data movement tasks — refreshing reference tables, updating dimension data, and loading small lookup datasets — while reserving Notebook activities for complex transformations that require PySpark logic.
When to Use Dataflow vs Notebook

rows: Simple: filter, rename, type conversion, merge
Complex: custom logic, loops, UDFs, multi-step chains
Any volume — Spark scales to billions of rows
Faster for complex logic (code is more expressive)
Non-engineers can read and modify Power Query steps
Reference table refresh, dimension table loads

blocks.content: Configuring the Dataflow Activity

The Dataflow activity's configuration is simpler than the Notebook activity because Dataflow Gen2 items are self-contained — the source connection, transformation logic, and destination are all defined inside the dataflow itself. The pipeline only needs to know which dataflow to run. NordGrid's pipeline pl_refresh_reference_data contains a Dataflow activity that triggers df_refresh_substations, which connects to the external substation database, applies filtering and type corrections, and writes the result to the Silver Lakehouse's substations table.

Unlike the Notebook activity, the Dataflow activity does not support parameter passing. If you need the dataflow to process different data based on a runtime value (like a date), you must either build the logic into the dataflow itself or use a Notebook activity instead. This limitation is one of the reasons NordGrid uses dataflows only for static reference data (which does not change based on date) and notebooks for time-series data (which processes a specific day's file based on the pipeline's date parameter).
Mixing Activities in a Pipeline

A single pipeline can contain both Notebook activities and Dataflow activities. NordGrid's comprehensive daily pipeline has three activities: a Copy activity that downloads the meter CSV, a Notebook activity that transforms it into the Silver table, and a Dataflow activity that refreshes the substation reference table. The three activities run in a specific order defined by the connections between them. The Copy and Dataflow activities run in parallel (they have no dependency on each other), and the Notebook activity runs after the Copy succeeds (because it needs the Bronze file to exist before it can read it).

blocks.title: NordGrid's mixed-activity pipeline
blocks.content:
┌────────────────────┐
              ┌────▶│ Notebook Activity    │
              │     │ nb_transform_meters  │
              │     │ (runs after Copy)    │
┌──────────┐  │     └────────────────────┘
│  Copy     │──┤
│ Activity  │  │
│ (SFTP→LH) │  │     ┌────────────────────┐
└──────────┘  └────▶│  (next activities    │
                     │   in pipeline...)    │
                     └────────────────────┘

┌──────────────────┐
│ Dataflow Activity │   ← Runs in parallel with Copy
│ df_refresh_subs   │     (no dependency between them)
└──────────────────┘
blocks.caption: The Copy and Dataflow activities have no dependency on each other, so they run in parallel. The Notebook activity depends on the Copy activity (it needs the Bronze file), so it runs after the Copy succeeds. This parallel execution reduces the total pipeline runtime.

blocks.title: Start simple, add activities as needed
blocks.content: NordGrid's first pipeline had only two activities: Copy and Notebook. The Dataflow activity was added three months later when the team needed automated reference table refresh. Pipelines are easy to extend — adding a new activity to an existing pipeline takes minutes. Start with the minimum set of activities that solves the immediate problem, and add more as needs emerge. A pipeline with two well-configured activities is better than a pipeline with eight activities where three are untested.

Activities on the pipeline canvas do not run in isolation — they are connected by dependency arrows that define the execution order and the conditions under which each activity runs. A dependency arrow says: 'run this activity after the previous one, but only if the previous one finished with this result.' The result can be success, failure, completion (regardless of outcome), or skip. These dependencies are what turn a collection of independent activities into a coordinated workflow. This lesson covers how dependencies work and how NordGrid uses them to handle both the normal path and the failure path.
The Four Dependency Conditions

rows: Only if the previous activity succeeded
Run Notebook after Copy succeeds — the Bronze file must exist
Only if the previous activity failed
Send failure notification if Copy fails — alert the on-call engineer
After the previous activity finishes, regardless of outcome
Run a cleanup activity that always runs, even after failures
Only if the previous activity was skipped
Rarely used — handles conditional skip logic in complex pipelines

blocks.content: Sequential Execution

The simplest dependency pattern is sequential: Activity A must succeed before Activity B starts. NordGrid's pipeline uses this pattern for the Copy-to-Notebook dependency. The Notebook activity (nb_transform_meters) is connected to the Copy activity with a green success arrow. If the Copy succeeds, the Notebook runs. If the Copy fails, the Notebook does not run — there is no point transforming data that was not copied. This sequential pattern ensures that each activity's preconditions are met before it executes.
Parallel Execution

When two activities have no dependency on each other, they run in parallel. In NordGrid's pipeline, the Copy activity (downloading meter data) and the Dataflow activity (refreshing substation reference data) have no connection between them — the reference data does not depend on today's meter file, and the meter file does not depend on the reference data. Fabric starts both activities simultaneously, which reduces the total pipeline runtime. If the Copy takes 4 minutes and the Dataflow takes 2 minutes, both are complete after 4 minutes — not 6.
Failure Handling

Failure dependencies define what happens when something goes wrong. NordGrid's Copy activity has a red failure arrow connected to a Web activity that sends a notification to the team's Slack channel. If the SFTP server is unreachable, the file is missing, or the network times out, the Copy activity fails and the notification fires. The Notebook activity does not run (it depends on success), and the team receives an alert. This pattern — success path for normal flow, failure path for error handling — is the foundation of reliable pipeline design.

blocks.title: NordGrid's pipeline with success and failure paths
blocks.content:
Success (green)
┌──────────┐  ──────────────────────────▶ ┌──────────────────┐
│  Copy     │                               │ Notebook          │
│ Activity  │                               │ nb_transform_     │
│           │                               │ meters            │
└──────────┘                               └──────────────────┘
      │
      │ Failure (red)
      ▼
┌──────────────────┐
│ Web Activity      │
│ Send Slack alert  │
│ "Copy failed for  │
│  {processing_date}│
│  Error: {error}"  │
└──────────────────┘
blocks.caption: The pipeline branches based on the Copy activity's outcome. On success, the Notebook runs and transforms the data. On failure, a Web activity sends a Slack notification. The Notebook never runs on failure — there is no data to transform.

blocks.title: Always add a failure path
blocks.content: A pipeline without a failure path is a pipeline that fails silently. If the Copy activity fails and no failure dependency exists, the pipeline simply stops — no notification, no alert, no record that something went wrong (beyond the monitoring hub, which requires someone to check it). NordGrid's policy requires every production pipeline to have at least one failure notification activity connected to the first activity in the pipeline. Silent failures are the most expensive failures because they are discovered latest.

A pipeline that runs only when someone clicks the Run button is not an automated pipeline — it is a manual workflow with a nicer interface. The real value of pipelines comes from scheduling: configuring the pipeline to run automatically at a specific time, on a specific frequency, without human intervention. NordGrid's daily pipeline runs at 02:00 UTC every day, 365 days a year. No one clicks a button. No one sets an alarm. The schedule ensures that by the time the operations team opens their dashboard at 08:00, the data is current. This lesson covers how to configure a schedule, what options are available, and the practical considerations that determine NordGrid's scheduling decisions.
Creating a Schedule

To schedule a pipeline, open it on the canvas, click the Schedule button in the toolbar, and configure the trigger. Fabric supports time-based triggers: you specify a start date, end date (optional), time zone, and recurrence pattern. The recurrence pattern can be as simple as 'daily at 02:00' or as complex as 'every 4 hours on weekdays only.' NordGrid's meter pipeline uses the simplest pattern: once daily at 02:00 UTC, with no end date (the pipeline runs indefinitely until the schedule is disabled).
Time Zone Considerations

Fabric schedules use time zones, and getting the time zone right is important because NordGrid's SFTP vendor generates the daily export at 01:30 UTC. If NordGrid scheduled the pipeline for 02:00 in the CET time zone (Central European Time) instead of UTC, the actual execution time would shift with daylight saving — running at 01:00 UTC in summer (before the file is ready) and 02:00 UTC in winter. NordGrid schedules all pipelines in UTC to avoid this problem. The rule is simple: if your data source operates on UTC, schedule your pipeline on UTC.
Scheduling Patterns

rows: Once per day at a fixed time
Batch pipelines that process one day's data
Near-real-time ingestion of frequently updated data
Once per week on a specific day
No schedule — triggered manually or by another pipeline

blocks.content: What Happens at Trigger Time

When the scheduled time arrives, Fabric creates a pipeline run — an instance of the pipeline that executes with the trigger time's context. The run appears in the monitoring hub with a start time, a status (in progress, succeeded, failed), and a duration. If the pipeline is still running from a previous trigger (for example, yesterday's run is delayed), Fabric's behavior depends on the concurrency setting. The default is to queue the new run until the previous one finishes. NordGrid uses this default because running two instances of the transformation pipeline simultaneously could produce corrupted Silver data (both instances writing to the same table).

NordGrid's scheduling decision was driven by two constraints: the SFTP file lands at 01:30 UTC (so the pipeline must start after that), and the operations dashboard is viewed at 08:00 UTC (so the pipeline must finish before that). The pipeline's total runtime is approximately 17 minutes (4 minutes for Copy, 13 minutes for the Notebook including cold start). Scheduling at 02:00 UTC provides a 30-minute buffer after the file lands and completes well before the 08:00 dashboard review.

blocks.title: Schedule with margins
blocks.content: Do not schedule a pipeline to start the moment the source data is expected. Sources are late. Networks are slow. SFTP servers need restarts. NordGrid's SFTP file is usually ready at 01:30 UTC, but twice in the past year it was late — arriving at 01:55 and 01:47. The 02:00 UTC schedule provides a 30-minute margin that absorbed both delays without intervention. If the pipeline had been scheduled at 01:35, both delays would have caused failures, overnight pages, and morning scrambles.

A pipeline that only works for one specific date or one specific source is a pipeline that requires editing every time the input changes. Pipeline parameters and expressions solve this by making pipelines dynamic — able to compute file paths, dates, and configuration values at runtime. NordGrid's pipeline does not hardcode '2025-03-23' as the processing date. Instead, it uses an expression that evaluates to today's date, making the same pipeline work for every day without modification. This lesson covers pipeline parameters, the expression language, and the most common expression patterns.
Pipeline Parameters

A pipeline parameter is a variable that can be set when the pipeline runs. Parameters are defined in the pipeline's Parameters tab and have a name, a type (string, int, bool), and a default value. NordGrid's pipeline defines one parameter: processing_date with a default of today's date. When the pipeline runs on schedule, it uses the default. When an engineer needs to reprocess a specific date, they trigger the pipeline manually and override the processing_date parameter with the target date. The parameter value flows into every activity that references it.
The Expression Language

Fabric pipeline expressions use the @ prefix and a set of built-in functions. Expressions are evaluated at runtime and can appear in any activity configuration field — file paths, parameter values, connection strings, and conditions. The expression language is not PySpark — it is a separate language specific to Data Factory pipelines. The most commonly used functions are date formatting, string concatenation, and parameter references.

The expressions above demonstrate the most common patterns. utcNow() returns the current UTC datetime. formatDateTime formats it into a string. concat joins strings together. adddays adds or subtracts days. pipeline().parameters.processing_date references the pipeline's processing_date parameter. NordGrid uses these expressions to build dynamic file paths, compute date ranges, and pass context to notebook parameters. The expression language is small — perhaps 30 functions — but it covers the vast majority of pipeline configuration needs.
Connecting Parameters to Activities

Pipeline parameters flow into activities through expression references. The Copy activity's destination folder path uses @concat('meter_exports/', pipeline().parameters.processing_date, '/') to write files to a date-specific folder. The Notebook activity's parameter override uses @pipeline().parameters.processing_date to pass the date to the notebook. Both activities reference the same pipeline parameter, ensuring consistency — the file is always written to the same date folder that the notebook processes.

blocks.title: Parameter flow from pipeline to activities
blocks.content:
Pipeline Parameter: processing_date = '2025-03-23'
         │
         ├──▶ Copy Activity
         │     Destination: meter_exports/@{processing_date}/
         │     → writes to: meter_exports/2025-03-23/
         │
         └──▶ Notebook Activity
               Base parameters: processing_date = @{processing_date}
               → notebook receives: processing_date = '2025-03-23'
               → reads from: meter_exports/2025/03/23/
blocks.caption: One pipeline parameter flows into multiple activities. The Copy activity uses it to determine the destination folder. The Notebook activity passes it to the notebook as a runtime parameter. Both activities stay synchronized because they reference the same source of truth.

blocks.title: Test expressions before scheduling
blocks.content: Pipeline expressions are evaluated at runtime, not at design time. A typo in an expression (missing parenthesis, wrong function name) will not be caught until the pipeline runs — and then it fails with a cryptic error message. NordGrid's practice is to test every expression by running the pipeline manually once before enabling the schedule. The manual run validates that all expressions resolve correctly, all paths are generated properly, and all parameter references work.

A scheduled pipeline that runs overnight is only useful if someone checks whether it succeeded. The monitoring hub is the Fabric portal's centralized view of all pipeline runs, notebook runs, dataflow refreshes, and other activity executions. NordGrid's data engineers start every workday by opening the monitoring hub and checking the overnight pipeline status. A green checkmark means the pipeline succeeded and the data is current. A red X means something failed and needs investigation. This lesson covers how to use the monitoring hub, what information it provides, and how NordGrid investigates failures.
The Monitoring Hub

The monitoring hub is accessible from the left navigation pane in the Fabric portal. It shows a table of recent runs with columns for the item name, item type, run status, start time, duration, and the user or schedule that triggered the run. You can filter by item type (show only pipeline runs), by status (show only failed runs), and by time range (show runs from the last 24 hours). NordGrid's engineers filter to pipeline runs from the last 24 hours as their default view, which shows the overnight pipeline execution at the top of the list.
Drill-Down — Activity-Level Detail

Clicking a pipeline run opens the activity-level view, which shows each activity's individual status, start time, duration, and output. For NordGrid's pipeline, this view shows the Copy activity (succeeded, 4 minutes), the Dataflow activity (succeeded, 2 minutes), and the Notebook activity (succeeded, 13 minutes). If any activity failed, its row shows a red status with an error message.
