Clicking a failed activity reveals the detailed error — for example, 'SFTP connection timed out after 30 seconds' for a failed Copy, or 'AnalysisException: Table silver_meters already exists with a different schema' for a failed Notebook.

blocks.title: Monitoring hub drill-down from pipeline to activity
blocks.caption: The monitoring hub shows pipeline runs in a summary table. Clicking a run reveals the activity-level detail, showing each activity's status and timing. NordGrid's engineers use this drill-down to identify which specific activity failed when a pipeline reports failure.

blocks.content: Investigating Failures

When a pipeline fails, NordGrid's engineers follow a three-step investigation process. Step one: identify which activity failed by checking the activity-level detail in the monitoring hub. Step two: read the error message for the failed activity — most failures have clear messages ('connection timed out', 'file not found', 'schema mismatch'). Step three: check whether the failure is transient (network timeout, temporary service outage) or persistent (source file missing, schema change). Transient failures often resolve on retry. Persistent failures require investigation of the source system or the transformation logic.

For Notebook activity failures, the monitoring hub includes a link to the notebook's Spark log, which shows the full stack trace and the specific cell that failed. NordGrid's engineers click this link to see the Python exception, the line number, and the data state at the time of failure. For Copy activity failures, the error message typically includes the HTTP status code and the source system's error response. For Dataflow failures, the error details show which Power Query step failed and why.

blocks.title: Monitoring is a daily habit, not a reaction to problems
blocks.content: NordGrid's engineers check the monitoring hub every morning, even when there are no alerts. A successful pipeline with an unusually long duration (25 minutes instead of the typical 17) might indicate an emerging problem — perhaps the data volume is growing, or the Spark cluster is under contention from another workload. Catching these trends early, before they become failures, is the difference between proactive operations and firefighting.

The previous nine lessons covered what pipelines are, how the canvas works, the Copy, Notebook, and Dataflow activities, dependency conditions, scheduling, parameters, and monitoring. This lesson assembles everything into NordGrid's complete daily pipeline — the same pipeline that runs every night at 02:00 UTC, moving data from the external SFTP server through Bronze and Silver to Gold, and alerting the team if anything fails. By the end of this lesson, you will understand every activity, every connection, and every parameter in a real production pipeline.
The Complete Pipeline

blocks.title: NordGrid's complete daily pipeline: pl_ingest_meters
blocks.content:
Schedule: Daily at 02:00 UTC
Parameter: processing_date = @formatDateTime(utcNow(), 'yyyy-MM-dd')

┌──────────────────────┐         ┌──────────────────────┐
│ Copy Activity         │ Success │ Notebook Activity     │
│ 'Copy Meter CSV'      │────────▶│ 'Silver Transform'    │
│                       │         │                       │
│ Source: SFTP server   │         │ Notebook: nb_transform│
│ Dest: Bronze LH/Files │         │ _meters               │
│ Path: meter_exports/  │         │ Param: processing_date│
│  {date}/readings.csv  │         │                       │
└───────────┬──────────┘         └───────────┬──────────┘
            │                                 │
            │ Failure                          │ Success
            ▼                                 ▼
┌──────────────────────┐         ┌──────────────────────┐
│ Web Activity          │         │ Notebook Activity     │
│ 'Alert: Copy Failed'  │         │ 'Gold Aggregation'    │
│                       │         │                       │
│ Slack webhook:        │         │ Notebook: nb_aggregate│
│ "Pipeline failed for  │         │ _gold                 │
│  {processing_date}"   │         │ Param: processing_date│
└──────────────────────┘         └──────────────────────┘
                                              │
┌──────────────────────┐                      │ Success
│ Dataflow Activity     │                      ▼
│ 'Refresh Substations' │         ┌──────────────────────┐
│                       │         │ Web Activity          │
│ Dataflow: df_refresh_ │         │ 'Alert: Pipeline OK'  │
│ substations           │         │                       │
│ (parallel, no deps)   │         │ Slack: "Daily pipeline │
└──────────────────────┘         │  completed for {date}"│
                                  └──────────────────────┘
blocks.caption: The complete pipeline has five activities. The Copy and Dataflow run in parallel. The Silver Notebook runs after Copy succeeds. The Gold Notebook runs after Silver succeeds. A failure alert fires if Copy fails. A success alert fires after Gold succeeds. One parameter (processing_date) flows through the entire chain.

blocks.content: The Execution Timeline

rows: Downloads readings.csv from SFTP to Bronze Lakehouse
Refreshes substation reference table in Silver
Reads Bronze CSV, transforms, writes Silver Delta table
Reads Silver table, computes regional summaries, writes Gold
Sends Slack message: 'Daily pipeline completed for 2025-03-23'
All activities succeeded. Data is current.

blocks.content: What the Team Sees at 08:00

When NordGrid's operations team opens the Power BI dashboard at 08:00 UTC, the data they see was ingested at 02:00, transformed at 02:04, aggregated at 02:17, and has been current for nearly six hours. The data engineering team received a Slack message at 02:20 confirming success. No one woke up, no one clicked a button, no one checked a status page. The pipeline ran, succeeded, notified the team, and the data is ready. This is the end state of everything you have learned in Modules 1 through 7: a fully automated, monitored, reliable data pipeline built entirely in Fabric.
The Complete Picture

You now have the full pipeline foundation. Pipelines orchestrate activities in sequence and parallel (lesson 1). The visual canvas provides a drag-and-drop design surface (lesson 2). The Copy activity moves data from external sources (lesson 3). The Notebook activity runs transformation code (lesson 4). The Dataflow activity triggers low-code transformations (lesson 5). Dependencies define execution order and failure handling (lesson 6). Schedules automate execution (lesson 7). Parameters and expressions make pipelines dynamic (lesson 8). The monitoring hub tracks every run (lesson 9). And NordGrid's complete daily pipeline ties everything together (this lesson).

blocks.title: What comes next
blocks.content: Module 8 dives into Dataflows Gen2 — the low-code transformation tool powered by Power Query Online. You saw the Dataflow activity briefly in this module as one step in NordGrid's pipeline. Module 8 teaches you how to build the dataflow itself: connecting to external data sources, applying visual transformations, and writing the results to Lakehouse tables. Dataflows complement notebooks — they handle simple reference table updates while notebooks handle complex transformations.

chapters.title: Dataflows Gen2 — Low-Code Transformations
chapters.description: Understand Dataflows Gen2 as Fabric's low-code transformation tool, learn when to use dataflows versus notebooks, and build NordGrid's reference table refresh pipeline using Power Query Online.

blocks.content: Modules 5 and 6 taught you how to transform data in notebooks using PySpark — writing code cell by cell, chaining DataFrame operations, and producing Delta tables. Notebooks are powerful, flexible, and the right tool for complex transformations. But not every transformation is complex. Refreshing a substation reference table from an external database — reading 200 rows, renaming two columns, and writing the result to a Lakehouse — does not require a Spark cluster, a PySpark notebook, or a 30-second cold start. Dataflow Gen2 is Fabric's low-code alternative: a visual transformation tool powered by Power Query Online that handles simple data movement and reshaping tasks without writing a single line of code.
The Power Query Foundation

Dataflow Gen2 is built on Power Query — the same transformation engine that Excel and Power BI use for data preparation. If you have ever used Get & Transform in Excel, or edited queries in Power BI Desktop, you have used Power Query. The interface is visual: you connect to a data source, see a preview of the data, apply transformation steps by clicking buttons on a ribbon (filter rows, remove columns, change types), and each step is recorded in a list that you can review, reorder, or undo. The underlying language is M (a functional language specific to Power Query), but you rarely need to write M directly — the visual interface generates it for you.
How Dataflows Fit in the Pipeline

Dataflows occupy a specific niche in NordGrid's architecture. They are not replacements for notebooks — they complement them. NordGrid uses notebooks for the heavy-lifting transformations: processing 500,000 meter readings with schema enforcement, multi-step filtering, deduplication, and partitioned Delta writes. NordGrid uses dataflows for the lightweight tasks: refreshing the 200-row substation reference table from an external SQL database, loading the region lookup table from a SharePoint list, and importing the customer classification spreadsheet from a shared drive. These tasks are too simple to justify a Spark notebook and too important to do manually.

blocks.title: Where dataflows fit in NordGrid's tool selection
blocks.content:
Transformation Complexity
│
│  HIGH    ┌─────────────────────────────────┐
│          │  NOTEBOOKS (PySpark)             │
│          │  • Meter reading transformation  │
│          │  • SCADA telemetry processing    │
│          │  • Complex joins and aggregations│
│          │  • Custom validation logic       │
│          └─────────────────────────────────┘
│
│  MEDIUM  ┌─────────────────────────────────┐
│          │  DATAFLOWS GEN2 (Power Query)    │
│          │  • Reference table refresh       │
│          │  • Dimension table loads         │
│          │  • Simple merge/append operations│
│          │  • Column rename and type fix    │
│          └─────────────────────────────────┘
│
│  LOW     ┌─────────────────────────────────┐
│          │  COPY ACTIVITY (no transform)    │
│          │  • Raw file ingestion from SFTP  │
│          │  • Database table copy           │
│          └─────────────────────────────────┘
│
└──────────────────────────────────────────────▶
                                        Volume
blocks.caption: Each tool handles a different complexity level. Copy activities move data without transformation. Dataflows handle simple transformations visually. Notebooks handle complex transformations with code. Choosing the right tool for the right task keeps the pipeline simple and maintainable.

blocks.content: Dataflows Gen2 vs Original Dataflows

If you have used Power BI dataflows (sometimes called Dataflow Gen1), Dataflow Gen2 is the next generation. The most important difference for data engineers is the destination: Gen1 dataflows write to Power BI's internal storage, while Gen2 dataflows can write directly to Lakehouse tables, Warehouse tables, or Azure SQL databases. This means Gen2 dataflows integrate naturally into the medallion architecture — they write managed Delta tables just like notebooks do. NordGrid uses Gen2 exclusively because Gen1 cannot write to Lakehouses.

blocks.title: The right tool for simple work
blocks.content: Using a Spark notebook to refresh a 200-row reference table is like driving a truck to deliver a letter. It works, but the overhead — 30-second cold start, Spark session, cluster resources — is disproportionate to the task. A Dataflow Gen2 completes the same refresh in 15 seconds with no Spark session, no cluster allocation, and no code to maintain. NordGrid's rule: if the transformation does not need code (no custom logic, no UDFs, no complex joins), use a dataflow. If it needs code, use a notebook.

The Power Query Editor is the development environment for Dataflow Gen2 — the place where you connect to data, apply transformations, and configure the output destination. Unlike the notebook editor (which is code-centric), the Power Query Editor is visual: transformations are applied by clicking buttons on a ribbon, and every action is recorded as a named step in a step list. This lesson introduces the editor's layout, the concept of applied steps, and the workflow NordGrid follows when building a dataflow.
Opening the Editor

To create a Dataflow Gen2, navigate to the workspace, click New, and select Dataflow Gen2. The Power Query Editor opens immediately. The editor has four main areas: the ribbon at the top (transformation commands organized by category — Home, Transform, Add Column, View), the query pane on the left (listing all queries in the dataflow), the data preview in the center (showing a sample of the current query's data), and the applied steps pane on the right (listing every transformation step applied to the current query).

blocks.title: The Power Query Editor layout
blocks.content:
┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  RIBBON: Home | Transform | Add Column | View                │
│  [Filter ▼] [Remove Cols] [Change Type] [Replace Values]     │
├──────────┬──────────────────────────────────┬────────────────┤
│          │                                  │                │
│ QUERIES  │       DATA PREVIEW               │ APPLIED STEPS  │
│          │                                  │                │
│ ○ subs   │ substation_id  name    cap_mw    │ 1. Source      │
│ ○ regions│ SUB-001        Oslo    450       │ 2. Navigation  │
│          │ SUB-002        Bergen  280       │ 3. Filtered    │
│          │ SUB-003        Malmö   520       │ 4. Renamed     │
│          │ ...                              │ 5. Changed Type│
│          │                                  │                │
└──────────┴──────────────────────────────────┴────────────────┘
blocks.caption: The Power Query Editor has four zones: the ribbon for transformation commands, the query list on the left, the data preview in the center, and the applied steps on the right. Every click on the ribbon adds a new step to the applied steps list.

blocks.content: Applied Steps — The Transformation Record

Every transformation you apply — filtering rows, removing columns, changing types, renaming columns — appears as a named step in the applied steps pane. Steps execute in order from top to bottom, and each step transforms the output of the previous step. You can click any step to see the data at that point in the transformation — step 3 shows the data after filtering but before renaming. You can delete a step to undo it, reorder steps by dragging, or insert a step between existing ones. This step-based model is similar to the cell-by-cell development in notebooks, but with visual manipulation instead of code.

NordGrid's data engineers appreciate the applied steps model because it makes dataflow logic transparent. When a colleague reviews a dataflow, they read the step list from top to bottom: Source (connected to the substation database), Navigation (selected the substations table), Filtered Rows (removed inactive substations), Renamed Columns (changed sub_id to substation_id), Changed Type (cast capacity to decimal). Each step is self-documenting — its name describes what it does. This transparency is harder to achieve in a notebook, where transformation logic can be spread across multiple cells with intermediate variables.
The Data Preview

The data preview shows a sample of the current query's data at the currently selected step. The preview updates automatically when you apply a transformation — filter out inactive substations, and the preview immediately shows only active ones. The preview typically shows the first 1,000 rows, which is sufficient for verifying that transformations work correctly. For NordGrid's 200-row substation table, the preview shows the complete dataset. The preview is read-only — it does not modify the source data. Changes only take effect when you publish the dataflow and it runs.

blocks.title: Visual transforms are M code underneath
blocks.content: Every visual transformation generates M language code behind the scenes. You can see this code by switching to the Advanced Editor (View → Advanced Editor). NordGrid's data engineers occasionally use the Advanced Editor to write M expressions that are difficult to express through the visual interface — such as custom date parsing or conditional column creation. But for most reference table work, the visual interface is sufficient, and NordGrid's convention is to use the visual tools whenever possible for readability.

Before you can transform data in a dataflow, you must connect to it. Dataflow Gen2 supports a wide range of data sources: Lakehouse tables, Azure SQL databases, SharePoint lists, web APIs, Excel files, CSV files, and dozens of other connectors. The connection process follows a consistent pattern: select the source type, provide connection details (server, database, credentials), navigate to the specific table or file, and the data appears in the preview. This lesson covers the connection workflow and the sources NordGrid uses for its reference data.
The Get Data Workflow

To connect to a data source, click Get Data on the Home ribbon. A connector gallery appears, showing all available source types organized by category: Fabric sources (Lakehouse, Warehouse, Datamart), Azure sources (Azure SQL, Blob Storage, Cosmos DB), database sources (SQL Server, PostgreSQL, MySQL), and many others. Select the source type, and a connection dialog prompts for the required details. For an Azure SQL database, you provide the server name, database name, and authentication method. For a Lakehouse, you select the workspace and Lakehouse. After connecting, a navigator shows the available tables, views, or files. Select the ones you need, and the data loads into the query preview.
NordGrid's Reference Data Sources

rows: Region codes, region names, country assignments
Customer types, tariff categories, billing codes
Weekly (classifications updated by the billing team)
Used for merge operations, not as a primary source

blocks.content: Authentication

Each external source requires authentication — the credential that proves the dataflow is authorized to read the data. Dataflow Gen2 supports several authentication methods: organizational account (your Microsoft Entra ID identity), basic authentication (username and password), and anonymous access (for public APIs). NordGrid's Azure SQL database uses organizational account authentication — the dataflow authenticates with the platform team's service principal, which has read-only access to the substation database. The credential is stored securely in Fabric and reused every time the dataflow runs.

The authentication model in dataflows differs from notebooks. In a notebook, the Spark session authenticates with your personal Fabric identity automatically. In a dataflow, you must configure the authentication for each external connection explicitly. NordGrid's platform team configures the connections once during initial dataflow creation, and the stored credentials are reused on every subsequent run. If a credential expires or a password changes, the platform team updates it in one place — the dataflow's connection settings — and all dependent dataflows continue to work.
Fabric Sources — The Simple Case

Connecting to data already in Fabric — a Lakehouse table or a Warehouse table — is the simplest case. No external credentials are needed because the dataflow uses your Fabric identity. You select the Fabric Lakehouse connector, browse to the workspace and Lakehouse, select the table, and the data appears in the preview. NordGrid uses this pattern when a dataflow needs to merge reference data with existing Lakehouse tables — for example, enriching the substation reference with Silver-layer meter counts by joining the external substation data with the Silver Lakehouse's meter table.

blocks.title: Test connections before building transformations
blocks.content: The most common dataflow failure is a broken connection — the external database moved, the credential expired, or the table was renamed. NordGrid's convention: after adding a new data source, verify that the preview shows the expected data before applying any transformations. Building ten transformation steps on top of a connection that fails at runtime wastes development time and produces a dataflow that breaks on its first scheduled run.

The Power Query Editor provides dozens of transformation operations through the ribbon and context menus. For NordGrid's reference table work, a small subset covers the vast majority of needs: filtering rows, removing columns, renaming columns, changing data types, replacing values, and splitting columns. Each operation is a single click (or a few clicks in a dialog), and each adds a step to the applied steps list. This lesson covers the transformations NordGrid uses most frequently, applied to the substation reference table as a running example.
Filtering Rows

Filtering removes rows that do not meet a condition. In the Power Query Editor, click the dropdown arrow on a column header and select a filter condition — equals, does not equal, greater than, contains, or a custom filter. NordGrid's substation dataflow filters out inactive substations by clicking the dropdown on the status column, unchecking 'INACTIVE', and clicking OK. The preview immediately updates to show only active substations, and a 'Filtered Rows' step appears in the applied steps pane. This is the visual equivalent of PySpark's filter(col('status') != 'INACTIVE').
Removing and Renaming Columns

The external substation database has columns that NordGrid's Silver table does not need — internal audit fields, legacy codes, and vendor-specific metadata. Removing them is a right-click operation: select the columns you want to remove, right-click, and choose Remove Columns. Alternatively, select the columns you want to keep, right-click, and choose Remove Other Columns. Renaming follows the same pattern: double-click the column header and type the new name. NordGrid renames sub_id to substation_id and cap_mw to capacity_mw to match the Silver schema convention.
Changing Data Types

Data types in the source may not match what the Lakehouse expects. The substation database stores capacity_mw as an integer (450 MW), but NordGrid's Silver schema expects a decimal (450.0 MW) for consistency with other numeric columns. In Power Query, click the type icon next to the column header (it shows ABC for text, 123 for numbers) and select the target type. A 'Changed Type' step appears in the applied steps. This is the visual equivalent of PySpark's cast(DoubleType()).
Replacing Values

The substation database uses 'N', 'S', 'E', 'W' as region codes, but NordGrid's Silver tables use 'NORTH', 'SOUTH', 'EAST', 'WEST'. The Replace Values transformation handles this: select the column, click Replace Values on the Transform ribbon, enter the old value ('N') and the new value ('NORTH'), and click OK. Repeat for each region code. Each replacement adds a step. This is more tedious than a PySpark mapping function, but for four values in a 200-row table, the manual approach takes thirty seconds and is transparently documented in the step list.

rows: Click type icon → select new type

blocks.title: Steps are reversible
blocks.content: Every transformation in Power Query is a step that can be deleted to undo it, or reordered to change the sequence. If you rename a column and then realize you renamed the wrong one, click the step in the applied steps pane and delete it — the column reverts to its original name. This undo capability is one of the advantages of the visual step model over notebook code, where undoing a transformation requires rerunning previous cells. NordGrid's engineers appreciate this when experimenting with different transformation sequences during dataflow development.

Many reference table workflows require combining data from multiple sources. NordGrid's enriched substation table combines data from the external substation database (substation IDs, names, capacities) with the region lookup spreadsheet (region codes, region names, country assignments). In PySpark, this would be a join. In Power Query, the equivalent operation is Merge Queries — a visual join that combines two queries based on matching columns. This lesson covers merge operations (joins) and append operations (unions) in the Power Query Editor.
Merge Queries — The Visual Join

Merge Queries combines rows from two queries based on matching column values — exactly like a SQL JOIN or a PySpark join. To perform a merge, click Merge Queries on the Home ribbon. A dialog appears where you select the second query, choose the matching columns from each query, and select the join type (inner, left outer, right outer, full outer, anti, cross). NordGrid's substation dataflow merges the substation query with the region lookup query on the region column, using a left outer join to keep all substations even if the region lookup is missing an entry.

After the merge, the matched columns from the second query appear as an expandable column in the data preview. You click the expand icon to select which columns to include — NordGrid selects region_name and country from the region lookup, ignoring the region_code column (which is already present in the substation data). The expand operation adds the selected columns to each row, producing the enriched substation table with both the original substation data and the region details.
Join Types in Power Query

rows: All rows from the first query, matching rows from the second
Substation + region: keep all substations even without region match
All rows from the second query, matching rows from the first
Rarely used — swap query order and use Left Outer instead
All rows from both queries, nulls where no match
Reconciliation: compare two versions of the same reference table
Only rows that match in both queries
Filtering: keep only substations in active regions
Rows from the first query that have NO match in the second
Finding substations missing from the region lookup
Rows from the second query that have NO match in the first
Finding region entries with no associated substations

blocks.content: Append Queries — The Visual Union

Append Queries stacks rows from two or more queries vertically — exactly like a SQL UNION ALL or a PySpark unionByName. Both queries must have the same column structure (or at least overlapping columns — Power Query fills missing columns with null). NordGrid uses append when combining reference data from multiple sources into a single table: appending the Northern Europe substation list with the Southern Europe substation list to create the full organization-wide reference table. Append is simpler than merge — there are no matching columns, no join types, just 'stack these rows on top of those rows.'

left.label: Merge Queries (Join)
points: Combines columns from two queries based on matching values
Result has more columns (added from the second query)
Requires a matching column and a join type
PySpark equivalent: df1.join(df2, on='key')

right.label: Append Queries (Union)
points: Combines rows from two queries by stacking vertically
Result has more rows (added from the second query)
Requires matching column names (or uses null for missing)
PySpark equivalent: df1.unionByName(df2)

blocks.title: Expand columns after merge
blocks.content: After a Merge Queries operation, the matched data from the second query appears as a single expandable column — not as individual columns. If you forget to click the expand icon and select the columns you need, the merged data stays hidden inside that expandable column and will not appear in the output. NordGrid's engineers have made this mistake often enough that 'expand the merged column' is a checklist item in their dataflow review process.

A dataflow that transforms data but writes it nowhere is useless. The data destination is the final step in a Dataflow Gen2 — it determines where the transformed data lands. Unlike Gen1 dataflows (which could only write to Power BI's internal storage), Gen2 dataflows can write directly to Lakehouse tables, Warehouse tables, and Azure SQL databases. This integration with Fabric's storage layer is what makes Gen2 dataflows a genuine data engineering tool rather than just a Power BI feature. This lesson covers how to configure destinations and the options NordGrid uses for its reference table outputs.
Adding a Destination

To configure a destination, select a query in the query pane, then click the destination icon (a small database symbol in the query's footer bar) or select Add data destination from the Home ribbon. A destination dialog opens where you choose the destination type (Lakehouse, Warehouse, or Azure SQL Database), select the specific item (which Lakehouse, which table), and configure the update method. The destination is associated with the query — when the dataflow runs, the query's output is written to the configured destination.
Destination Types

rows: A managed Delta table in the Tables section
Most common destination for data engineering dataflows
A table in a Fabric Warehouse
Use when the table needs to be writable by T-SQL as well
A table in an external Azure SQL Database
Not used by NordGrid (no external SQL write targets)

blocks.content: Update Methods

When configuring a Lakehouse destination, you choose an update method that determines how the dataflow handles existing data. The Replace method drops the existing table and creates a new one with the dataflow's output — equivalent to overwrite mode in PySpark. The Append method adds the dataflow's rows to the existing table without removing old data — equivalent to append mode. NordGrid uses Replace for all reference table dataflows because the dataflow writes the complete, current version of the reference table every time it runs. Append would be appropriate for incremental loads, but NordGrid's reference tables are small enough that a full replace is simpler and avoids deduplication complexity.

left.label: Replace
points: Drops existing table and writes fresh data
Table always reflects the latest dataflow output exactly
No duplicate risk — entire table is replaced
PySpark equivalent: mode('overwrite')
NordGrid use: all reference table refreshes

right.label: Append
points: Adds new rows to existing data
Table grows with each run — may contain old + new data
Duplicate risk if the same rows are loaded twice
PySpark equivalent: mode('append')
NordGrid use: none currently (tables are small enough for replace)

blocks.content: Column Mapping

When the destination table already exists, Fabric checks whether the dataflow's output columns match the table's schema. If the column names match, the mapping is automatic. If they differ, the destination dialog shows a column mapping interface where you map source columns to destination columns. NordGrid's convention is to rename columns in the Power Query transformation steps (before the destination) so that the output columns exactly match the target Lakehouse table schema. This makes the column mapping step trivial — every column maps automatically by name.

blocks.title: Destination creates the table if it does not exist
blocks.content: If you configure a Lakehouse destination and the target table does not yet exist, the dataflow creates it automatically on first run. The table's schema is derived from the dataflow's output columns and types. This means you do not need to pre-create the table in the Lakehouse — the dataflow handles both creation and population. NordGrid's substation reference table was created this way: the dataflow's first run created the table, and subsequent runs replace it with refreshed data.

sections.title: The M Language — A First Look

blocks.content: Every visual transformation in the Power Query Editor generates M language code behind the scenes. M is a functional programming language designed specifically for data transformation. You do not need to learn M to use Dataflow Gen2 — the visual interface handles the vast majority of reference table work. But understanding M at a basic level helps in two situations: debugging dataflows that produce unexpected results (the generated M code sometimes reveals logic the visual interface hides), and writing custom transformations that the visual interface cannot express. This lesson introduces M syntax at a conceptual level — enough to read generated code, not enough to write it from scratch.
The let...in Structure

Every Power Query query is an M expression with a let...in structure. The let block defines a series of named steps (exactly the steps you see in the applied steps pane), and the in block specifies which step's result is the final output. Each step references the previous step by name and applies one transformation. The visual editor generates this structure automatically — every time you click a button on the ribbon, a new line is added to the let block.

Reading this code from top to bottom tells the same story as reading the applied steps pane: connect to the database, navigate to the table, filter inactive rows, remove unnecessary columns, rename columns, and change a type. Each step is a function call that takes the previous step's result as input. Table.SelectRows is the M equivalent of PySpark's filter(). Table.RemoveColumns is the M equivalent of drop(). Table.RenameColumns is the M equivalent of withColumnRenamed(). The M functions are verbose compared to PySpark, but the mapping is direct.
When to Use the Advanced Editor

The Advanced Editor (View → Advanced Editor) shows the full M code for the selected query. NordGrid's data engineers use it in three situations. First, to debug unexpected results: if a filter step removes more rows than expected, reading the M expression reveals the exact condition being applied, which may differ from what the visual interface showed. Second, to write custom expressions: some transformations — like parsing a non-standard date format or computing a hash of multiple columns — are easier to express in M than through the visual interface. Third, to copy transformation logic: when building a new dataflow similar to an existing one, copying M code from the Advanced Editor is faster than recreating every step visually.
M vs PySpark — Not a Competition

M and PySpark are not competing tools — they serve different contexts. M runs inside Power Query, which runs inside Dataflow Gen2, which handles small to medium reference data transformations. PySpark runs inside Spark, which runs inside notebooks, which handles large-scale analytical transformations. NordGrid never processes 500,000 meter readings in M — the Power Query engine is not designed for that volume. And NordGrid never refreshes a 200-row reference table in PySpark — the Spark overhead is disproportionate. Each language is the right choice in its context.

blocks.title: You do not need to master M
blocks.content: At the junior level, reading M code is useful. Writing M code from scratch is not required. The visual interface handles everything NordGrid's reference table dataflows need. If you encounter a situation where the visual interface is insufficient, the mid-level track covers advanced M patterns. For now, the important skill is recognizing that every visual step has an M equivalent, and knowing where to find the code (View → Advanced Editor) when you need to debug or understand what a step is actually doing.

A Dataflow Gen2 can be refreshed in two ways: manually (clicking Refresh Now in the workspace) or on a schedule. Scheduled refresh is the production approach — the dataflow runs automatically at a defined frequency without human intervention. NordGrid schedules its substation reference dataflow to refresh weekly on Monday at 03:00 UTC, and the customer classification dataflow to refresh weekly on Wednesday at 03:00 UTC. This lesson covers both refresh methods and how dataflow refresh relates to pipeline scheduling.
Manual Refresh

To refresh a dataflow manually, find it in the workspace item list, click the three-dot menu, and select Refresh Now. The dataflow connects to its data sources, runs all transformation steps, and writes the results to the configured destinations. The refresh status appears in the workspace and in the monitoring hub. NordGrid's engineers use manual refresh during development (to test that a new dataflow works correctly) and after making changes (to verify that updated transformation steps produce the expected output).
Scheduled Refresh

To schedule a dataflow, open its settings (three-dot menu → Settings), navigate to the Scheduled Refresh section, and toggle the schedule on. Configure the frequency (daily, weekly, or multiple times per day), the time zone, and the specific times. NordGrid's substation reference dataflow runs weekly on Mondays because substations change infrequently — a new substation is added perhaps twice a year. Running the refresh daily would produce the same result six out of seven days, wasting compute for no benefit. The weekly schedule balances freshness (at most one week stale) with efficiency (runs once instead of seven times).
Dataflow Refresh vs Pipeline Schedule

Dataflows can be refreshed in two ways from a scheduling perspective: through their own built-in scheduled refresh, or through a Dataflow activity in a pipeline. The built-in refresh is simpler — toggle it on, set the frequency, done. The pipeline approach is more powerful — it allows the dataflow to run as part of a larger workflow with dependencies, error handling, and notifications. NordGrid uses both: the substation reference dataflow uses its built-in weekly schedule (because it runs independently), while the customer classification dataflow runs through a pipeline activity (because it needs to complete before the Silver transformation notebook runs).

left.label: Built-in Scheduled Refresh
points: Configured in the dataflow's settings
Runs independently on its own schedule
No dependency management — cannot wait for another activity
No failure notification (unless configured separately)
NordGrid use: independent reference refreshes (substations)

right.label: Pipeline Dataflow Activity
points: Configured in the pipeline canvas
Runs as part of a pipeline with dependencies
Full dependency management — can depend on Copy or Notebook activities
Pipeline failure path can send notifications
NordGrid use: dependent refreshes (customer data before transformation)

blocks.content: Refresh History

Every dataflow refresh — manual or scheduled — is logged in the monitoring hub and in the dataflow's own refresh history (accessible through Settings → Refresh History). The history shows the start time, end time, duration, status (success or failure), and any error messages. NordGrid's engineers check the refresh history when a Silver table contains stale reference data — if the substation dataflow failed last Monday, the Silver table's substation join uses week-old data, which may be acceptable or may need manual intervention depending on whether any substations were added or modified.

blocks.title: Scheduled refresh continues even if you forget about it
blocks.content: Once enabled, a scheduled refresh runs indefinitely until you disable it. NordGrid's workspace had a test dataflow that was scheduled to refresh hourly during development. The engineer forgot to disable the schedule after testing, and the dataflow ran 168 times per week for three months — consuming capacity for a test that produced no value. NordGrid's cleanup checklist now includes: 'disable scheduled refresh on all test dataflows before closing the development task.'

Throughout this module, the comparison between dataflows and notebooks has been implicit. This lesson makes it explicit: a structured decision framework for choosing between the two tools. The framework is not about which tool is 'better' — both are production-grade Fabric tools with different strengths. The framework is about matching the tool to the task so that each pipeline step uses the simplest, most appropriate approach. NordGrid's data engineering team refers to this framework when designing new pipelines, and it has prevented both over-engineering (using notebooks for trivial tasks) and under-engineering (using dataflows for tasks that need code).
The Decision Framework

rows: Does the transformation need custom Python logic (UDFs, loops, conditionals)?
Use a notebook — dataflows cannot run custom Python
Is the data volume over 1 million rows?
Use a notebook — Spark scales; Power Query does not
Does the transformation require multi-step chaining with intermediate validation?
Use a notebook — cell-by-cell verification is essential
Is the transformation a simple filter/rename/type-change on reference data?
Use a dataflow — faster to build, no Spark overhead
Does the source require a Power Query connector not available in Spark?
Use a dataflow — Power Query has 200+ connectors
Do non-engineers need to read and maintain the transformation?
Use a dataflow — visual steps are more accessible than code

blocks.content: NordGrid's Actual Usage

In practice, NordGrid uses dataflows for approximately 20% of their transformation steps and notebooks for the remaining 80%. The dataflow tasks are all reference data: substations (200 rows from Azure SQL), regions (4 rows from SharePoint), customer classifications (50 rows from a web API), and a handful of small lookup tables. The notebook tasks are all analytical data: meter readings (500,000 rows from CSV), SCADA telemetry (millions of rows from Parquet), weather forecasts (100 rows from JSON, but requiring complex nested-structure parsing that Power Query handles poorly), and all Gold-layer aggregations.

The 80/20 split is not a rule — it is NordGrid's specific outcome based on their data characteristics. An organization with more reference data and less analytical processing might use dataflows for 60% of their tasks. The split depends on two factors: data volume (large volumes favor notebooks) and transformation complexity (complex logic favors notebooks). Reference data is almost always small-volume and simple-logic, which is why it consistently favors dataflows.
Cost and Performance Comparison

Dataflows and notebooks consume different types of capacity. A Spark notebook requires a Spark session — even for a small transformation, the cold start takes 30-60 seconds and the session consumes significant capacity units during that time. A Dataflow Gen2 does not use Spark — it runs on a different engine that starts faster and consumes fewer capacity units for small workloads. NordGrid's substation reference refresh takes 15 seconds as a dataflow and would take approximately 45 seconds as a notebook (30 seconds cold start plus 15 seconds processing). For a task that runs weekly, the difference is small. For a task that runs hourly, the accumulated overhead matters.

blocks.title: The simplicity test
blocks.content: When choosing between a dataflow and a notebook, apply the simplicity test: can you describe the entire transformation as a sequence of filter, rename, type-change, merge, and replace operations? If yes, use a dataflow — it will be built in 15 minutes and maintained by visual inspection. If the transformation requires 'compute X from Y using custom logic,' 'iterate over rows conditionally,' or 'join three tables with complex conditions and validate at each step,' use a notebook — code is more expressive and more debuggable for complex logic.

The previous nine lessons covered what Dataflow Gen2 is, how the Power Query Editor works, how to connect to data sources, apply transformations, merge and append queries, configure destinations, read M code, schedule refresh, and choose between dataflows and notebooks. This lesson assembles everything into NordGrid's complete reference data pipeline — a Dataflow Gen2 that connects to the external substation database, applies transformation steps, merges with the region lookup, and writes the result to the Silver Lakehouse. This dataflow runs weekly and keeps NordGrid's reference tables current.
The Complete Dataflow

blocks.title: NordGrid's substation reference dataflow: df_refresh_substations
blocks.content:
QUERY 1: Substations                    QUERY 2: Regions
┌─────────────────────────┐             ┌─────────────────────┐
│ Source: Azure SQL DB      │             │ Source: SharePoint    │
│  → substations_db.dbo.    │             │  → Region_Lookup.xlsx │
│    substations            │             │                      │
│                           │             │ Steps:                │
│ Steps:                    │             │  1. Source            │
│  1. Source                │             │  2. Changed Type      │
│  2. Navigation            │             └──────────┬───────────┘
│  3. Filtered (active only)│                        │
│  4. Removed audit columns │                        │
│  5. Renamed to Silver     │                        │
│     schema                │                        │
│  6. Changed Types         │                        │
│  7. Replaced region codes │                        │
│     (N→NORTH, etc.)       │                        │
│  8. Merge with Regions ───┼────── Left Outer Join ─┘
│  9. Expanded region_name, │
│     country               │
│ 10. Final column selection│
└─────────────┬─────────────┘
              │
              ▼
    ┌──────────────────────┐
    │ DESTINATION           │
    │ Lakehouse: lh_meter_  │
    │  readings (Silver)    │
    │ Table: ref_substations│
    │ Update: Replace       │
    └──────────────────────┘

    Schedule: Weekly, Monday 03:00 UTC
blocks.caption: The complete dataflow has two queries (substations and regions), ten transformation steps, a merge operation, and a Lakehouse destination. It produces a clean, enriched reference table that the Silver transformation notebook uses for substation joins.

blocks.content: The Step-by-Step Walkthrough

items: Connect to the Azure SQL Database using the stored connection credential (conn_sql_substations). Navigate to the dbo.substations table. The preview shows 210 rows including inactive substations.
Filter the status column to exclude 'INACTIVE' substations. The preview drops to 198 active substations.
Remove the audit_date and vendor_code columns — these are internal to the source system and not needed in the Silver table.
Rename sub_id to substation_id and cap_mw to capacity_mw to match NordGrid's Silver naming convention.
Change capacity_mw from integer to decimal type for consistency with other Silver numeric columns.
Replace the abbreviated region codes (N, S, E, W) with full names (NORTH, SOUTH, EAST, WEST) using four Replace Values steps.
Merge with the Regions query (sourced from the SharePoint Excel file) using a Left Outer join on the region column. Expand region_name and country from the merged result.
Select the final column set: substation_id, name, capacity_mw, region, region_name, country. Remove any extra columns from the merge.
Configure the Lakehouse destination: Silver Lakehouse (lh_meter_readings), table name ref_substations, update method Replace.
Publish the dataflow and schedule it for weekly Monday refresh at 03:00 UTC.

blocks.content: The Complete Picture

You now have the full Dataflow Gen2 foundation. Dataflows are low-code transformation tools powered by Power Query (lesson 1). The Power Query Editor provides a visual step-based development experience (lesson 2). Connections authenticate with external data sources (lesson 3). Common transformations — filter, rename, type-change, replace — are single-click operations (lesson 4). Merge and append combine data from multiple sources (lesson 5). Destinations write results to Lakehouse or Warehouse tables (lesson 6). M code underlies every visual step (lesson 7). Scheduled refresh automates execution (lesson 8). The decision framework guides tool selection between dataflows and notebooks (lesson 9). And NordGrid's complete substation dataflow demonstrates the entire workflow in production (this lesson).

blocks.title: What comes next
blocks.content: Module 9 takes you to the other side of the Lakehouse — the SQL analytics endpoint and the Fabric Warehouse. You have spent Modules 4 through 8 writing data into Lakehouses using notebooks, pipelines, and dataflows. Module 9 teaches you how to query that data using T-SQL, create views for analysts, and use the Warehouse for read-write SQL workloads. The data is in the Lakehouse. Now you learn how to serve it.

chapters.title: The SQL Analytics Endpoint and Warehouse
chapters.description: Learn to query Lakehouse data using T-SQL through the SQL analytics endpoint, understand the Fabric Warehouse as a dedicated SQL workload, and build NordGrid's analytical views and summary tables.

sections.title: Two Ways to Query with SQL

blocks.content: Modules 5 through 8 focused on writing data into Lakehouses — using notebooks, pipelines, and dataflows to transform Bronze files into Silver tables and Gold aggregates. This module focuses on reading data out using T-SQL. Fabric provides two T-SQL experiences: the SQL analytics endpoint (auto-generated with every Lakehouse, read-only) and the Fabric Warehouse (a dedicated item you create explicitly, read-write). Both use standard T-SQL syntax, both connect to OneLake data, and both serve NordGrid's analytical needs — but they serve different audiences and different use cases. This lesson introduces both and explains when to use each.
The SQL Analytics Endpoint

Every Lakehouse in Fabric comes with an auto-generated SQL analytics endpoint — a read-only T-SQL interface to the Lakehouse's Delta tables. You learned about this in Module 4, lesson 7. The endpoint supports SELECT queries, views, and functions, but not INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE, or CREATE TABLE. Its purpose is consumption: letting analysts, reporting tools, and data engineers query Lakehouse data using familiar SQL without running Spark. NordGrid's analysts use the Silver Lakehouse's SQL endpoint for all their ad-hoc queries and for creating views that simplify common query patterns.
The Fabric Warehouse

The Fabric Warehouse is a separate item type — you create it explicitly in a workspace, like you create a Lakehouse or a notebook. Unlike the SQL analytics endpoint, the Warehouse is read-write: it supports CREATE TABLE, INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE, stored procedures, and cross-database queries. Its purpose is production SQL workloads: building aggregation tables, maintaining materialized summaries, and running SQL-based transformations that do not require Spark. NordGrid uses the Gold Warehouse (dw_nordgrid_analytics) for pre-computed monthly billing aggregates and quarterly regulatory summaries.

left.label: SQL Analytics Endpoint
points: Auto-generated with every Lakehouse
Read-only: SELECT, views, functions
Reads from the Lakehouse's Delta tables
No additional cost — included with the Lakehouse
NordGrid use: analyst queries on Silver data, views

right.label: Fabric Warehouse
points: Created explicitly as a separate workspace item
Read-write: SELECT, INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE, CREATE TABLE
Has its own tables, plus cross-database queries to Lakehouses
Consumes capacity when running queries
NordGrid use: Gold-layer aggregation tables, billing summaries

blocks.content: Choosing Between Them

The decision is straightforward. If you only need to read Lakehouse data with SQL — running SELECT queries, creating views, building reports — use the SQL analytics endpoint. It is already there, it costs nothing extra, and it requires no setup. If you need to write SQL tables — creating aggregation tables, maintaining dimension tables, running INSERT...SELECT pipelines — you need a Warehouse. NordGrid uses both: the SQL endpoint for reading Silver data, and the Warehouse for writing Gold aggregates. Most junior data engineers interact with the SQL endpoint far more frequently than the Warehouse.

blocks.title: SQL is the second language of data engineering
blocks.content: PySpark is NordGrid's primary transformation language — it handles the heavy-lifting Silver transformations. But T-SQL is the language that connects data engineers to analysts, reporting tools, and business users. When an analyst asks 'how many meters reported yesterday?', the fastest answer comes from a SQL query on the endpoint, not from spinning up a Spark notebook. Knowing enough T-SQL to write verification queries, create views, and build simple aggregations makes you a more effective data engineer, even if PySpark remains your primary tool.

Module 4, lesson 7 introduced the SQL analytics endpoint at a conceptual level. This lesson goes deeper into the practical experience: opening the endpoint, navigating the SQL editor, understanding what tables are available, and writing your first queries against Lakehouse data. The SQL analytics endpoint is the tool NordGrid's data engineers use every morning to verify pipeline output, and it is the tool analysts use all day to answer business questions. Mastering it means you can validate your own pipeline output in seconds and help analysts who have questions about the data you produce.
Opening the Endpoint

The SQL analytics endpoint appears as a separate item in the workspace, alongside the Lakehouse and the semantic model. Its icon looks like a small database cylinder. Click it to open the SQL editor — a web-based T-SQL editor with a query pane, an object explorer on the left, and a results pane at the bottom. The object explorer shows all Delta tables from the Lakehouse's Tables section, any views you have created, and any functions you have defined. The tables appear with their column names and types, making it easy to browse the schema without running any query.

blocks.title: The SQL analytics endpoint editor
blocks.caption: The SQL editor has three zones: the object explorer on the left (showing tables and views), the query editor in the upper right (where you write T-SQL), and the results pane below (showing query output as a table). The experience is identical to SQL Server Management Studio or Azure Data Studio.

blocks.content: Automatic Table Detection

The SQL analytics endpoint automatically reflects every Delta table in the Lakehouse's Tables section. When a notebook writes a new table with saveAsTable('silver_meters'), the table appears in the endpoint's object explorer within seconds — no registration, no configuration, no refresh. If the notebook adds a column to the table using mergeSchema, the endpoint reflects the new column immediately. This automatic sync means the SQL endpoint is always in sync with the Lakehouse — there is no 'stale metadata' problem.
Querying Lakehouse Tables

Queries against the SQL analytics endpoint use standard T-SQL syntax. If you have written SQL against SQL Server, PostgreSQL, MySQL, or any other relational database, the syntax is familiar. NordGrid's analysts write the same SELECT, WHERE, GROUP BY, ORDER BY, and JOIN statements they would write anywhere else. The only difference is that the data comes from Delta tables in OneLake rather than from a traditional database's storage engine. Query performance depends on the table's size, partitioning, and the complexity of the query — but for typical analytical queries against partitioned Silver tables, results return in seconds.

-- Basic query: count meters per region for a specific date
SELECT region, COUNT(*) AS meter_count
FROM silver_meters
WHERE date = '2025-03-23'
GROUP BY region
ORDER BY region;

-- Analytical query: daily average consumption over the past 30 days
SELECT
    date,
    region,
    AVG(daily_kw) AS avg_daily_kw,
    SUM(daily_kw) AS total_daily_kw
FROM silver_meters
WHERE date >= DATEADD(DAY, -30, CAST('2025-03-23' AS DATE))
GROUP BY date, region
ORDER BY date, region;

blocks.title: Partition pruning works in SQL too
blocks.content: When the Silver table is partitioned by year and month (as NordGrid's tables are), the SQL engine leverages partition pruning. A query with WHERE date = '2025-03-23' only reads the year=2025/month=03 partition, skipping all other months. This means the SQL endpoint benefits from the same partitioning decisions that the data engineer made in the notebook's saveAsTable call. Good data engineering practices (partitioning, column selection, proper types) directly improve SQL query performance for analysts.

This lesson covers the T-SQL patterns NordGrid's data engineers use most frequently when working with Lakehouse data through the SQL analytics endpoint. These are not comprehensive SQL tutorials â�� the PySpark Junior-Level Track's Module 10 covers SQL fundamentals, and dedicated SQL courses exist for deeper study. This lesson focuses on the specific query patterns that a Fabric data engineer needs for daily verification, ad-hoc analysis, and supporting analyst requests.
Verification Queries

NordGrid's morning verification routine uses three standard queries. The first checks the total row count for yesterday's data — a quick sanity check that the pipeline produced the expected volume. The second checks for null values in columns that should never be null after transformation. The third checks that all four regions are present with reasonable counts. These three queries run in under five seconds total and catch the most common pipeline issues.

-- Verification 1: Row count for yesterday
SELECT COUNT(*) AS row_count
FROM silver_meters
WHERE date = DATEADD(DAY, -1, CAST(GETDATE() AS DATE));

-- Verification 2: Null check on required columns
SELECT
    SUM(CASE WHEN meter_id IS NULL THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS null_ids,
    SUM(CASE WHEN daily_kw IS NULL THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS null_kw,
    SUM(CASE WHEN region IS NULL THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS null_regions
FROM silver_meters
WHERE date = DATEADD(DAY, -1, CAST(GETDATE() AS DATE));

-- Verification 3: Region completeness with summary statistics
SELECT
    region,
    COUNT(*) AS meters,
    ROUND(AVG(daily_kw), 2) AS avg_kw,
    MAX(daily_kw) AS max_kw,
    MIN(daily_kw) AS min_kw
FROM silver_meters
WHERE date = DATEADD(DAY, -1, CAST(GETDATE() AS DATE))
GROUP BY region
ORDER BY region;
Aggregation Queries

Aggregation queries summarize large datasets into smaller result sets — the same pattern as PySpark's groupBy().agg(). NordGrid's analysts frequently request monthly summaries, regional comparisons, and trend analyses. These queries use GROUP BY with aggregate functions (SUM, AVG, COUNT, MIN, MAX) and often include date manipulation functions to extract year, month, or quarter from the date column. The result set is typically much smaller than the source data: a monthly regional summary produces 48 rows per year (12 months × 4 regions) from 182 million source rows.

-- Monthly energy consumption summary by region
SELECT
    YEAR(date) AS year,
    MONTH(date) AS month,
    region,
    COUNT(*) AS reading_count,
    ROUND(SUM(daily_kw), 2) AS total_kw,
    ROUND(SUM(daily_kw) / 1000, 2) AS total_mw,
    ROUND(AVG(daily_kw), 2) AS avg_kw
FROM silver_meters
WHERE YEAR(date) = 2025
GROUP BY YEAR(date), MONTH(date), region
ORDER BY year, month, region;
Join Queries

The SQL analytics endpoint supports joins between tables in the same Lakehouse. NordGrid's Silver Lakehouse contains both the silver_meters table and the ref_substations reference table (written by the dataflow from Module 8). Analysts join these tables to enrich meter readings with substation names and capacities — the same join that the PySpark notebook performs, but expressed in SQL for analysts who prefer that language.

-- Join meter readings with substation reference data
SELECT
    m.region,
    s.name AS substation_name,
    s.capacity_mw,
    COUNT(*) AS meter_count,
    ROUND(SUM(m.daily_kw), 2) AS total_kw
FROM silver_meters m
INNER JOIN ref_substations s
    ON m.region = s.region
WHERE m.date = '2025-03-23'
GROUP BY m.region, s.name, s.capacity_mw
ORDER BY m.region, s.name;

blocks.title: The endpoint is read-only
blocks.content: If you try to run INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE, or CREATE TABLE on the SQL analytics endpoint, the query will fail with a permissions error. This is by design — the endpoint protects Lakehouse data from accidental modification through SQL. If you need to write data with T-SQL, you need a Fabric Warehouse (lesson 5). NordGrid's analysts sometimes try to create tables on the endpoint and are confused by the error — reminding them that the endpoint is read-only (but views are allowed) resolves the confusion.

Although the SQL analytics endpoint cannot create tables, it can create views — saved queries that appear as virtual tables in the object explorer. Views are one of the most valuable tools a data engineer can offer analysts because they simplify access to complex data. Instead of writing a 15-line query with joins, filters, and aggregations every time they need a monthly summary, an analyst can query a view that encapsulates all that logic. NordGrid's Silver endpoint has eight production views that cover the most common analyst needs.
What a View Does

A view is a named query stored in the SQL endpoint's metadata. It does not copy or store any data — every time someone queries the view, the view's underlying query runs against the current table data. This means views are always current: when the notebook writes new data to silver_meters tonight, a query against v_monthly_summary tomorrow morning returns results that include tonight's data. Views consume no additional storage and add no maintenance overhead. They are pure convenience — a way to name and reuse a query.

-- View 1: Monthly regional summary — the most frequently used view
CREATE VIEW v_monthly_region_summary AS
SELECT
    YEAR(date) AS year,
    MONTH(date) AS month,
    region,
    COUNT(*) AS reading_count,
    ROUND(SUM(daily_kw), 2) AS total_kw,
    ROUND(AVG(daily_kw), 2) AS avg_kw,
    MAX(daily_kw) AS max_kw
FROM silver_meters
GROUP BY YEAR(date), MONTH(date), region;

-- View 2: North region meters only — for the NORTH operations team
CREATE VIEW v_north_meters AS
SELECT meter_id, daily_kw, date, customer_type
FROM silver_meters
WHERE region = 'NORTH';

-- View 3: Yesterday's data — for the morning verification routine
CREATE VIEW v_yesterday AS
SELECT *
FROM silver_meters
WHERE date = DATEADD(DAY, -1, CAST(GETDATE() AS DATE));

The three views above demonstrate different view purposes. The monthly summary view pre-computes aggregations that analysts run daily — instead of writing the GROUP BY query every time, they simply run SELECT * FROM v_monthly_region_summary WHERE year = 2025 AND month = 3. The region filter view restricts data to one region — the NORTH operations team queries only their meters without seeing other regions. The yesterday view provides a convenient shorthand for the most common verification filter. Each view saves time for its consumers and ensures consistent logic — every analyst computing monthly totals uses the same rounding and the same aggregation.
Views as a Data Contract

NordGrid uses views as a contract between the data engineering team and the analyst team. The data engineers are responsible for the underlying Silver tables — their schema, quality, and freshness. The analysts are responsible for using the views to access that data. If the data engineering team needs to change the Silver table's column names (for example, renaming daily_kw to daily_kwh), they update the view definition to map the new column name to the old view column name. The analysts' queries against the view continue to work without modification. This decoupling protects analysts from schema changes and gives data engineers the freedom to evolve the underlying tables.

-- If the underlying table renames daily_kw to daily_kwh,
-- the view absorbs the change — analysts see no difference
ALTER VIEW v_monthly_region_summary AS
SELECT
    YEAR(date) AS year,
    MONTH(date) AS month,
    region,
    COUNT(*) AS reading_count,
    ROUND(SUM(daily_kwh), 2) AS total_kw,   -- column renamed in table
    ROUND(AVG(daily_kwh), 2) AS avg_kw,     -- view keeps the old name
    MAX(daily_kwh) AS max_kw
FROM silver_meters
GROUP BY YEAR(date), MONTH(date), region;

blocks.title: Views are the analyst's interface
blocks.content: Think of views as the API between data engineering and data analysis. The data engineer builds the tables and defines the views. The analyst queries the views. The views hide complexity, enforce consistency, and absorb schema changes. NordGrid's convention: every Silver table that analysts query directly should have at least one production view. The table is the implementation. The view is the interface.

The SQL analytics endpoint handles reading. The Fabric Warehouse handles everything: reading, writing, creating tables, running stored procedures, and executing cross-database queries. The Warehouse is a separate item type that you create explicitly in a workspace — it does not come free with a Lakehouse. NordGrid creates the Warehouse in the Gold workspace (dw_nordgrid_analytics) for a specific purpose: building and maintaining the pre-computed aggregation tables that the reporting team queries. This lesson explains what the Warehouse offers beyond the SQL endpoint, how to create it, and when it is the right choice.
What the Warehouse Adds

The Warehouse provides the full T-SQL surface that the SQL analytics endpoint lacks. CREATE TABLE lets you define tables with explicit schemas. INSERT INTO...SELECT lets you populate tables from queries. UPDATE and DELETE let you modify existing data. Stored procedures let you encapsulate multi-step SQL logic. Cross-database queries let you read from Lakehouses and other Warehouses in different workspaces using three-part names. These capabilities make the Warehouse suitable for building Gold-layer aggregation tables — the pre-computed summaries that Power BI dashboards and business systems consume.
Creating a Warehouse

To create a Warehouse, navigate to the workspace (ws_nordgrid_gold), click New, and select Warehouse. Fabric prompts for a name — NordGrid uses dw_nordgrid_analytics. The Warehouse opens in a SQL editor interface similar to the SQL analytics endpoint, but with full read-write capabilities. The Warehouse starts empty — no tables, no views, no data. You build it by creating tables and populating them with INSERT INTO...SELECT queries that read from Lakehouse tables.
Warehouse vs Lakehouse for Gold

NordGrid's Gold layer uses both a Lakehouse and a Warehouse. The Gold Lakehouse (lh_grid_analytics) stores Delta tables written by PySpark notebooks — the daily regional summary, for example, is computed by the Gold aggregation notebook and written with saveAsTable. The Gold Warehouse (dw_nordgrid_analytics) stores tables created by T-SQL queries — the monthly billing aggregate, for example, is computed by an INSERT INTO...SELECT query that reads from the Silver Lakehouse through a cross-database query. The choice depends on the tool: if the aggregation is computed in PySpark, write to the Lakehouse. If the aggregation is computed in T-SQL, write to the Warehouse.

left.label: Gold Lakehouse Table
points: Written by PySpark notebooks using saveAsTable
Stored as Delta tables in OneLake
Queryable via the Lakehouse's SQL endpoint and via Spark
NordGrid use: daily_region_summary (PySpark aggregation)

right.label: Gold Warehouse Table
points: Written by T-SQL using INSERT INTO...SELECT or CREATE TABLE AS SELECT
Stored as Warehouse-managed tables in OneLake
Queryable via the Warehouse's SQL editor and via cross-database queries
NordGrid use: monthly_billing (T-SQL aggregation with complex joins)

blocks.content: Both approaches are valid. NordGrid uses PySpark for the daily regional summary because it is a simple groupBy that the existing Gold notebook handles in three lines of code. They use T-SQL for the monthly billing aggregate because it requires a complex multi-table join with window functions and conditional logic that is more naturally expressed in SQL than in PySpark. The choice is about readability and team expertise, not about technical capability — both tools can compute both aggregations.

blocks.title: The Warehouse is for SQL-native teams
blocks.content: If your Gold-layer aggregations are naturally expressed as SQL queries — GROUP BY, window functions, CASE WHEN, multi-table joins — the Warehouse is the right home for them. If they are naturally expressed as PySpark DataFrame operations — groupBy, agg, withColumn chains — the Lakehouse is the right home. NordGrid uses both because their Gold logic is a mix of both styles. Teams that are purely SQL-oriented might use only the Warehouse for Gold. Teams that are purely PySpark-oriented might use only the Lakehouse.

Creating a Warehouse table is a SQL operation — you write a CREATE TABLE statement or use CREATE TABLE AS SELECT to create and populate a table in one step. Unlike Lakehouse tables (which are created by notebook writes), Warehouse tables are created by SQL statements executed in the Warehouse's SQL editor. This lesson walks through creating NordGrid's Gold Warehouse tables and populating them with aggregated data from the Silver Lakehouse.
Creating Tables

-- Create the monthly billing aggregate table in the Warehouse
CREATE TABLE monthly_billing (
    year          INT         NOT NULL,
    month         INT         NOT NULL,
    region        VARCHAR(20) NOT NULL,
    customer_type VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL,
    meter_count   INT         NOT NULL,
    total_kw      DECIMAL(18,2) NOT NULL,
    total_mw      DECIMAL(18,4) NOT NULL,
    avg_kw        DECIMAL(18,2) NOT NULL,
    max_kw        DECIMAL(18,2) NOT NULL,
    computed_at   DATETIME2   NOT NULL DEFAULT GETDATE()
);

The CREATE TABLE statement defines the table's schema explicitly — column names, types, nullability, and default values. This is the same pattern as any SQL database. NordGrid's billing table has columns for the time period (year, month), the grouping dimensions (region, customer_type), the aggregated metrics (meter_count, total_kw, total_mw, avg_kw, max_kw), and a metadata column (computed_at) that records when the aggregation ran. The schema serves as documentation: anyone reading it knows exactly what the table contains and at what granularity.
Populating with INSERT INTO...SELECT

-- Populate the monthly billing table from Silver Lakehouse data
-- This uses a cross-database query to read from the Silver Lakehouse
INSERT INTO monthly_billing (year, month, region, customer_type,
                              meter_count, total_kw, total_mw, avg_kw, max_kw)
SELECT
    YEAR(date) AS year,
    MONTH(date) AS month,
    region,
    customer_type,
    COUNT(DISTINCT meter_id) AS meter_count,
    ROUND(SUM(daily_kw), 2) AS total_kw,
    ROUND(SUM(daily_kw) / 1000, 4) AS total_mw,
    ROUND(AVG(daily_kw), 2) AS avg_kw,
    ROUND(MAX(daily_kw), 2) AS max_kw
FROM lh_meter_readings.dbo.silver_meters  -- Cross-database query to the Silver Lakehouse
WHERE YEAR(date) = 2025 AND MONTH(date) = 3
GROUP BY YEAR(date), MONTH(date), region, customer_type;

The INSERT INTO...SELECT statement reads from the Silver Lakehouse's silver_meters table using a cross-database query (lh_meter_readings.dbo.silver_meters) and writes the aggregated result to the Warehouse's monthly_billing table. The query groups by year, month, region, and customer_type — producing one row per combination. For NordGrid's four regions and five customer types, a single month produces approximately 20 rows. The Warehouse table is tiny compared to the Silver source (20 rows vs 15 million for a month), which is exactly the point of Gold-layer aggregation.
The Refresh Pattern

NordGrid refreshes the monthly billing table on the first day of each month. The refresh first deletes the previous month's data (in case it needs to be recomputed) and then inserts the fresh aggregation. This delete-then-insert pattern is the Warehouse equivalent of PySpark's overwrite mode. The refresh is triggered by a pipeline that runs on the first of each month, containing a SQL activity that executes the DELETE and INSERT statements in sequence.

-- Monthly refresh: delete previous month's data and recompute
-- Run on the 1st of each month for the previous month
DELETE FROM monthly_billing
WHERE year = 2025 AND month = 2;  -- Delete February's data

-- Then INSERT the fresh aggregation (same query as above)
INSERT INTO monthly_billing (...)
SELECT ...
FROM lh_meter_readings.dbo.silver_meters
WHERE YEAR(date) = 2025 AND MONTH(date) = 2;

blocks.title: Cross-database queries require access
blocks.content: A cross-database query from the Warehouse to a Lakehouse requires that you have at least read access to the Lakehouse's workspace. If you can query the Silver Lakehouse directly in its own SQL endpoint, you can also query it from the Gold Warehouse using the three-part name. If you lack access, the cross-database query fails with a permission error. NordGrid's engineers have Viewer access to the Silver workspace, which is sufficient for read-only cross-database queries from the Gold Warehouse.

One of the Warehouse's most powerful capabilities is cross-database querying — the ability to join data from multiple Lakehouses and Warehouses in a single SQL statement. In NordGrid's architecture, the Silver data lives in the Silver Lakehouse and the Gold aggregates live in the Gold Warehouse. Without cross-database queries, computing a Gold aggregate that combines Silver meter data with Gold reference dimensions would require copying data between items. With cross-database queries, the Warehouse reads directly from the Lakehouse — no copy, no pipeline, no staleness.
The Three-Part Name

Cross-database queries use a three-part naming convention: database.schema.table. In Fabric, the database name is the Lakehouse or Warehouse item name, the schema is typically dbo (the default schema), and the table is the table name. NordGrid's cross-database query from the Gold Warehouse to the Silver Lakehouse uses lh_meter_readings.dbo.silver_meters. The three-part name tells the SQL engine exactly where to find the table — which item in which workspace.

-- Cross-database query: join Silver meters with Gold dimension table
-- Read from the Silver Lakehouse AND the Gold Warehouse in one query
SELECT
    s.region,
    d.region_name,
    d.country,
    COUNT(DISTINCT s.meter_id) AS active_meters,
    ROUND(SUM(s.daily_kw), 2) AS total_kw
FROM lh_meter_readings.dbo.silver_meters s           -- Silver Lakehouse
INNER JOIN dw_nordgrid_analytics.dbo.dim_regions d   -- Gold Warehouse
    ON s.region = d.region_code
WHERE s.date = '2025-03-23'
GROUP BY s.region, d.region_name, d.country
ORDER BY s.region;

The query above reads from two different Fabric items in one statement: silver_meters from the Silver Lakehouse and dim_regions from the Gold Warehouse. The SQL engine handles the cross-database join transparently — you write the query as if both tables were in the same database. NordGrid uses this pattern extensively in the Gold layer: computing billing aggregates by joining Silver meter data with Warehouse dimension tables that contain customer classifications, tariff rates, and regional metadata that the dataflow refreshed from external sources.
Cross-Workspace Queries

Cross-database queries work across workspaces as long as the items are in the same Fabric capacity and you have read access to both. NordGrid's Silver Lakehouse is in ws_nordgrid_silver and the Gold Warehouse is in ws_nordgrid_gold — two different workspaces. The cross-database query from the Warehouse to the Lakehouse works because NordGrid's engineers have Viewer access to the Silver workspace. The three-part name resolves across workspace boundaries automatically.
When to Use Cross-Database Queries

rows: Gold Warehouse needs Silver data for aggregation
Copy data to the Warehouse first (adds latency and storage)
Verification: compare Silver and Gold row counts
Join Silver and Gold tables in one query
Run separate queries and compare manually
Reporting: combine Lakehouse facts with Warehouse dimensions
Join across items in one query
Create shortcuts and query in one Lakehouse (also valid)
Debugging: check if a Silver value matches the Gold aggregate
Open two SQL editors side by side (slower, error-prone)

blocks.title: Cross-database queries eliminate data copying
blocks.content: In NordGrid's old Azure architecture, computing Gold aggregates required copying Silver data into the Synapse warehouse — a nightly ETL step that added latency, storage cost, and a staleness window. In Fabric, the Gold Warehouse reads Silver data directly through a cross-database query. No copy. No ETL. No staleness. The Silver table is always current, and the Gold aggregation always reads the latest version. This is one of the practical advantages of OneLake's shared storage model.

T-SQL provides a rich library of built-in functions for aggregation, date manipulation, string operations, and conditional logic. NordGrid's data engineers and analysts use these functions daily — in verification queries, in view definitions, in Warehouse aggregation logic, and in ad-hoc analysis. This lesson covers the T-SQL functions that appear most frequently in NordGrid's Fabric SQL code, organized by category. This is not a comprehensive function reference — it is a practical selection of the functions you will use in the first month of working with Fabric.
Aggregation Functions

Aggregation functions reduce many rows to one summary value. They are the SQL equivalent of PySpark's agg() functions. COUNT, SUM, AVG, MIN, and MAX are the five most common. COUNT(*) counts all rows; COUNT(column) counts non-null values in that column. SUM and AVG operate on numeric columns. MIN and MAX work on any comparable type — numbers, dates, and strings. NordGrid uses all five in nearly every aggregation query: the monthly billing table computes COUNT(DISTINCT meter_id), SUM(daily_kw), AVG(daily_kw), and MAX(daily_kw) in a single GROUP BY query.
Date Functions

-- Date extraction: break a date into its components
SELECT
    YEAR(date)       AS year,        -- 2025
    MONTH(date)      AS month,       -- 3
    DAY(date)        AS day,         -- 23
    DATEPART(QUARTER, date) AS quarter,  -- 1
    DATENAME(WEEKDAY, date) AS day_name  -- 'Sunday'
FROM silver_meters
WHERE meter_id = 'M-00001' AND date = '2025-03-23';

-- Date arithmetic: add or subtract days
SELECT
    DATEADD(DAY, -30, CAST('2025-03-23' AS DATE)) AS thirty_days_ago,   -- 2025-02-21
    DATEADD(MONTH, -1, CAST('2025-03-23' AS DATE)) AS one_month_ago,    -- 2025-02-23
    DATEDIFF(DAY, '2025-01-01', '2025-03-23') AS days_in_year;          -- 82

Date functions are essential because most analytical queries filter by time. NordGrid's morning verification queries use DATEADD(DAY, -1, GETDATE()) to select yesterday's data. Monthly billing queries use YEAR(date) and MONTH(date) to group by period. Trend analyses use DATEDIFF to compute the number of days between events.
